1. Introduction

Achieving first-time success is crucial in the ASIC design league considering the soaring cost,
tight time-to-market window, and competitive business environment. One key factor in
ensuring first-time success is a well-defined ASIC design methodology.

 ASIC Design Challenges

The ever-changing and ever-challenging ASIC design environment consistently brings up
new challenges in the ASIC design league.

There are long-existing challenges such as:

  • Soaring cost (design, verification, fabrication)
  • High design complexity
  • Tight time-to-market window
  • Competitive business environment

 Design & Verification Complexity Growth

The complexity of design and verification has grown tremendously in the search for more
computational power and capacity, which is also in accordance with Moore's law.

Nowadays the implementation of specific applications such as face recognition in a
System-on-Chip (SoC) has become a practice.

 

Technology Advancement in ASICs

Technology advancement is the development and use of Network-on-Chip (NoC) for
communication inside the chip.

All the advancement in technology today without doubt has enriched the ASIC products
while at the same time introducing more and more challenges into the ASIC design and
verification.

 Reasons for First Silicon Failure

(Heading 2 – Dark Green)

The long and many steps in the design and verification process of an ASIC may often lead to
first silicon failure.

This is due to factors such as:

  •  Human errors
  •  Immature electronic design automation (EDA) software
  •  Lack of experience and discipline

 Important Note

A disciplined design methodology is critical to reduce risks and achieve first-time success
 in ASIC design.

 1.1: VLSI Design Flow

The ASIC design methodology is comprised of two major parts: the front end and the
back end.

Every development of a commercial electronic product is derived from the
user requirements, which are translated into a system specification.

 Front-End and Back-End Design

Modern day Integrated Circuit (IC) design is split up into:

  • Front-end Design using Hardware Description Languages (HDLs)
  • Back-end Design, also known as Physical Design

 Inputs to Physical Design

The inputs to physical design are:

  1. A netlist
  2. Library information on the basic devices in the design
  3. A technology file containing the manufacturing constraints

 

Physical Design Completion

Physical design is usually concluded by Layout Post Processing, in which
amendments and additions to the chip layout are performed.

 Fabrication and Manufacturing

This is followed by the Fabrication or Manufacturing Process where designs are
transferred onto silicon dies, which are then packaged into ICs.

 Physical Design Flow – Overview

 Design Phases and Flows

Each of the phases mentioned above has design flows associated with them.
These design flows lay down the process and guidelines/framework for that phase.

 Physical Design Flow

The physical design flow uses the technology libraries that are provided by the fabrication houses.

These technology files provide information regarding:

  •  The type of silicon wafer used
  •  The standard cells used
  •  The layout rules (like DRC in VLSI)

 These libraries act as the foundation for physical implementation.

IC Physical Design Categories

Typically, the IC physical design is categorized into two types:

 1. Full-Custom Design

  • The designer has full flexibility in the layout design
  •  No predefined cells are used
  • Every transistor and layout structure is manually designed

 Used for high-performance, analog, or critical blocks

 2. Semi-Custom Design

  • Pre-designed library cells are used
  • Cells are preferably tested with DFM
  • Designer has flexibility in:
  • Placement of cells
  • Routing

 Most commonly used approach in industry

 ASIC vs FPGA Usage

  • ASIC is used for Full-Custom design
  • FPGA is used for Semi-Custom design flows

 Reason for This Choice

The reason being that one has the flexibility to design/modify design blocks from vendor-provided libraries in ASIC.

This flexibility is missing for Semi-Custom flows using FPGAs (e.g., Altera).

1.2: Physical Design Flow

 Main Steps in ASIC Physical Design Flow

The main steps in the ASIC physical design flow are:

  1. Design Netlist (after synthesis)
  2. Floor planning
  3. Design partitioning
  4. Placement
  5. Clock-tree Synthesis (CTS)
  6. Routing
  7. Physical Verification
  8. Layout Post Processing with Mask Data Generation

 Important Note

These steps are just the basics.
There are detailed PD flows that are used depending on the tools used and the
methodology/technology.

 Backend Design Tools / Software

Some of the tools/software used in the backend design are:

 1. Cadence

  • Cadence Encounter RTL Compiler
  • Encounter Digital Implementation
  • Cadence Voltus IC Power Integrity Solution
  • Cadence Tempus Timing Signoff Solution

 2. Synopsys

  • Design Compiler
  • IC Compiler II
  • IC Validator
  • PrimeTime
  • PrimePower
  • PrimeRail

 3. Magma

  • BlastFusion, etc.

 4. Mentor Graphics

  • Olympus SoC
  • IC-Station
  • Calibre

 Technology Libraries

The ASIC physical design flow uses the technology libraries that are provided by the
fabrication houses.

 Technology Classification

Technologies are commonly classified according to minimal feature size.

Standard sizes (order of miniaturization):

  • 2µm
  • 1µm
  • 0.5µm
  • 0.35µm
  • 0.25µm
  • 180nm
  • 130nm
  • 90nm
  • 65nm
  • 45nm
  • 28nm
  • 22nm
  • 18nm
  • 14nm

 Manufacturing Approaches

They may be also classified according to major manufacturing approaches:

  • n-Well process
  • twin-well process
  • SOI process, etc.

1.2.1: Floor Planning

As an ever-increasing demand for SoC speed, performance, and features, the industry is moving toward lower technology nodes. The current market has become more and more demanding, in turn forcing complex architectures and reduced time to market. The complex integrations and smaller design cycle emphasize the importance of floor planning, i.e., the first step in the netlist-to-GDSII design flow.

Floorplanning not only captures the designer's intent but also presents the challenges and opportunities that affect the entire design flow, from design to implementation and chip assembly.

 SoC Complexity and Challenges

A typical SoC can include:

  • Hard-IP and Soft-IP macros
  • Memories
  • Analog blocks
  • Multiple power domains

Because of increases in:

  • Gate count
  • Power domains
  • Power modes
  • Special architectural requirements

most SoCs today are hierarchical designs.

The SoC interacts with the outside world through:

  • Sensors
  • Antennas
  • Displays
  • Other elements

which introduce a lot of analog components in the chip.
All these limitations directly result in various challenges in floorplanning.

 What Floorplanning Includes

Floorplanning includes:

  • Macro / Block placement
  • Design partitioning
  • Pin placement
  • Power planning
  • Power grid design

Decisions taken during macro placement, partitioning, I/O-pad placement, and power planning directly or indirectly impact the overall implementation cycle.

 Iterations and Optimization

Lots of iterations happen to get an optimum floorplan.

The designer takes care of:

  • Power
  • Area
  • Timing
  • Performance

These estimations are repeatedly reviewed based on feedback from:

  • Implementation team
  • IP owners
  • RTL designers

 Outcome of floorplanning:

  • Proper arrangement of macros/blocks
  • Power grid
  • Pin placement
  • Partitioned blocks that can be implemented in parallel

 Importance in Hierarchical Designs

In hierarchical designs, the quality of the floorplan is analyzed after blocks are integrated at the top level.
This can result in:

  • Unnecessary iterations
  • Wasted resource hours
  • Longer cycle times
  • Missed market opportunities

 This underscores the importance of floorplanning.

 Rule of Thumb in Floorplanning

The first rule of thumb is to arrange the hard macros and memories such that the core area (for SoC placement) is square in shape.

However, this is not always possible due to:

  • Large number of analog-IP blocks
  • Memories
  • Various other design requirements

 Important Floorplanning Terminology

Before going into details, the designer must understand the following terms:

1. Track

  • Virtual guideline/path for routing signals
  • Defined for each metal layer
  • Available in preferred and non-preferred directions
  • Router assumes the track is at the center of the metal

2. Row

  • Area defined for standard-cell placement
  • Row height depends on standard-cell height
  • Multiple row heights may exist based on cell type

3. Guide

  • Guided placement of a logical module
  • Soft constraint
  • Some logic may be placed outside the guide
  • Other logic from outside modules may enter the guide

4. Region

  • Hard constraint
  • Module logic must stay within region boundary
  • Outside modules may still place some logic inside

5. Fence

  • Strict hard constraint
  • Only the specified module can be placed inside
  • No outside logic allowed

6. Halo

  • Placement blockage around macros
  • Prevents standard cells from being placed too close

7. Routing Blockage

  • Obstruction for metal routing
  • Prevents routing over defined areas

8. Partial Blockage

  • Porous placement restriction
  • Controls placement density
  • Helps avoid congestion

 Example:
If partial blockage is 40%, placement density is limited to 60%.

9. Buffer Blockage / Soft Blockage

  • Only buffer cells allowed
  • Used during optimization or legalization
  • Other standard cells are restricted

 Hierarchical Design Approach

The design approach is shifting toward hierarchical closure due to:

  • Safety requirements
  • Tool limitations with high gate counts
  • Late IP delivery
  • Multiple power modes

Hierarchically partitioned blocks are implemented independently in terms of:

  • Placement
  • Routing
  • Timing
  • Noise closure

 Top-Level Integration Challenges

When partitions are merged at the top level:

  • Nets detour across partition boundaries
  • Timing violations increase
  • Routing resources are wasted
  • Buffers are inserted to avoid DRVs

This leads to:

  • Increased power consumption
  • Higher placement density
  • Clock latency issues

 Often results in reopening closed partitions
 Impacts design cycle time and resources

 Routing Resource Planning

The floorplanner must:

  • Plan metal channels at the beginning
  • Reserve routing resources inside partitions
  • Prevent internal block routing from blocking top-level routes

 Early routing planning avoids late-stage iterations.

 Final Note

Similarly, various issues must be addressed during the floorplan stage.

1.2.2: Design Partitioning

Partitioning is the process of dividing the chip into small blocks.
This is done mainly to:

  • Separate different functional blocks
  • Make placement and routing easier

 Why Partitioning is Needed

Partitioning helps manage:

  • Large chip size
  • High design complexity
  • Routing congestion
  • Timing closure challenges

By dividing the chip into smaller, manageable blocks, the overall design becomes easier to handle.

 Partitioning in RTL Design Phase

Partitioning can be done during the RTL design phase, where:

  • The design engineer divides the entire design into sub-blocks
  • Each sub-block is designed as an independent module
  • All modules are connected together in the main module, called the:

 TOP LEVEL module

 Logical Partitioning

This type of partitioning is commonly referred to as:

Logical Partitioning

In logical partitioning:

  • The focus is on functionality
  • Each block represents a specific function
  • Connectivity between blocks is clearly defined at RTL level

 Goal of Partitioning

The main goal of partitioning is to:

Split the circuit such that the number of connections between partitions is minimized

 Fewer interconnections result in:

  • Easier routing
  • Better timing
  • Reduced congestion
  • Improved power and performance

 Key Takeaway

Good partitioning at early stages directly improves physical design quality and reduces iterations later in the flow.

1.2.3: Placement

Placement is the process of finding a suitable physical location for each cell in a block.
This selection of location is made to optimize several parameters, such as:

 Interconnection
  Cell density
  Timing
  Power
  Area

 Optimization During Placement

During placement:

  • Interconnection modelling is performed
  • Cost estimation is done
  • Multiple placement solutions are evaluated

These steps help the tool and the designer choose the most optimized placement solution.

 Placement Quality Comparison

The picture below shows two different solutions to the placement problem:

 Left Side Placement

  • Least optimized
  • Complex and longer interconnections

 Right Side Placement

  • More optimized
  • Simpler and shorter interconnections

Because the interconnections are much simpler, the right-side placement results in:

  • Better timing
  • Lower power
  • Reduced congestion

 Key Insight

Good placement directly impacts routing quality, timing closure, power consumption, and overall chip performance.

1.2.4: Clock Tree Synthesis (CTS)

 Objective of CTS

The goal of clock tree synthesis (CTS) is to minimize skew and insertion delay.

 Clock Behavior Before and After CTS

  • The clock is not propagated before CTS
  • After CTS, hold slack should improve

 Clock Tree Definition

The clock tree:

  • Begins at the .sdc defined clock source
  • Ends at the stop pins of a flop

There are two types of stop pins:

  •  Ignore pins
  •  Sync pins

 Ignore Pins Concept

  • 'Don't touch' circuits and pins defined in the front end (logic synthesis)
  • Are treated as 'ignore' circuits or pins in the back end (physical synthesis)
  • Ignore pins are ignored for timing analysis

 If the clock is divided, then a separate skew analysis is necessary.

 Types of Clock Skew

 Global Skew

  • Achieves zero skew between two synchronous pins
  • Does not consider the logic relationship

 Local Skew

  • Achieves zero skew between two synchronous pins
  • Considers the logic relationship

 Useful Skew

  • Clock is intentionally skewed
  • Done to improve setup slack
  • Known as useful skew

 Clock Tree Optimization (CTO)

  • Clock can be shielded
  • Prevents noise coupling with other signals
  •  Shielding increases area by 12% to 15%

Because the clock is global in nature:

  • The same metal layer used for power routing
  • Is also used for the clock

 CTO Techniques

Clock Tree Optimization is achieved by:

  • Buffer sizing
  • Gate sizing
  • Buffer relocation
  • Level adjustment
  • HFN (High Fanout Net) synthesis

 Setup vs Hold Strategy

  • Setup slack improvement is attempted:
  • Pre-placement
  • Placement
  • Post-placement optimization
  • Hold slack is neglected before CTS

 After CTS:

  • Hold slack is improved during post-placement optimization

 Buffer Insertion Impact

  • CTS introduces a large number of buffers
  • Generally:
  • For 100k gates
  • Around 650 buffers are added

 Clock Tree Levels

  • The colors represent different levels within the clock tree
  • A level is determined by:
  • How many buffers the clock signal passes through
  • Before reaching that point

1.2.5: Routing

 Definition

The process of the interconnection of cells to complete the netlist is called routing.

 Types of Routing

Routing can be divided into the following categories:

 Grid Routing
  Global Routing
  Detailed Routing
  Clock Routing
  Power Routing

 Global Routing

  • Global routing is more abstract
  • Provides only a rough route for the interconnections
  • Used to estimate routing resources and congestion

 Detailed Routing

  • Detailed routing shows the precise paths or routes
  • Actual metal layers, vias, and tracks are assigned
  • Ensures:
  • DRC compliance
  • Correct connectivity
  • Manufacturability

 Clock Routing

  • Clock routing is of specific interest
  • The clock is connected to all sequential elements in the design
  • This procedure can also be called Clock Tree Synthesis (CTS)

 Clock Tree Synthesis (CTS)

CTS is a technique used to:

  • Distribute the clock equally to all sequential elements
  • Maintain:
  • Minimum clock skew
  • Minimum clock latency

 Student Note

  • Routing directly impacts:
  • Timing
  • Power
  • Signal integrity
  • Design closure
  • Poor routing can cause:
  • Timing violations
  • Congestion
  • DRC errors

1.2.6: Physical Verification

 Physical Verification

Physical verification checks the correctness of the generated layout design.

 This includes verifying that the layout:

 1. Design Rule Checking (DRC)

 Complies with all technology requirements
  Ensures layout follows foundry-defined rules

 2. Layout vs. Schematic (LVS)

 Ensures the layout is consistent with the original netlist
  Confirms logical correctness vs physical implementation

 3. Antenna Rule Checking

 Verifies that the design has no antenna effects
  Prevents gate oxide damage during fabrication

 4. Density Verification

 Performed at the full chip level
  Cleaning density is a very critical step in lower technology nodes

 5. Electrical Rule Checking (ERC)

 Ensures the layout complies with all electrical requirements
  Detects issues such as:

  • Floating gates
  • Power/ground shorts
  • Missing connections

 Student Takeaway

Physical verification is the final safety gate before tape-out.
Any failure here can result in first silicon failure, especially in advanced nodes.

1.2.7: Layout Post-processing

 Layout Post Processing

Layout Post Processing, also known as mask data preparation, often concludes with
physical design and verification.

 Purpose

It converts the physical layout (polygons) into mask data
 (instructions for the photomask writer).

 It includes:

 1. Chip Finishing

  • Inserting company / chip labels
  • Adding final structures such as:
  • Seal ring
  • Filler structures

 2. Reticle Layout Generation

  • Generating a reticle layout
  • Includes:
  • Test patterns
  • Alignment marks

 3. Layout-to-Mask Preparation

  • Extends layout data with graphics operations
  • Includes:
  • Resolution Enhancement Technologies (RET)
  • Adjusts layout data to:
  • Mask production devices
  • Photomask writer

 Student Understanding Note

Layout post-processing is the final transformation step that prepares the verified layout
for actual silicon manufacturing.

1.2.8: Import Design

Inputs to the Physical Design Flow

  • Library models
  • Technology Data
  • Design Netlist
  • Timing constraints
  • Power Network (Pushdown from top level / Build from bottom-up)

Overview

The first stage in the physical design flow is reading in the netlist and the design constraints to your tool of choice. However, every tool uses pretty much the same flow and even the same format files.

1. Gate Level Netlist

Once you choose a process and a library, a synthesis tool will translate your RTL into a collection of interconnected logic gates that define the logic. The most common format is Verilog.

2. Standard Cell Library

In digital design, you have a ready-made standard cell library which will be used for synthesis and subsequent layouts. Your netlist will have an instantiation of these cells. For digital layout, you need layout and timing abstracts for these cells.

3. Layout Model

An abstract model of the standard cell layout is used instead of the complete layout. This will have PINs defined, to facilitate automatic routing by the tool as per your netlist.

  • Synopsys IC Compiler uses FRAM views as a PnR abstract
  • FRAM view is a cell view that has only the PINs and metal and via blockages defined
  • This ensures:
  • Automatic routing between PINs
  • No routing over existing metal/via areas
  • Avoidance of shorts

4. Timing Model

Tools also need a timing model in the form of a .lib file. ICC takes a .db file, which is generated from a .lib.

  • Liberty file contains timing numbers for various arcs
  • Timing is generally represented using lookup models
  • .lib files may also include cell power information

5. Technology File

The rules pertaining to the selected process must be provided to the PNR tool. This includes:

  • Metal widths
  • Metal spacing
  • Via definitions

Tool-specific formats:

  • ICC → Milkyway techfile format
  • EDI tools → Technology LEF file

6. Timing Constraints

SDC files define the timing constraints of the design. These include:

  • Clock definitions
  • False paths
  • Input delay constraints
  • Output delay constraints

Apart from these, some general setups are also done at this stage:

  • Search path setting
  • Timing and optimization setup
  • Scenario files

 This section describes all mandatory inputs required before starting physical implementation.

2. EDA Tools in VLSI

What is EDA?

EDA stands for Electronic Design Automation.
EDA is also known as Electronic Computer-Aided Design (ECAD).

Electronic Design Automation, or EDA, is a market segment consisting of software, hardware, and services with the collective goal of assisting in the definition, planning, design, implementation, verification, and the subsequent manufacturing of semiconductor devices (chips).

Semiconductor Manufacturing Context

Regarding the manufacturing of these devices, the primary providers of this service are semiconductor foundries or fabs. These highly complex and costly facilities are either:

  • Owned and operated by large, vertically integrated semiconductor companies, or
  • Operated as independent “pure-play” manufacturing service providers

This latter category has become the dominant business model in the semiconductor industry.

Features of EDA Tools

EDA tools use multiple features such as:

  • Logical design
  • Circuit schematic design
  • Layout generation
  • Design simulation
  • And many more features for VLSI design

Role of EDA in Chip Manufacturing

While EDA solutions are not directly involved in the manufacture of chips, they play a critical role in three major ways:

1. Technology Computer-Aided Design (TCAD)

EDA tools are used to design and validate the semiconductor manufacturing process to ensure that it delivers the required:

  • Performance
  • Density

This segment of EDA is called Technology Computer-Aided Design (TCAD).

2. Design for Manufacturability (DFM)

EDA tools are used to verify that a design meets all the requirements of the manufacturing process.

Deficiencies in this area can cause:

  • The chip to not function, or
  • Function at reduced capacity
  • Create reliability risks

This area of focus is known as Design for Manufacturability (DFM).

3. Silicon Lifecycle Management (SLM)

This is a relatively new area.

After the chip is manufactured, there is a growing requirement to:

  • Monitor device performance from post-manufacturing test
  • Track performance during deployment in the field

The goal is to:

  • Ensure the device performs as expected throughout its lifetime
  • Ensure the device is not tampered with

This application is referred to as Silicon Lifecycle Management (SLM).

Semiconductor Intellectual Property (IP)

Another market segment closely associated with EDA is Semiconductor Intellectual Property (IP).

This market segment provides:

  • Pre-designed circuits of varying complexity
  • Circuits that can be used as-is or adapted for specific applications

Semiconductor IP allows highly complex chips to be designed in far less time by reusing existing design work.

Due to the strong dependence of IP use and reuse on EDA tools, EDA and IP markets are typically viewed as one.

Popular EDA Tools in the Market

List of Electronic Design Automation (EDA) tools:

  1. Cadence Virtuoso
  2. Synopsys
  3. Mentor Graphics
  4. Xilinx
  5. Tanner
  6. Electric
  7. Silvaco
  8. Glade
  9. Alliance

 2.1: Input files to the Physical design flow

 Table: 2.1 – Input Files for Physical Design Flow

Name of the Input

File format

Developed by

Netlist

.v (verilog)

Synthesis team

SDC

.sdc (written in TCL)

Synthesis team

Timing library / logical library

.lib (liberty file)

Vendors like TSMC

Physical library

.lef (layout exchange format)

Vendors like TSMC

Technology file

.techlef / .tf

Foundry

TLU+ / table look up

.tlup

Foundry

 1. Netlist (.v)

Netlist is a textual description of a circuit made of components.
Components are generally gates, so generally a Netlist is a connection of gates.
A netlist can also be a connection of resistors, capacitors, or transistors, which is a netlist when used in analog simulation tools like spice.
The scope of this site is limited to 'gate' level netlist only.

A netlist can be written by hand, but more generally it's the output of the process called synthesis.
In this case the netlist then corresponds to a description of a design written using an HDL when written in an RTL style.
The netlist is then supposed to perform the same function as the corresponding HDL code.

The netlist out of the synthesis tool is then fed into the backend.
During this process, the netlist may be modified but remains functionally equal to its corresponding HDL code.

The netlist then written by the backend tool after the floorplan, placement and Routing has been done is often called post-layout-netlist.
The noticeable difference between the pre-layout-netlist and post-layout-netlist is the inclusion of clock tree buffers in the post-layout-netlist.

 Example of Netlist

module and_gate(y,a, b);

input a, b;

output y;

AND2 U1(.Y(y),.A(a), .B(b));

endmodule

 2. Synopsys Design Constraints (.sdc)

An important stage in the physical design flow is to set the timing and design constraints.
SDC is a short form of "Synopsys Design Constraint".

SDC is a common format for constraining the design which is supported by almost all synthesis, PnR and other tools.
Generally, timing, power and area constraints of design are provided through the SDC file and this file has an extension .sdc.
SDC is based on the tool command language (Tcl).

SDC file contains the following information:

  • i. SDC version (optional)
  • ii. SDC units (optional)
  • iii. Design Constraints
  • iv. Design Objects
  • v. Comments (optional)

The information specified in design constraints is operating conditions, wire load models, system interface, design rule constraints, timing constraints, timing exceptions, area constraints, multi-voltage & power optimization constraints and logic assignments.

Objects can be design, clock, port, pin, cell, net, library, etc.

 Example of SDC

# Constrain clock port clk with a 10-ns requirement

create_clock -period 10 [get_ports clk]

# Constrain the input I/O path

set_input_delay -clock clk -max 3 [all_inputs]

set_input_delay -clock clk -min 2 [all_inputs]

# Constrain the output I/O path

set_output_delay -clock clk -max 3 [all_inputs]

 3. Timing Library / Logical Library (.lib or .db)

This file is also called the Liberty timing file.
The .lib file is an ASCII representation of the timing and power parameters associated with any cell in particular semiconductor technology.

Lib file is basically a timing model file that contains:

  • cell delay
  • cell transition time
  • setup and hold time requirement

Lib file is generated and provided to ASIC designer by a standard cell library vendor or Foundry.

Common part of the Lib file contains

  • i. Library name and technology name
  • ii. Units (time, power, voltage, current, resistance, capacitance)
  • iii. Operating conditions (Process, Voltage, Temperature – Max/Min/Typical)
  • iv. Separate lib files for Max, Min and Typical corners

Cell-specific information

  • i. Cell name
  • ii. PG Pin name
  • iii. Area of cell
  • iv. Leakage power
  • v. Pin details

 Example of .lib

cell (AN2D0BWP40P140LVT) {

area: 0.504;

cell_footprint: "an2d1";

pg_pin (VDD) {

pg_type: primary_power;

voltage_name: COREVDD1;

}

pg_pin (VSS) {

pg_type: primary_ground;

voltage_name: COREGND1;

}

}

Characterization Techniques

  • i. CCS (Composite Current Source)
  • ii. NLDM (Non-Linear Delay Model)

CCS Lib file is more accurate, NLDM Lib file has faster runtime and smaller size.

 4. Library Exchange Format (.lef)

An ASCII data format used to describe a standard cell library.
Includes the design rules for routing and the abstract of the cells.
No information about the internal netlist of the cells.

LEF file contains

  • i. Technology
  • ii. Site
  • iii. Macros

 Example of .lef

MACRO AN2D0BWP40P140LVT

CLASS CORE;

SIZE 0.560 BY 0.900;

PIN Z

DIRECTION OUTPUT;

PORT

LAYER M1;

RECT 0.485 0.125 0.535 0.755;

END

END Z

 5. Technology File (.tf)

Provides information regarding the desired technology used for physical implementation.
Includes metal widths, spacing, via definitions, etc.

 Example of .tf

Technology {

unitTimeName = "ns"

unitLengthName = "micron"

gridResolution = 5

}

 6. Interconnect Technology File (.itf)

Describes the process cross-section and connectivity.
Used to extract RC values for the chip.
Can be used to generate TLUPlus files.

 7. TLU+ File (.tluplus)

A binary file that contains advanced process effects for RC extraction.
Used to calculate net delays accurately.
Generated from ITF file.

Functions

  1. Finds R, C parasitics
  2. Used for calculating Net Delays
  3. Uses Min, Max TLU+ and MAP file
  4. MAP file maps .tf and .itf

2.1 Input Files to the Physical Design Flow

Physical design requires several input files generated by front-end tools and foundries. These files define the functionality, timing, physical rules, and technology details needed to implement the design in silicon.

Table 2.1: Input Files for Physical Design

Input Name

File Format

Developed By

Netlist

.v (Verilog)

Synthesis Team

Timing Constraints

.sdc (TCL-based)

Synthesis Team

Timing / Logical Library

.lib / .db

Foundry / Vendors (e.g., TSMC)

Physical Library

.lef

Foundry / Vendors

Technology File

.tf / .techlef

Foundry

Interconnect Tech File

.itf

Foundry

Parasitic Model

.tluplus

Foundry

1. Netlist (.v)

A netlist is a textual representation of a circuit describing how logic gates are connected.
In digital physical design, we mainly deal with gate-level netlists.

Key Points:

  • Generated by synthesis tools from RTL (Verilog/VHDL)
  • Functionally equivalent to RTL
  • Used as backend input for floorplanning, placement, and routing
  • Post-layout netlist includes additional elements such as clock tree buffers

Example:

module and_gate(y,a,b);

input a, b;

output y;

AND2 U1(.Y(y), .A(a), .B(b));

endmodule

2. Synopsys Design Constraints (SDC – .sdc)

SDC defines timing, area, power, and design constraints for synthesis and physical design tools.

Characteristics:

  • Written in TCL
  • Supported by almost all EDA tools
  • Controls how the design is optimized

Contents of an SDC File:

  1. SDC version (optional)
  2. Units (optional)
  3. Design constraints
  4. Design objects (ports, clocks, cells, nets)
  5. Comments (optional)

Example:

create_clock -period 10 [get_ports clk]

set_input_delay -clock clk -max 3 [all_inputs]

set_input_delay -clock clk -min 2 [all_inputs]

set_output_delay -clock clk -max 3 [all_outputs]

3. Timing / Logical Library (.lib / .db)

Also called the Liberty file, this contains timing, power, and electrical models of standard cells.

Key Features:

  • ASCII format (.lib) or binary (.db)
  • Provided by foundry or standard cell vendor
  • Used by synthesis and timing analysis tools

Common Information:

  • Library name and technology
  • Units (time, voltage, power, capacitance)
  • PVT corners (Min, Max, Typical)

Cell-Specific Information:

  • Cell name
  • Area
  • Power (leakage and dynamic)
  • Pin details (direction, capacitance, timing arcs)

Timing Models:

  • NLDM – Faster, less accurate
  • CCS – More accurate, larger size, slower

4. Library Exchange Format (.lef)

LEF describes the physical abstract view of standard cells and routing rules.

LEF Contains:

  1. Technology information (layers, vias)
  2. Site definitions
  3. Macro definitions (cells)

Layer Attributes:

  • Layer type (routing, cut, masterslice)
  • Width, spacing, pitch
  • Routing direction
  • Resistance and capacitance
  • Antenna rules

Purpose:

  • Used by place-and-route tools
  • Does not include internal cell netlist

5. Technology File (.tf / .techlef)

Defines process technology rules used during physical implementation.

Includes:

  • Metal layer stack information
  • Via definitions
  • Design rules (min width, spacing)
  • Electrical units and precision
  • Display properties

Tool Dependency:

  • Synopsys → .tf (Milkyway)
  • Cadence → .techlef

6. Interconnect Technology File (.itf)

ITF describes process cross-sections and is used to model RC parasitics.

Contains:

  • Conductor layers
  • Dielectric layers
  • Via definitions
  • Thickness, dielectric constant (ER)
  • Resistance per square

Usage:

  • Used to generate TLU+ files
  • Helps in RC extraction when TLU+ is unavailable

7. TLU+ File (.tluplus)

TLU+ is a binary lookup table used for accurate RC extraction.

Key Points:

  • Technology dependent
  • Provides resistance and capacitance per unit length
  • Accounts for width, spacing, density, temperature

Files Required:

  1. Max TLU+
  2. Min TLU+
  3. Map file (maps layers between .tf and .itf)

Purpose:

  • Accurate net delay calculation
  • Preferred over ITF for signoff-quality extraction

3. Synthesis

Synthesis converts RTL design into a gate-level netlist while meeting timing, area, and power constraints.

Main Steps:

  1. RTL to generic logic
  2. Mapping to technology cells
  3. Optimization based on constraints

Common Tools:

  • Synopsys Design Compiler
  • Cadence Genus

3.1 Logic Synthesis

Logic synthesis maps RTL to logic gates using timing libraries and constraints.

Goals:

  • Optimize timing, area, and power
  • Generate netlist for PnR

Challenge:

At advanced nodes, physical effects (wire delay, IR drop, parasitics) cause mismatch between logical and physical behavior, leading to multiple iterations.

3.2 Physical Synthesis

Physical synthesis considers placement and routing effects early in synthesis.

Advantages:

  • Early floorplanning
  • Congestion-aware optimization
  • Accurate parasitic estimation
  • Fewer iterations (shift-left approach)

Result:

  • Predictable and convergent design flow
  • Faster time-to-market
  • Better quality silicon

Goals of Synthesis

  1. Generate gate-level netlist
  2. Insert clock gating
  3. Optimize logic
  4. Insert DFT logic (scan, BIST)
  5. Maintain RTL–netlist equivalence

3.3 Inputs to the Synthesis Tool

  1. Technology file (.tf)
  2. Timing library (.lib)
  3. RTL code (.v)
  4. Timing constraints (.sdc)
  5. Power intent (.upf)
  6. Scan configuration (.scandef)

Synthesis Flow of a Digital Design

The diagram illustrates the major stages involved in the synthesis of a digital design.
The synthesis process begins with RTL code written in Verilog or VHDL and ends with the generation of a gate-level netlist along with associated constraint and test files.

At the RTL stage, the design is described purely in logical terms. There is no concept of physical hardware such as wires, transistors, or physical nodes. Instead, the design represents functionality using high-level constructs that describe how data flows and is processed.

The synthesis flow starts from RTL and produces the following key outputs:

  • Gate-Level Netlist
  • SDC (Timing Constraints)
  • UPF (Power Intent)
  • SCANDEF (DFT Information)

1. RTL (Register Transfer Level)

RTL stands for Register Transfer Level, which is a design abstraction used to model synchronous digital circuits in terms of:

  • Data transfer between registers
  • Logical operations performed on the data

RTL is written using Hardware Description Languages (HDLs) such as Verilog or VHDL.

Key Characteristics:

  • Describes what the circuit does, not how it is physically implemented
  • Independent of technology
  • Forms the foundation for all downstream design stages
  • Most modern digital designs are created at the RTL level

2. Analyze and Elaborate

In this stage, the RTL design is read into the synthesis tool (e.g., Synopsys Design Compiler).

This stage consists of two steps:

  • Analyze:
  • Checks HDL syntax
  • Parses the design files
  • Converts HDL code into an internal representation
  • Elaborate:
  • Resolves module hierarchy
  • Binds parameters
  • Creates a complete design database

At the end of this stage, the synthesis tool has a fully resolved logical model of the design.

3. Read Constraints (SDC) and Power Intent (UPF)

Once the design is elaborated, constraints are applied.

Design Constraints:

Constraints instruct the synthesis tool how to optimize the design.

They are mainly derived from design specifications such as:

  • Performance targets
  • Power requirements
  • Interface timing

Types of Constraints:

  1. Design Rule Constraints
  • Maximum fanout
  • Maximum transition
  • Maximum capacitance
  1. Optimization Constraints
  • Clock definitions
  • Input/output delays
  • Multicycle paths
  • False paths
  • Area and power goals

4. Unified Power Format (UPF)

UPF specifies the power intent of the design.

Purpose of UPF:

  • Defines power domains
  • Specifies power rails and ground rails
  • Describes power switching behavior
  • Defines level shifters, isolation cells, and retention registers

Key Feature:

UPF is applied consistently across all design stages:

  • RTL
  • Gate-level
  • Place and Route

This ensures power-aware design implementation throughout the flow.

5. Clock Gating

Clock gating is a power optimization technique used to reduce dynamic power consumption.

How Clock Gating Works:

  • Disables the clock to portions of the design when they are idle
  • Prevents unnecessary switching of flip-flops
  • Reduces clock tree activity

Key Points:

  • Saves significant dynamic power
  • Adds additional gating logic
  • Widely used in low-power designs
  • Approaches the efficiency of asynchronous circuits as gating granularity increases

6. Compile (Logic Mapping and Optimization)

This is the core synthesis stage.

Compile Process:

  1. Maps high-level RTL constructs to generic logic
  2. Performs Boolean logic optimization
  3. Maps optimized logic to technology-specific standard cells
  4. Checks and fixes design rule violations
  5. Optimizes timing to meet SDC constraints
  6. Performs area recovery without violating timing

The synthesis tool continuously balances:

  • Timing
  • Area
  • Power

7. DFT Insertion (Design for Testability)

DFT logic is added to ensure the chip can be tested efficiently after fabrication.

Purpose of DFT:

  • Improve controllability of internal nodes
  • Improve observability of internal signals
  • Enable fault detection during manufacturing test

Common DFT Techniques:

  • Scan chains
  • Boundary scan
  • Built-In Self-Test (BIST)

DFT insertion is critical for achieving high test coverage and reducing test cost.

8. Output Files of Synthesis

After synthesis is complete, the following outputs are generated:

  1. Gate-Level Netlist (.v)
  • Technology-mapped logical representation
  1. SDC (.sdc)
  • Updated timing constraints
  1. UPF (.upf)
  • Power intent specification
  1. SCANDEF (.scandef)
  • Scan chain configuration for DFT

These outputs are then passed to the Physical Design (Place & Route) flow.

. Clock in Digital Systems

With the rapid growth in digital devices, most modern systems are synchronous in nature and consist of flip-flops as fundamental storage elements.
The transition of a digital system from one state to the next occurs synchronously, and this transition is governed by a signal known as the clock.

The clock defines when data is sampled and when state changes occur in the system.

Importance of Clock Signals

Clock signals are often referred to as the heartbeat of digital systems.
The correct and stable operation of a digital system depends heavily on the quality of the clock signal.

Ideal Clock Characteristics:

  • Zero clock skew
  • Minimum rise time and fall time
  • Fixed duty cycle (ideally 50%)
  • Low jitter and noise

Practical Clock Characteristics:

In real systems:

  • Clock skew is non-zero
  • Rise and fall times are finite
  • Duty cycle can deviate from 50%

In large digital systems, up to 10% of the clock period may be allocated to tolerate clock skew. This issue is especially critical in high-speed VLSI designs, where timing margins are very tight.

Simple Onchip Clock Generation using circuit using ring oscillators

Three Level Buffer Clock Distribution Network

Clock Mesh Type Clock DIstribution Network

On-Chip Clock Generation

A simple method for on-chip clock generation is the ring oscillator, which consists of an odd number of inverting stages connected in a loop.

  • Used in low-end microprocessors
  • Suitable for basic or low-performance applications
  • Not preferred for high-precision clocking due to jitter and PVT sensitivity

Key Clock Design Considerations for High-Speed VLSI

The following factors must be carefully considered during clock design, especially in high-speed digital systems:

1. Duty Cycle

  • Ideal duty cycle is 50%
  • A 50% duty cycle allows the clock to propagate more reliably through chains of inverting buffers
  • Duty cycle can be improved using feedback techniques based on voltage averaging

2. Rise Time and Fall Time

  • Excessively fast rise and fall times can cause signal reflections
  • Proper control of edge rates is necessary to maintain signal integrity

3. Load Capacitance

Clock load capacitance should be minimized by:

  • Reducing fan-out
  • Shortening interconnect lengths
  • Using smaller gate capacitances

Lower capacitance improves clock speed and reduces power consumption.

4. Characteristic Impedance Control

  • Clock line impedance should be reduced
  • Achieved by increasing the (w/h) ratio, where:
  • w = metal width
  • h = vertical distance from substrate
  • Lower impedance improves signal integrity

5. Inductive Compensation

  • Inductive loads can partially cancel parasitic capacitance
  • Acts as a matching network at the clock receiver
  • Helps improve clock signal quality

6. Crosstalk Reduction

  • Adequate spacing must be maintained between high-speed clock lines
  • Placing power or ground rails between clock lines helps shield them
  • Reduces noise coupling and timing uncertainty

Types of Clock Signals

Generated Clocks

A generated clock is derived from another clock within the design.

Examples:

  • Clock divider
  • PLL output
  • Frequency-multiplied clock

Generated clocks require separate timing and skew analysis.

Gated Clocks

A gated clock is a clock signal controlled by gating logic.

Purpose:

  • Reduce dynamic power consumption
  • Disable clock when a circuit block is inactive

Note:

Improper clock gating can introduce glitches and timing violations, so clock gating cells are typically used.

Summary

  • The clock governs all synchronous operations in a digital system
  • Clock quality directly impacts performance, power, and reliability
  • Clock design is one of the most critical and challenging aspects of VLSI design
  • Proper handling of skew, duty cycle, crosstalk, and loading is essential for high-speed designs


4.1: D-Type Latch

A latch is a basic storage element that can retain data under specific conditions.
There are several types of latches, such as D-type latches and RS (Reset–Set) latches.
As an example, the operation of a D-type latch is described below.

D-Type Latch Operation

A D-type latch consists of the following signals:

  • D (Data input)
  • LE (Latch Enable)
  • Q (Output)

The behavior of the D-type latch depends on the value of the Latch Enable (LE) signal:

  • When LE = Low (0)
  • The output Q retains its previous value
  • Changes in D do not affect the output
  • When LE = High (1)
  • The output Q follows the input D
  • Any change in D is immediately reflected at Q

Thus, the latch is said to be transparent when LE is High and opaque when LE is Low.

Timing Diagram

The timing diagram of a D-type latch illustrates:

  • The transparency of the latch when LE is High
  • The data-holding behavior when LE is Low

4.2: D-Type Flip-Flop

A flip-flop is a storage element that can retain data under specific conditions.
The term flip-flop is often abbreviated as FF.
There are several types of flip-flops, such as D-type and JK flip-flops.
As an example, the operation of a D-type flip-flop is described below.

Difference Between D-Type Latch and D-Type Flip-Flop

A D-type flip-flop differs from a D-type latch in the following way:

  • A D-type flip-flop retains output data even after the clock becomes inactive (Low in this example).
  • A D-type latch transfers data from D to Q while the Latch Enable (LE) input is High.

D-Type Flip-Flop Pins

A D-type flip-flop consists of:

  • D – Input data pin
  • CK – Clock pin
  • Q – Output data pin

Operation of a D-Type Flip-Flop

  • The flip-flop latches input data (D) on the rising edge of the clock (CK).
  • The latched data is transferred to the output Q.
  • Q remains unchanged until the next rising edge of CK, regardless of changes at input D.
  • In other words, Q retains the input data (D) latched at the previous rising edge of CK.

The timing diagram of a D-type flip-flop illustrates this behavior.

Clear and Preset Pins

Some flip-flops include additional control pins:

  • CLR (Clear)
  • PR (Preset)

These pins are used to initialize the internal state of the flip-flop to a known value.

Applications of Flip-Flops

Flip-flops are commonly used for:

  • Synchronizers for asynchronous signals
  • Delay circuits for digital signals
  • Counters
  • Frequency dividers

Operation of a D-Type Flip-Flop (Internal Structure)

A D-type flip-flop is constructed using two D-type latches.

Rising Edge of Clock (CK)

  • When a rising clock edge is applied to CK:
  • D-type latch #1 becomes active.
  • While the clock is High, latch #1 remains active.
  • The first clocked inverter in D-type latch #2 is also active.
  • The data stored in latch #1 is transferred to the output Q
     (highlighted by the blue arrow).
  • The output Q remains unchanged, even if the input changes.

Falling Edge of Clock (CK)

  • When a falling clock edge is applied to CK:
  • D-type latch #2 becomes active.
  • The data stored in latch #2 continues to appear at the output Q
     (highlighted by the green arrow).
  • Again, the output Q remains unchanged, even if the input changes.

Important Note

  • The output value Q is unknown until a known input is latched on the rising edge of the clock (CK).

In other words, if there is a toggling of data at the latch input close to negative edge (while the latch is closing), there will be an uncertainty as if data will be captured reliably or not. For data to be captured reliably, it must be available at the input of loop transmission gate at the arrival of closing clock edge. To be able to present at Node D at the closing edge, it must be there at latch input some time prior to the clock edge. This time in reaching from latch input to Node D is termed as setup time for this latch.

4.3.2: Latch Hold Time (Library Hold Time for Latch)

When the data is not propagated to the output, it must not cross the input transmission gate so that it does not disturb the present state of the latch.
This requirement serves as the hold time for the latch.

Assuming CLK′ takes one inverter delay, the input transmission gate will close only after one inverter delay.
Therefore, the hold time for data is:

Hold Time = One inverter delay − Transmission gate delay

Clock Inversion and Transmission Gate Closure

CLK′ is formed from CLK after a delay equivalent to one inverter delay.
Only after this delay will the input transmission gate switch off.

If we want the data not to propagate to the output, we must ensure that it does not cross the input transmission gate.
Hence, data should not be present at the transmission gate’s input at time:

T(inv) − T(tg)

Latch Hold Time Definition

In other words, the data must be held stable for this duration after the CLK edge.
This duration is defined as the hold time for the latch.

ta preserved

4.3.3: Flip-Flop Setup Time (Library Setup of Flop)

A master–slave negative edge–triggered D flip-flop using transmission gates and latches is shown in Figure 13.

For this flip-flop to capture data reliably, the data must be present at Node F at the arrival of the negative edge of the clock.

Data Propagation Path

To reach Node F, the data must propagate through the following path before the clock edge arrives:

Node A → Node B → Node C → Node D → Node E → Node F

Setup Time Explanation

To reach Node F at the closing edge of Latch 1, the data must be present at Node A at some earlier time.

The time taken by the data to travel from Node A to Node F is defined as the setup time of the flip-flop under consideration, assuming that CLK and CLK′ are present instantaneously.
If CLK and CLK′ are not instantaneous, their delays will be accounted for accordingly.

Key Observation

The setup time of a flip-flop can also be viewed as the setup time of the master latch.

4.3.4: Flip-flop Hold Time (Library setup of a flop)

From the above figure, if D input goes through some logic (say scan logic, enable logic, set-reset logic, etc.) before TG1 and is considered part of the Flip-flop:

  • There is a delay between CLK and CLK′
  • Transmission gates (TGs) take time to turn ON and turn OFF
  • Hence, it is necessary to maintain a stable value at the input

This stability ensures:

  • A stable value at Node F
  • Which is in turn necessary to get a stable output at Q

 Therefore, a Hold Time requirement exists

This is very similar to the latch Hold time.

4.3.5: Setup and Hold Checks

Setup Check

  • Setup check ensures that the design transitions to the next state as desired
  • It is mainly associated with state machine behavior
  • The setup check is performed at the next active clock edge relative to the edge at which data is launched
    → This is called the     default setup check

This corresponds to:

  • The requirement of the state machine to move to the next state
  • The possibility of meeting both setup and hold checks under delay variations across timing corners

Setup Check Example

For a timing path from:

  • Positive edge–triggered register → Negative edge–triggered register

The setup check shows:

  • Data launched by flop1 on the positive edge
  • Will be captured by flop2 on the forthcoming negative edge
  • The data must be stable at the input of flop2
  • At least setup time before the negative edge

Hold Check

  • Hold check ensures that the design does not move to the next state before the stipulated time
  • The design must retain its present state
  • The hold check is performed one active edge prior to the edge at which setup is checked
    (unless there is some     architectural care-about in the state machine design)

This hold check is called the default hold check

Default Hold Check Explanation

The default hold check corresponding to the default setup check (from Figure 15) shows that:

  • Data launched on the positive edge by flop1
  • Should be captured by the next negative edge
  • Not by the previous negative edge

4.3.6: Setup Time in a Sequential Circuit

Setup time is the required time duration that the input data MUST be stable before the triggering edge of the clock.

  • If data is changing within this setup time window, the input data might be lost
  • The data may not be stored in the flip-flop
  • Metastability might occur

Metastability
 When setup and hold time requirements are violated, the flip-flop state becomes unstable, and after an unpredictable duration, the state of the flip-flop can settle either way (1 or 0).

The output Q1 passes through the slow logic and arrives late at the input D2 of FF2, which leads to:

  • Setup time violation
  • Loss of new data

4.3.7: Hold Time in a Sequential Circuit

Hold time is the required duration that the input data MUST be stable after the triggering edge of the clock.

  • Similar to setup time violation
  • Hold time violation will cause data metastability
  • New data might not be correctly stored in the flip-flop

The output Q1 passes through the fast logic and arrives too early at the input D2 of FF2, which leads to:

  • Hold time violation
  • Metastability

4.4: Slack

Slack is the difference between the desired arrival time and the actual arrival time for a signal.

Slack time determines (for a timing path) whether the design is working at the desired frequency.

  • Positive Slack
     → Design is meeting timing and still has margin for improvement
  • Zero Slack
     → Design is critically working at the desired frequency
  • Negative Slack
     → Design has not achieved the specified timings at the specified frequency
    → Indicates a     timing violation

 Slack should always be positive.
Negative slack indicates a timing failure.

4.4.1: Required Time

The time within which data is required to arrive at some internal node of the design.

  • This value is specified by the designer
  • Set using timing constraints

4.4.2: Arrival Time

The time at which data actually arrives at the internal node.

  • Includes all net delays
  • Includes all logic delays
  • Measured from the reference input point to the destination node

Slack Equations

  • Setup Slack = Required Time − Arrival Time
  • Hold Slack = Arrival Time − Required Time

4.5: Minimum Time Period

The minimum time period (one cycle time) is required to acquire stable data for the succeeding stage processing.

  • To satisfy the minimum time period:
  • Minimum pulse width must be met

4.6: Minimum Pulse Width

The minimum pulse width check ensures that the clock pulse width is more than the required value.

  • It depends on:
  • Frequency of operation
  • Technology

Example:

  • If design frequency = 1 GHz
  • Clock period = 1 ns
  • With 50% duty cycle:
  • High pulse width = 0.5 ns
  • Low pulse width = 0.5 ns

4.7: Timing Analysis

(Suggested heading color: Dark Blue)

There are two ways we can do a timing analysis upon the design:

  1. STA (Static Timing Analysis)
  2. DTA (Dynamic Timing Analysis)

4.7.1: STA

(Suggested heading color: Teal / Dark Green)

Static timing analysis (STA) is a method of validating the timing performance of a design by
checking all possible paths for timing violations. STA breaks a design down into timing paths,
calculates the signal propagation delay along each path, and checks for violations of timing
constraints inside the design and at the input/output interface.

Another way to perform timing analysis is to use dynamic simulation, which determines the
full behaviour of the circuit for a given set of input stimulus vectors. Compared to dynamic
simulation, static timing analysis is much faster because it is not necessary to simulate the
logical operation of the circuit. STA is also more thorough because it checks all timing paths,
not just the logical conditions that are sensitized by a set of test vectors. However, STA can
only check the timing, not the functionality, of a circuit design.

Timing Paths in STA

(Suggested subheading color: Purple)

When performing timing analysis, STA first breaks down the design into timing paths. Each
timing path consists of the following elements:

  1. Start Point –
    The start of a timing path where data is launched by a clock edge or where
    the data must be available at a specific time. Every start point must be either
    an input port or a register clock pin.
  2. Combinational Logic Network –
    Elements that have no memory or internal state.
    Combinational logic can contain AND, OR, XOR, and inverter elements, but cannot
    contain flip-flops, latches, registers, or RAM.
  3. Endpoint –
    The end of a timing path where data is captured by a clock edge or where
    the data must be available at a specific time. Every endpoint must be either
    a register data input pin or an output port.

Timing Paths in a Simple Design

(Suggested heading color: Dark Blue)

The following figure shows the timing paths in a simple design example.

In the above example, each logic cloud represents a combinational logic network. Each path
starts at a data launch point, passes through some combinational logic, and ends at a data
capture point.

Timing Path Classification

(Suggested subheading color: Teal)

Path

Startpoint

Endpoint

Path1

Input port

Data input of a sequential element

Path2

Clock pin of a sequential element

Data input of a sequential element

Path3

Clock pin of a sequential element

Output port

Path4

Input port

Output port

A combinational logic cloud might contain multiple paths, as shown in the following figure.
STA uses the longest path to calculate a maximum delay and the shortest path to calculate
a minimum delay.

Types of Timing Paths Considered by STA

(Suggested heading color: Purple)

STA also considers the following types of paths for timing analysis:

  1. Clock path –
    A path from a clock input port or cell pin, through one or more buffers
    or inverters, to the clock pin of a sequential element; for data setup and
    hold checks.
  2. Clock-gating path –
    A path from an input port to a clock-gating element; for clock-gating
    setup and hold checks.
  3. Asynchronous path –
    A path from an input port to an asynchronous set or clear pin
    of a sequential element; for recovery and removal checks.

Delay Calculation in STA

(Suggested heading color: Dark Green)

After breaking down a design into a set of timing paths, an STA tool calculates the delay
along each path. The total delay of a path is the sum of all cell and net delays in the path.

Cell Delay

Cell delay is the amount of delay from input to output of a logic gate in a path. In the absence
of back-annotated delay information from an SDF file, the tool calculates the cell delay from
delay tables provided in the logic library for the cell.

Typically, a delay table lists the amount of delay as a function of one or more variables, such
as input transition time and output load capacitance. From these table entries, the tool
calculates each cell delay.

Net Delay

Net delay is the amount of delay from the output of a cell to the input of the next cell in a
timing path. This delay is caused by the parasitic capacitance of the interconnection
between the two cells, combined with net resistance and the limited drive strength of the
cell driving the net.

Timing Constraint Checks

(Suggested heading color: Dark Orange)

STA then checks for violations of timing constraints, such as setup and hold constraints.

Setup Constraint

A setup constraint specifies how much time is necessary for data to be available at the input
of a sequential device before the clock edge that captures the data in the device. This
constraint enforces a maximum delay on the data path relative to the clock edge.

Hold Constraint

A hold constraint specifies how much time is necessary for data to be stable at the input of
a sequential device after the clock edge that captures the data in the device. This constraint
enforces a minimum delay on the data path relative to the clock edge.

Example: Setup and Hold Checking in STA

(Suggested heading color: Blue)

The following example shows how STA checks setup and hold constraints for a flip-flop.

For this example, assume that the flip-flops are defined in the logic library to have a
minimum setup time of 1.0-time units and a minimum hold time of 0.0-time units.
The clock period is defined in the tool to be 10-time units. The time unit size, such as ns
or ps, is specified in the logic library.

By default, the tool assumes that signals are propagated through each data path in one
clock cycle. Therefore, when the tool performs a setup check, it verifies that the data
launched from FF1 reaches FF2 within one clock cycle and arrives at least 1.0-time unit
 before the data gets captured by the next clock edge at FF2.

If the data path delay is too long, it is reported as a timing violation. For this setup check,
the tool considers the longest possible delay along the data path and the shortest
possible delay along the clock path between FF1 and FF2.

When the tool performs a hold check, it verifies that the data launched from FF1 reaches
FF2 no sooner than the capture clock edge for the previous clock cycle. This check ensures
that the data already existing at the input of FF2 remains stable long enough after the clock
edge that captures data for the previous cycle.

For this hold check, the tool considers the shortest possible delay along the data path and
the longest possible delay along the clock path between FF1 and FF2. A hold violation can
occur if the clock path has a long delay.

Timing Exceptions in STA

(Suggested heading color: Maroon)

If certain paths are not intended to operate according to the default setup and hold
behaviour assumed by the STA tool, you need to specify those paths as timing exceptions.
Otherwise, the tool might incorrectly report those paths as having timing violations.

An STA tool may let you specify the following types of exceptions:

  1. False path –
    A path that is never sensitized due to the logic configuration, expected
    data sequence, or operating mode.
  2. Multicycle path –
    A path designed to take more than one clock cycle from launch to capture.
  3. Minimum or maximum delay path –
    A path that must meet a delay constraint that you explicitly specify as a
    time value.

An example of an STA tool is Prime Time; a single, golden, trusted signoff solution for
timing, signal integrity, power, and variation-aware analysis.

4.7.2: DTA (Dynamic Timing Analysis)

(Suggested heading color: Dark Blue)

Dynamic timing analysis verifies the functionality of the design by applying input vectors
and checking for correct output vectors whereas Static Timing Analysis checks static delay
requirements of the circuit without any input or output vectors.

Dynamic timing analysis has to be accomplished and the functionality of the design must be
cleared before the design is subjected to Static Timing Analysis (STA).

Dynamic Timing Analysis (DTA) and Static Timing Analysis (STA) are not alternatives to each
other. The quality of the Dynamic Timing Analysis (DTA) increases with the increase of input
test vectors. Increased test vectors increase simulation time.

Dynamic timing analysis can be used for synchronous as well as asynchronous designs.
Static Timing Analysis (STA) can't run on asynchronous designs and hence Dynamic Timing
Analysis (DTA) is the best way to analyze asynchronous designs.

Dynamic Timing Analysis (DTA) is also best suitable for designs having clocks crossing
multiple domains.

An example of a Dynamic Timing Analysis (DTA) tool is Modalism (from Mentor Graphics),
VCS (from Synopsys).

DTA is also carried out on the post-layout netlist to verify that the functionality of the design
has not changed. Test vectors remain the same for both.

Table 4.2: STA vs DTA (In Brief)

(Suggested heading color: Purple)
 (Table header background: Light Blue or Light Grey)
 (Text color: Black)

STA

DTA

Functionality is not required; paths are required

The functionality of the design must be cleared before the design is subjected to STA

STA is faster as the logic simulation is not a mandatory procedure

DTA is slower as the logic simulation is a mandatory procedure with numerous iterations of input vectors

STA is mainly used in synchronous design

DTA used on synchronous & asynchronous designs

5: STA VS DTA

(Suggested heading color: Dark Blue | Font size: 16–18 | Bold)

Static timing analysis is one of the techniques used to verify the timing of a digital design.
STA is static since the analysis of the design is carried out statically and does not depend
upon the data values being applied at the input pins of the design. STA is a complete and
exhaustive verification of all the timing checks of a design.

Timing analysis methods such as simulation can only verify the portions of the design that
get exercised by the stimulus. Verification through timing simulation is only as exhaustive
as the test vectors used. To simulate and verify all the timing conditions of a design with
10–100 million gates is very slow and timing cannot be verified completely.

So, STA on the other hand provides a faster and simpler way of checking and analyzing all
the timing paths in the design for any timing violations.

Dynamic Timing Analysis requires a comprehensive set of input vectors to check the timing
paths in the design. It determines the full behaviour of the circuit for a given set of input
vectors. Dynamic simulation can verify the functionality of the design as well as timing
requirements.

Figure 5.1:
 Graph depicting the Runtime memory and Design/Size/complexity with respect
to STA and DTA

Advantages of STA

(Suggested heading color: Green)

  1. All timing paths are considered for the timing analysis. This is not the case in simulation.
  2. Analysis times are relatively short when compared with event and circuit simulation.
  3. Timing can be analyzed for worst case, best case simultaneously. This type of analysis
    is not possible in dynamic timing analysis.
  4. Static Timing Analysis (STA) works with timing models. STA has more pessimism and
    thus gives maximum delay of the design. DTA performs full timing simulation. The
    problem associated with DTA is the computational complexity involved in finding the
    input patterns (vectors) that produce maximum delay at the output and hence it is slow.

Disadvantages of STA

(Suggested heading color: Red)

  1. All paths in the design may not run always in worst case delay. Hence the analysis is
    pessimistic.
  2. Clock related all information has to be fed to the design in the form of constraints.
  3. Inconsistency or incorrectness or under constraining of these constraints may lead
    to disastrous timing analysis.
  4. STA does not check for logical correctness of the design.
  5. STA is not suitable for asynchronous circuits.

Disadvantages of DTA

(Suggested heading color: Dark Orange)

  1. Unacceptable performance, except for functional checks.
  2. Incomplete timing coverage.
  3. Vector creation takes too long.

5.1: Synchronous Circuits

(Suggested heading color: Purple)

A synchronous circuit is a digital circuit in which the changes in the state of memory
elements are synchronized by a clock signal. In a sequential digital logic circuit, data are
stored in memory devices called flip-flops or latches.

5.2: Sequential Circuits

(Suggested heading color: Purple)

A sequential circuit is a combination of a combinational circuit and a memory element
connected in a feedback path. The memory elements are devices capable of storing binary
information within them.

The binary information stored in the memory elements at any given time defines the state
of the sequential circuit. The sequential circuit receives binary information from external
inputs. Thus, a sequential circuit is specified by a time sequence of inputs, outputs, and
internal states.

Types of Sequential Circuits

  1. Synchronous sequential circuit
  2. Asynchronous sequential circuit

5.3: Synchronous Sequential Circuit

(Suggested heading color: Teal)

A sequential circuit whose output behaviour depends on the input at a discrete time is called
synchronous sequential circuits. Synchronous sequential circuits that use clock pulses in
the inputs of memory elements are called clocked sequential circuits.

5.4: Asynchronous Sequential Circuit

(Suggested heading color: Teal)

The sequential circuit whose output depends on the sequence in which the input changes
are called asynchronous sequential circuits.

Information (such as delay, transition etc.) through a set of elements which can be
accumulated and verified against a specified set of rules.

A timing path can be supposed to be consisting of two sub-paths:

  • A reference path through which reference signal traverses
  • A constrained path through which constrained signal traverses

Both essentially originate from the same source (or have a definite relationship at their
respective sources). At the terminal end of both, there is a relationship governing the
arrival of the constrained signal to the arrival of the reference signal.

Depending upon the type of reference signal and constrained signal, the type of elements
encountered by these and the check that is formed between the two, we govern the type of
path.

Path Classification

(Suggested heading color: Brown)

A, Z and CLK are ports of the block/IP

  1. Path 1 is in2reg
  2. Path 2 is reg2reg
  3. Path 3 is reg2out
  4. Path 4 is in2out

We shall learn in detail about each of these paths in the next chapter.

6: Timing Closure

(Suggested heading color: Dark Blue | Bold | Font size: 16–18)

The layout of an integrated circuit (IC) must not only satisfy geometric requirements, e.g.,
non-overlapping cells and routability, but also meet the design's timing constraints, e.g.,
setup (long-path) and hold (short-path) constraints. The optimization process that meets
these requirements and constraints is often called timing closure.

Timing Optimization Overview

(Suggested subheading color: Teal)

Timing optimization engines must estimate circuit delays quickly and accurately to improve
circuit timing. Timing optimizers adjust propagation delays through circuit components,
with the primary goal of satisfying timing constraints, including—

  • Setup (long-path) constraints, specify the amount of time a data input signal should be
    stable (steady) before the clock edge for each storage element (e.g., flip-flop or latch).
  • Hold-time (short-path) constraints, specify the amount of time a data input signal should
    be stable after the clock edge at each storage element.

Setup Constraint Condition

(Suggested subheading color: Purple)

Setup constraints ensure that no signal transition occurs too late. Initial phases of timing
closure focus on these types of constraints, which are formulated as follows.

tcycle ≥ tcombDelay + tsetup + tskew

Here,

  • tcycle is the clock period
  • tcombDelay is the longest path delay through combinational logic
  • tsetup is the setup time of the receiving storage element (e.g., flip-flop)
  • tskew is the clock skew

Static Timing Analysis in Timing Closure

(Suggested subheading color: Green)

Checking whether a circuit meets set-up constraints requires estimating how long signal
transitions will take to propagate from one storage element to the next. Such delay
estimation is typically based on static timing analysis (STA), which propagates actual
arrival times (AATs) and required arrival times (RATs) to the pins of every gate or cell.

STA quickly identifies timing violations and diagnoses them by tracing out critical paths in
the circuit that are responsible for these timing failures.

STA Pessimism

(Suggested subheading color: Dark Orange)

Motivated by efficiency considerations, STA does not consider circuit functionality and
specific signal transitions. Instead, STA assumes that every cell propagates every 0–1 (1–0)
transition from its input(s) to its output and that every such propagation occurs with the
worst possible delay¹.

Therefore, STA results are often pessimistic for large circuits. This pessimism is generally
acceptable during optimization because it affects competing layouts equally, without
biasing the optimization toward a particular layout. It is also possible to evaluate the
timing of several competing layouts with more accurate techniques in order to choose the
best solution.

False Paths in STA

(Suggested subheading color: Brown)

One approach to mitigate pessimism in STA is to analyze the most critical paths. Some of
these can be false paths—those that cannot be sensitized by any input transition because
of the logic functions implemented by the gates or cells.

IC designers often enumerate false paths that are likely to become timing-critical to
exclude them from STA results and ignore them during timing optimization.

Timing Slack

(Suggested subheading color: Teal)

STA results are used to estimate how important each cell and each net are in a particular
layout. A key metric for a given timing point g—that is, a pin of a gate or cell—is
timing slack, the difference between g's RAT and AAT:

slack(g) = RAT(g) − AAT(g)

  • Positive slack indicates that timing is met
  • Negative slack indicates that timing is violated

Algorithms for timing-driven layout guide the placement and routing processes according
to timing slack values. Guided by slack values, physical synthesis restructures the netlist to
make it more suitable for high-performance layout implementation.

Hold-Time Constraints

(Suggested subheading color: Purple)

Hold-time constraints ensure that signal transitions do not occur too early. Hold violations
can occur when a signal path is too short, allowing a receiving flip-flop to capture the signal
at the current cycle instead of the next cycle.

The hold-time constraint is formulated as follows.

tcombDelay ≥ thold + tskew

Here,

  • tcombDelay is the delay of the circuit's combinational logic
  • thold is the hold time required for the receiving storage element
  • tskew is the clock skew

As clock skew affects hold-time constraints significantly more than setup constraints,
hold-time constraints are typically enforced after synthesizing the clock network.

Timing Closure Definition

(Suggested subheading color: Dark Blue)

Timing closure is the process of satisfying timing constraints through layout optimizations
and netlist modifications. It is common to use verbal expressions such as:

“The design has closed timing”

when the design satisfies all timing constraints.

6.1: Basic Terms in Timing Analysis

(Suggested heading color: Dark Blue | Bold | Font size: 16–18)

6.1.1: Timing Arcs

(Suggested subheading color: Teal)

A timing arc defines the propagation of signals through logic gates/nets and defines a timing
relationship between two related pins. The timing arc is one of the components of a timing
path. Static timing analysis works on the concept of timing paths. Each path starts from
either primary input or a register and ends at a primary output or a register.

In-between, the path traverses through what is known as timing arcs. We can define a
timing arc as an indivisible path/constraint from one pin to another that tells the EDA tool
to consider the path/relationship between the pins. For instance, AND, NAND, NOT, full
adder cell etc. gates have arcs from each input pin to each output pin. Also, sequential cells
such as flops and latches have arcs from clock pin to output pins and data pins. Net
connections can also be identified as timing arcs.

Common Terminology Related to Timing Arcs

(Suggested subheading color: Purple)

  1. Source pin: The pin from which the timing arc originates (pin IN1 and IN2 for cell arcs,
    pin OUT for net arc in figure1). This also means constraining pin in case of setup/hold
    timing checks (for example clock is the source pin for setup check)
  2. Sink pin: The pin at which timing arc ends (pin OUT for cell arc, pin AND2/IN2 for net
    arc in figure2). This also means constrained pin in case of setup/hold timing arcs (for
    example data pin is sink pin for setup check)

Types of Timing Arcs

(Suggested subheading color: Green)

Timing arcs can be categorized into two categories based upon the type of element they are
associated with – cell arcs and net arcs.

  1. Cell arcs: These are between an input pin and output pin of a cell. In other words, the
    source pin is an input pin of a cell and the sink pin is a pin of the same cell (output pin
    in case of delay arcs and input pin in case of timing check arcs). In the figure shown
    above, arcs (IN1 → OUT) and (IN2 → OUT) are cell arcs. Cell arcs are further divided
    into sequential and combinational arcs as discussed below.
  2. Net arcs: These arcs are between the driver pin of a net and the load pin of a net. In
    other words, the source pin is an output pin of one cell and the sink pin is an input pin
    of another cell. In the figure shown above, arc (OUT → IN2) is a net arc. Net arcs are
    always delay timing arcs.

Sequential and Combinational Arcs

(Suggested subheading color: Dark Orange)

Cell arcs can be sequential or combinational. Sequential arcs are between the clock pin of a
sequential cell and either input or output pin. Setup and hold arcs are between the input
data pin and clock pin and are termed as timing check arcs as they constrain a form of the
timing relationship between a set of signals.

The sequential delay arc is between the clock pin and output pin of sequential elements. An
example of a sequential delay arc is clk-to-q delay arc in a flip-flop. On the other hand,
combinational arcs are between input data and output data pins of a combinational cell or
block.

Timing Sense of an Arc (Unateness)

(Suggested subheading color: Brown)

Timing sense of an arc is defined as the sense of traversal from the source pin of the timing
arc to the sink pin of the timing arc. Timing sense is also called as “unateness” of the
timing arc. Timing sense can be positive unate, negative unate and non-unate.

  1. Positive unate timing arc: The unateness of an arc is said to be positive unate if rise
    transition at the source pin causes rise transition (if at all) at the sink pin and vice
    versa. Cells of type AND, OR gate etc. have positive unate arcs. All net arcs are
    positive unate arcs.
  2. Negative unate timing arc: The unateness of an arc is said to be negative unate if rise
    transition at the source pin causes fall transition at the sink pin and vice-versa.
    NAND, NOR and Inverter have negative unate arcs.
  3. Non-unate timing arc: If there is no such relationship between the source and sink
    pins of a timing arc, the arc is said to be non-unate. XOR and XNOR gates have non-
    unate timing arcs.

The existence of a timing arc is picked from liberty files. The cell has a function defined that
identifies if the arc is there from its input (say ‘x’) to output (say ‘y’). In most of the cases,
the value (delay, unateness, etc.) of the arc is also picked from liberty; but in case you have
read SDF, the delay is picked from SDF (Standard Delay Format) file (other properties
picked from liberty in this case also).

On the other hand, for net arcs, the existence of arc is picked from connectivity information
(netlist). The net arcs are calculated based on the parasitic values given in SPEF (Standard
Parasitics Exchange Format) file, or SDF (like in case above).

Timing arcs have a very important role in VLSI design industry. Whole of the optimization
process right from gate level netlist till final signoff revolves around timing arcs. The
presence of correct timing arcs in liberty file is very essential for high quality signoff or
there may not be correlation between simulation and silicon).

6.1.2: Clock to Q Delay of a Flip Flop

(Suggested subheading color: Teal)

Clock to Q delay or Tclkq is also called propagation delay of a flop. It is the time a flip-flop
