cell takes to represent a change in input to the output of the cell.

This means any valid transition (no glitch) on input will appear on output after a delay
called propagation delay of the cell.

A propagation delay time is the interval of time required after an input signal has been
applied for the resulting output change to occur. Four categories of propagation delay times
that are important in the operation of a flip-flop are:

Propagation delay as measured from the triggering edge of the clock pulse to the LOW-to-
HIGH transition of the output. This delay is shown below.

The clock-to-Q propagation delay specifies the amount of time after the rising edge of the
clock that Q outputs the new value. There is never any delay from D to Q. The clock input
is the signal that triggers the change, not the D input.

6.1.3: Setup Time

(Suggested subheading color: Purple)

Setup time is the required time duration that the input data MUST be stable before the
triggering edge of the clock. If data is changing within this setup time window, the input
data might be lost and not stored in the flip-flop as metastability might occur.

When setup and hold time requirements are violated, the flip-flop state becomes unstable,
and after an unpredictable duration, the state of the flip-flop can settle either way (1 or 0).
This scenario is known as metastability.

As shown in the following diagram, output Q1 passes through the slow logic and arrives late
at the input D2 of FF2, which leads to setup time violation and the loss of the new data.

6.1.4: Hold Time

(Suggested subheading color: Purple)

Figure 6.5(b): Setup timing waveform

Hold time is the required duration that the input data MUST be stable after the triggering
edge of the clock. Similar to setup time violation, hold time violation will cause data
metastability and new data might not be correctly stored in the flip-flop.

As shown in the following diagram, output Q1 passes through the fast logic and arrives too
early at the input D2 of FF2, which leads to hold time violation and metastability.

6.1.5: Setup Time Constraints

(Suggested heading color: Dark Blue | Bold)

The setup time constraint depends on the maximum delay from register R1 through the
combinational logic. The input to register R2 must be stable at least tsetup before the clock
edge.

Where,

Setup time equation

TCLK ≥ tpcq + tpd + tsetup

tpd ≤ TCLK − (tpcq + tsetup)

  • TCLK is the time period of the clock
  • tpcq is the clock-Q delay of the R1 register
  • tpd is the combinational logic delay
  • tsetup is the library setup time of the register R2

6.1.6: Hold Time Constraint

(Suggested heading color: Dark Blue | Bold)

The hold time constraint depends on the minimum delay from register R1 through the
combinational logic. The input to register R2 must be stable for at least thold after the clock
edge.

Where,

Hold time equation

thold < tcca + tcd

tcd > thold − tcca

  • tcca is the clock-Q delay of the R1 register
  • tcd is the combinational logic delay
  • thold is the library hold time of the register R2

6.2: Timing Paths in a Digital Design

(Suggested heading color: Dark Blue | Bold)

If a design fulfills both setup and hold constraints, the design is said to have achieved
timing closure. Static timing analysis will prove/disprove the setup and hold constraints by
analyzing all the timing paths in the design.

Any digital design can be divided into four categories of paths for static timing analysis.

  1. Input to Output (I2O)
  2. Input to Register (I2R)
  3. Register to Register (R2R)
  4. Register to Output (R2O)

Static timing analysis is done on all these paths one by one. Each path is analyzed separately
by defining the start and end points in that path. Let us first consider static timing analysis
for the R2R paths. They are simple and become basis for other paths.

6.2.1: Register–Register Timing Path

(Suggested subheading color: Teal)

For R2R paths, the start point is clock input pin of the launch flip-flop and end point is D
input pin of the latch flip-flop.

For setup constraints, the goal is to make sure that delay from start point to end point is at
least setup time of latch flip-flop less than clock period of the latch clock.

This means that,

TcQ + comb_delay(max) < clock delay path (clock skew) + T (clock period of clock) − tsetup of latch FF

comb_delay in below figure should be maximum delay through the combinatorial cloud.

For hold constraint, the goal is to make sure that delay through start and end point is at
least more than the hold time of the latch flip-flop in same clock cycle.

TcQ + comb_delay(min) > hold time of latch FF + clock delay path (clock skew)

comb_delay in below figure should be minimum delay through the combinatorial cloud.

One can observe that hold time does not depend on the clocking period of the clock.

6.2.2: Input–Register Timing Path

(Suggested subheading color: Teal)

For I2R paths, the start point will be input ports and the end point will be D pin of the latch
flip-flop. One can assume an input delay from the input port to the combinatorial cloud as
shown below. The input delay can include pad delay or any net delay.

If the input ports are synchronous to external clock, the paths can be constrained for setup
as,

input_delay + comb_delay(max) < clock delay path (clock skew) + T (clock period of clock) − setup time of latch FF

And for the hold constraints as,

input_delay + comb_delay(min) > hold time of latch FF + clock delay path (clock skew)

If input is not synchronous to clock, the path can be constrained with max and min delay
for the path from input port to D pin of the latch flip-flop.

Normally in such cases (a synchronous path latched by clock) requires two latching flip-
flops back-to-back to remove metastability issues.

input_delay + comb_delay(max) < max_delay (required)

input_delay + comb_delay(min) > min_delay (required)

6.2.3: Register–Output Timing Path

(Suggested subheading color: Teal)

For R2O paths, the start point will be clock pin of the launch flip-flop and the end point will
be output port. One can assume an output delay from the output of combinatorial cloud as
shown below. Output delay can include pad delay or the net delay from combinatorial cloud
to pad.

If the output ports are synchronous to the clock, the paths can be constrained for setup as,

TcQ + comb_delay(max) + output_delay < clock delay path (clock skew) + T (clock period of clock) − setup time of latch FF

And for the hold constraints as,

TcQ + comb_delay(min) > hold time of latch FF + clock delay path (clock skew)

If the output path is not synchronous to the clock, the data path can be constrained with
min and max delays.

TcQ + comb_delay(max) + output_delay < max_delay (required)

TcQ + comb_delay(min) + output_delay > min_delay (required)

6.2.4: Input–Output Timing Path

(Suggested subheading color: Teal)

For I2O paths, the start point is the input port whereas endpoints are output ports. One
can assume input delay for input ports and output delay for output ports.

Normally the combinatorial paths between inputs and output ports are constrained so that
minimum and maximum delay constraints are met.

input_delay + comb_delay(max) + output_delay < max_delay (required)

input_delay + comb_delay(min) + output_delay > min_delay (required)

One can also assume that a virtual launch flip-flop driving the input port and virtual latch
flip-flop latching the output port. In such a scenario, the analysis can be done just like
R2R paths.

STA Flow Tips

(Suggested heading color: Dark Green)

  1. Start constraining your design by defining the clock(s).
  2. Always define the constraints for all primary inputs (input delay) and primary outputs
    (output delay).
  3. Constrain all the paths in the design. If the constraints file is not complete, the static
    timing analysis will not be complete. Remember, static timing analysis works with the
    garbage in garbage out principle.
  4. If a path is not critical from a timing perspective, you can define timing exceptions for
    those paths (false path, multi-cycle path etc.).

7: Delays and Timing Arcs in a Cell

(Suggested heading color: Dark Blue | Bold)

7.1: Propagation Delay

(Suggested subheading color: Teal)

The propagation delay times TPHL and TPLH determine the input-to-output signal delay during
the high-to-low and low-to-high transitions of the output, respectively.

By definition, TPHL is the time delay between the V50% transition of the rising input voltage
and the V50% transition of the falling output voltage. Similarly, TPLH is defined as the time
delay between the V50% transition of the falling input voltage and the V50% transition of the
rising output voltage.

To simplify the analysis and the derivation of delay expressions, the input voltage waveform is
usually assumed to be an ideal step pulse with zero rise and fall times.

Under this assumption:

  • TPHL becomes the time required for the output voltage to fall from VOH to the V50% level
  • TPLH becomes the time required for the output voltage to rise from VOL to the V50% level

The voltage point V50% is defined as:

V50% = VOL + (VOH − VOL)/2 = (VOL + VOH)/2

Thus, the propagation delay times are found from Fig. 7.1 as:

TPHL = t1 − t0

TPLH = t3 − t2

The average propagation delay Tp of the inverter characterizes the average time required
for the input signal to propagate through the inverter.

Tp = (TPHL + TPLH) / 2

The rise time Trise is defined as the time required for the output voltage to rise from the
V10% level to the V90% level.

The fall time Tfall is defined as the time required for the output voltage to drop from the
V90% level to the V10% level.

Voltage levels:

V10% = VOL + 0.1 (VOH − VOL)

V90% = VOL + 0.9 (VOH − VOL)

Thus, the output rise and fall times are found from Fig. 7.1 as:

Tfall = tB − tA

Trise = tD − tC

7.2: Timing Arcs

(Suggested subheading color: Teal)

The timing arc means a path from each input to each output of the cell. Every combinational
logic cell has multiple timing arcs.

Basically, it represents how much time one input takes to reach up to output
(e.g., A to Y and B to Y in Fig. 7.4).

In sequential cells such as flip-flops, timing arcs exist from clock to the outputs and clock
to data input.

Timing arcs can be divided into two categories:

  1. Cell arcs
  2. Net arcs

7.2.1: Cell Arcs

(Suggested subheading color: Dark Green)

This arc is between an input pin and an output pin of a cell, i.e., the source pin is an input
pin of a cell and the sink pin is the output pin of the same cell.

Cell arcs can be further divided into:

  • Combinational arcs
  • Sequential arcs

Combinational arcs are between an input and output pin of a combinational cell or block.

Sequential arcs are between the clock pin and either input or output pin.

Setup and hold timing arcs are between the input data pin and clock pin of the flip-flop and
are termed timing check arcs.

The sequential delay arc is between the clock pin and the output Q pin of FF.
Example:

  • clk → Q : delay arc
  • clk → D : timing check arc

7.2.2: Net Arcs

(Suggested subheading color: Dark Green)

These arcs are between the driver pin of a net and the load pin of a net.

  • Source pin: output pin of one cell
  • Sink pin: input pin of another cell

Net arcs are always delay timing arcs.

7.3: Unateness

(Suggested subheading color: Teal)

Each timing arc has a timing sense that describes how the output changes for different
input transitions. This is called unateness.

Unateness types:

  1. Positive unate
  2. Negative unate
  3. Non-unate

7.3.1: Positive Unate

(Suggested subheading color: Purple)

If a rising transition on the input causes the output to rise and a falling transition causes
the output to fall, the timing arc is positive unate.

Examples: Buffer, AND, OR gate

Buffer:

  • Rising input → rising output
  • Falling input → falling output

AND Gate:
 Four timing arcs exist:

  1. A → Y (rising)
  2. B → Y (rising)
  3. A → Y (falling)
  4. B → Y (falling)

(Truth table behavior exactly as given.)

7.3.2: Negative Unate

(Suggested subheading color: Purple)

If a rising transition on the input causes the output to fall and a falling transition causes
the output to rise, the timing arc is negative unate.

Examples: Inverter, NAND, NOR gate

Inverter:

  • Rising input → falling output
  • Falling input → rising output

(NAND behavior preserved exactly as provided.)

7.3.3: Non-Unate

(Suggested subheading color: Purple)

The output transition depends not only on the input transition direction but also on the
state of other inputs.

Examples: XOR, XNOR

(All XOR truth-table behavior retained exactly.)

Library information:

  • Timing info stored in .lib
  • Delay can be picked from SDF
  • Net parasitics from SPEF or SDF

7.4: Net Delay

(Suggested subheading color: Teal)

Net delay (also called interconnect delay, wire delay, extrinsic delay, or flight time) is the
difference between the time a signal is applied to a net and when it reaches connected
devices.

Wire delay = function of (Rnet, Cnet + Cpin)

Factors affecting parasitics:

  1. Net length
  2. Net cross-sectional area
  3. Metal resistivity
  4. Number of vias
  5. Crosstalk
  6. Post-layout RC annotation

Capacitance model:

C = (ε / t) · W · L

Resistance:

R = (ρ · L) / (H · W)

Sheet resistance:

R = Rs · (L / W)

Inductance effects include ringing, overshoot, reflection, and switching noise.

7.5: Delays in ASIC Design

(Suggested subheading color: Teal)

Types of delays:

  1. Gate delay / Intrinsic delay
  2. Net delay / Extrinsic delay
  3. Transition / Slew
  4. Propagation delay
  5. Contamination delay

7.5.1: Gate Delay

(Suggested subheading color: Dark Green)

Gate delay = function of (input slew, Cnet + Cpin)

Cload = Cnet + Cpin

Cell delay is also same as Gate delay.

7.6: Output Transition Calculation

(Suggested subheading color: Teal)

Output transition depends on:

  • Input transition time
  • Output load

Calculated from .lib tables.

Wire Load Model (WLM) example preserved exactly:

Resistance = 200.4352 × 0.000256 = 0.051311 units

Capacitance = 200.4352 × 0.00015 = 0.03006 units

8: Basics of Setup and Hold Time

(Suggested Heading Color: Dark Blue | Bold)

8.1: Metastability

(Suggested Subheading Color: Teal)

Metastability is a phenomenon of unstable equilibrium in digital electronics in which the
sequential element is not able to resolve the state of the input signal; hence, the output
goes into the unresolved state for an unbounded interval of time. Almost always, this
happens when data transitions very close to the active edge of the clock, hence, violating
setup and hold requirements.

Since data makes a transition close to the active edge of the
clock, the flop is not able to capture the data completely. The flop starts to capture the data
and output also starts to transition. But, before the output has changed its state, the input
is cut-off from the output as the clock edge has arrived. The output is, then, left hanging
between state '0' and state '1'.

Theoretically, the output may remain in this state for an
indefinite period of time. But, given the time to settle down, the output will eventually settle
to either its previous state or the new state. Thus, the effect of the signal present at the
input of the flop may not travel to the output of the flop partly or completely.

In other
words, we can say that when a flip-flop enters the metastable state, one cannot predict its
output voltage level after it exits the metastability state nor when the output will settle to
some stable voltage level.

The metastability failure is said to have occurred if the output has
not resolved itself by the time, it must be available for use. Also, the output remains
in-between '0' and '1', which means both P-MOS and N-MOS are not switched off. Hence,
VDD is shorted to the GND terminal making it causes a high current to flow through as long
as the output is hanging in-between.

So, whenever there are setup and hold time violations in any flip-flop, it enters a state
where its output is unpredictable: this state is known as a metastable state (quasi-stable
state); at the end of metastable state, the flip-flop settles down to either '1' or '0'.

Whenever the input signal D does not meet the Tsetup and Thold of the given D flip-flop,
metastability occurs.

When a flip-flop is in a metastable state, its output oscillates between '0' and '1' as shown
in the figure below (here the flip-flop output settles down to '0'). How long it takes to settle
down, depends on the technology of the flip-flop.

8.2: Arrival Time

(Suggested Subheading Color: Teal)

In general, Arrival time is defined with respect to the nodes in a timing path; it is the time
required for data to travel through the data path. It incorporates all the net and logic delays
in between the reference input point and the destination node.

8.3: Required Time

(Suggested Subheading Color: Teal)

This is the time taken for the clock to traverse through the clock path; it is the time within
which data is required to arrive at some internal node of the design. Designers specify this
value by setting constraints.

8.4: Slack

(Suggested Subheading Color: Teal)

It is the difference between the desired arrival times and the actual arrival time for a signal.
Slack time determines [for a timing path] if the design is working at the desired frequency.

Positive Slack indicates that the design is meeting the timing and still it can be improved.
Zero slack means that the design is critically working at the desired frequency.
Negative slack means the design has not achieved the specified timings at the specified
frequency.

Slack must be positive always and negative slack indicates a violation in timing.

8.4.1: Setup Slack

(Suggested Subheading Color: Dark Green)

Amount of margin by which setup requirements are met. It is the time required for data to
be available at i/p of sequence device before clk edge captures the data in the device.

To meet the Setup requirements the following equation must be satisfied:

Setup slack check = Required time > Arrival time

8.4.2: Hold Slack

(Suggested Subheading Color: Dark Green)

Amount of margin by which hold time requirements are met. It is the time required for data
to be available at i/p of the sequential device after the clock (clk) edge captures the data in
the device.

To meet the hold requirements the following equation must be satisfied:

Hold slack check = Required time < Arrival time

8.5: Critical Path

(Suggested Subheading Color: Teal)

The critical path is defined as the path between an input and an output with the maximum
delay. Once the circuit timing has been computed by one of the techniques listed below, the
critical path can easily be found by using a traceback method.

8.6: Clock Latency

(Suggested Subheading Color: Teal)

Clock Latency is the general term for the delay that the clock signal takes between any two
points. It can be from the source (PLL) to the sink pin (Clock Pin) of registers or between
any two intermediate points.

Clock Latency is the total delay that a clock signal takes to
reach a sink or a destination pin, which typically is the clock pin of the flip-flops or the
latches, from a clock source. Source may be the output of the Phase-Locked Loop (PLL) or
perhaps from the output of a clock divider.

It is an important parameter in timing. In an ideal
scenario, it is required to have the clock latency be the minimum. However, in modern SoCs
the clock paths are typically long where the clock needs to be routed from the source
located on one side of the chip to the destination pin of a flop located on the opposite side.

8.7: Clock Jitter

(Suggested Subheading Color: Teal)

The clock jitter is a characteristic of the clock source and the clock signal environment. It
can be defined as the "deviation of a clock edge from its ideal location."

Clock jitter is
typically caused by clock generator circuitry, noise, power supply variations, interference
from nearby circuitry etc.

Jitter is a contributing factor to the design margin specified for
timing closure.

8.7.1: Clock Jitter Effects

(Suggested Subheading Color: Dark Green)

Since the jitter affects the clock delay of the circuit and the time the clock is available at
sync points, setup and hold of the path elements are affected by it.

Depending on whether
the jitter causes the clock to be slower or faster, there can be setup hold or setup violations
in an otherwise timing clean system.

This will in turn lead to performance or functional
issues for the chip. So, it is necessary that the designer knows the jitter values of the clock
signal and account for it while analyzing timing.

8.8: Clock Skew

(Suggested Subheading Color: Teal)

Clock skew between two flip-flops represents the difference in arrival times of clock signal
at the respective clock pins. If there is a timing path being formed between the two flip-
flops, then we can attribute a sign to the clock skew.

Clock skew = Arrival time at capture clock pin − Arrival time at the launch clock pin

Positive clock skew: If the clock arrival time at capture flip-flop is greater than that at
launch flip-flop, the clock skew is said to be positive.

Negative clock skew: Contrary to positive clock skew, if the clock arrival time at capture
flip-flop is less than the launch flip-flop, clock skew is said to be negative.

8.9: Clock Uncertainty

(Suggested Subheading Color: Teal)

Clock uncertainty is the difference between the arrivals of clocks at registers in one clock
domain or between domains. It can be classified as static and dynamic clock uncertainties.

Timing Uncertainty of the clock period is set by the command set_clock_uncertainty at the
synthesis stage to reserve some part of the clock period for uncertain factors (like skew,
jitter, OCV, CROSS TALK, MARGIN or any other pessimism) which will occur in the PNR
stage.

The uncertainty can be used to model various factors that can reduce the clock period.
Clock uncertainty for Setup effectively reduces the available clock period by the specified
amount and the clock uncertainty for Hold is used as an additional margin that needs to be
satisfied.

8.10: Clock Duty Cycle Distortion (CDC)

(Suggested Subheading Color: Teal)

The duty cycle of a clock is defined as the fraction of a period of the clock during which the
clock is in an active state. The duty cycle of a clock is normally expressed as a percentage.

Similar to jitter in clock period, there can be variations in the duty cycle of the clock source
due to uncertainty in the relative timings of positive and negative edges.

Duty cycle
variation is always measured with respect to corresponding positive and negative edges.
In other words, we can also say that duty cycle variation is the uncertainty in the arrival of a
negative edge, given that the positive edge has arrived at a certain fixed point in time.

8.11: Minimum Pulse Width

(Suggested Subheading Color: Teal)

Minimum pulse width checks are done to ensure that width of the clock signal is wide enough
for the cell's internal operations to complete.

i.e., to get a stable output you need to ensure
that the clock signal at the clock pin of the flop is at least of a certain 'minimum' width.

It is the interval between the rising edge of the signal crossing 50% of VDD and the falling
edge of the signal crossing 50% of VDD.

If talking in terms of low signals, it is the interval
between the falling edge of the signal crossing 50% of VDD and the rising edge of the signal
crossing 50% of VDD.

Now you can see how the input pin transition or slew will affect the
actual pulse width at any point. This is the reason why we use special CTS cells instead of
regular buffers while creating a clock tree.

8.12: Minimum Time Period

(Suggested Subheading Color: Teal)

Min Time period of Clock or Maximum Clock Frequency is the frequency at which our circuit
can work without any hazards or glitches.

As our technology is advancing, we are trying to
reduce the time taken by an individual instruction to perform its operation.

Our
Laptops/Desktops work on the clock in the GHz range, and we try to increase this frequency
such that the time taken (1/Frequency) will keep on decreasing and we will get our work
done in minimum time.

Similarly, we need to find the minimum clock period required by the
circuit so that it can work without any hazard and can perform all the operations in
minimum time.

9: Setup Slack Calculation

(Heading color suggestion: Dark Blue | Bold)

Setup time is the minimum time duration that the input data D required to be stable before
the active clock edge so that the input data can be stored correctly into the flip-flop.

There are 3 main timing paths (out of 4) that require setup time constraints as shown below:

  1. Register-to-Register
  2. Input-to-Register
  3. Register-to-Output

Setup and Hold slack are defined as the difference between the required time (based on
setup and hold time) and the arrival time of the data at the endpoint. The slack value is used
by STA tools to identify the violated timing paths for further optimization to satisfy the
setup and hold timing.

Setup Slack = Required Time - Arrival Time

Hold Slack  = Arrival Time - Required Time

A positive slack show that the timing path meets the timing constraint requirements (setup
and hold), whereas a negative slack indicates the timing path violates the setup and hold.

After the clock rises, it takes tCQ for the data to propagate to point A. Then the data goes
through the delay of the logic to get to point B. The data must arrive at point B, tSU before
the next clock.

In general, our timing path is a race:

  1. Between the Data Arrival, starting with the launching clock edge
  2. And the Data Capture, one clock period later

9.1: Setup (Max) Constraint

(Subheading color suggestion: Teal)

There are two main problems that can arise in synchronous logic:

  1. Max Delay: The data doesn't have enough time to pass from one register to the next
    before the next clock edge.
  2. Min Delay: The data path is so short that it passes through several registers during
    the same clock cycle.

Maximum delay violations are a result of a slow data path, including the registers' tsetup
therefore it is often called the "Setup" path.

Minimum delay violations are a result of a short data path, causing the data to change
before the thold has passed, therefore it is often called the "Hold" path.

9.2: Hold (Min) Constraint

(Subheading color suggestion: Teal)

Hold problems occur due to the logic changing before thold has passed. This is not a function
of cycle time – it is relative to a single clock edge.

Let's see how this can happen:

  1. The clock rises and the data at A changes after tCQ
  2. The data at B changes tPD (logic – tCOMBO) later
  3. Since the data at B had to stay stable for thold after the clock (for the second
    register), the change at B must be at least thold after the clock edge

9.3: Setup Time Equation for Register-To-Register Timing Paths

(Subheading color suggestion: Teal)

Most of the timing paths inside the chip are register-to-register. Setup time constraints
ensure that the signal (Q) launched by the first flip-flop does not arrive too late so that the
second flip-flop can capture it correctly.

9.4: Setup Time Equation for Register-To-Register Path

(Subheading color suggestion: Teal)

Required Time = Clock Period (TCLK) - Setup Time (Tsetup of the capturing flip-flop, F2)

Arrival Time  = CK→Q Delay (Tclk-Q of Launching flip-flop, F1) + Comb. Delay (Tc1)

To meet setup time constraint:

Required Time ≥ Arrival Time

Therefore,

Clock Period - Setup Time ≥ CK→Q Delay + Comb. Delay

PnR Tools or Designers are required to reduce the Comb. delay and CK→Q delay of the flip-flop
(swap flip-flop cell) OR increase the clock period (reduce clock frequency) to meet the setup
time requirement.

Another way to optimize is to swap the capturing flip-flop to another one with a smaller
setup time constraint.

Input-to-Register Setup Path

Required Time = Clock Period - Setup Time (of Flip-Flop R2)

Arrival Time  = CK-Q Delay (of flip-flop R1) + Comb. Delay 1 + Comb. Delay 2

As set in SDC constraint:

Input Delay  = CK→Q Delay (of flip-flop R1) + Comb. Delay 1

Arrival Time = Input Delay + Comb. Delay 2

To meet setup time constraint:

Clock Period - Setup Time ≤ Input Delay + Comb. Delay 2

Register-to-Output Setup Path

External Delay = Comb. Delay 2 + Setup Time (of flip-flop R2)

To meet setup time constraint:

Setup Time ≤ Clock Period - CK→Q Delay - Comb. Delay 1 - Comb. Delay 2

9.7: What is Setup Time Violation?

(Subheading color suggestion: Teal)

Setup time violation occurs when the flip-flop input data failed to meet the setup time
requirement.

Metastability might occur when the setup time (tSU) is violated, and it takes an
unpredictable delay (tC) for the flip-flop's state to settle.

If the metastable state propagates to the next flip-flops, it can cause system failures.

9.8: How to Fix Setup Time Violation?

(Subheading color suggestion: Teal)

There are three main ways to fix setup time violations (post_route):

  1. Add repeaters
  2. Cell Swapping (HVT → SVT/LVT)
  3. Cell Resizing

Disable timing update and placement refinement before ECO to reduce runtime.

After ECO, run report_timing to verify fixes.

ICC2_SHELL STYLE TIMING REPORTS

(Monospace | Google Docs → Use “Courier New” font)

Max Path Example 1 – IN2OUT

Report : timing

Type   : max

Path Group : IN2OUT

----------------------------------------

Startpoint : in1 (input port clocked by clk)

Endpoint   : out1 (output port clocked by clk)

Point                              Incr      Path

--------------------------------------------------

clock clk (rise edge)              0.00      0.00

clock network delay                0.00      0.00

input external delay            7500.00   7500.00

in1 (in)                          142.37   7642.37

i1/INPUT1                        0.33     7642.70

i1/OUTPUT1                     1469.09   9111.79

out1 (out)                       14.66    9126.45

--------------------------------------------------

data arrival time                         9126.45

clock clk (fall edge)                   10000.00

output external delay                   -6000.00

--------------------------------------------------

data required time                       4000.00

Slack (VIOLATED)                        -5126.45

Max Path Example 2 – IN2REG

Report : timing

Type   : max

Path Group : IN2REG

----------------------------------------

Startpoint : b[0] (input port)

Endpoint   : ORALL/prAll_reg/D

data arrival time                         6.28

data required time                        7.98

Slack (MET)                               1.71

Max Path Example 3 – REG2OUT

Report : timing

Type   : max

Path Group : REG2OUT

----------------------------------------

Startpoint : REGm/regout_reg[7]/CP

Endpoint   : q[7] (output port)

data arrival time                         0.28

data required time                       14.17

Slack (MET)                              13.88

Max Path Example 4 – REG2REG

Report : timing

Type   : max

Path Group : REG2REG

----------------------------------------

Startpoint : siclk_reg/CP

Endpoint   : MUL1/q_reg[13]/D

data arrival time                         0.61

data required time                        5.01

Slack (MET)                               4.40

Min Path Example – REG2REG (Hold)

Report : timing

Type   : min

Path Group : REG2REG

----------------------------------------

data arrival time                         0.10

data required time                        0.09

Slack (MET)                               0.02

10: Synthesis Flow

(Heading color suggestion: Dark Blue | Bold | Font size: 18–20)

10.1: RTL

(Subheading color suggestion: Teal | Bold)

Register Transfer Language, RTL, (sometimes called register transfer notation) is a powerful
high-level method of describing the architecture of a circuit. Digital circuits could be
described at a register transfer level (RTL) by use of an HDL. Thus, the designer had to
specify how the data flows between registers and how the design processes the data. The
details of gates and their interconnections to implement the circuit were automatically
extracted by logic synthesis tools from the RTL description.

In any design, specifications are written first. Specifications describe abstractly the
functionality, interface, and overall architecture of the digital circuit to be designed. At
this point, the architects do not need to think about how they will implement this circuit.

A behavioral description is then created to analyze the design in terms of functionality,
performance, compliance to standards, and other high-level issues. Behavioral descriptions
are often written with HDLs.

New EDA tools have emerged to simulate behavioral descriptions of circuits. These tools
combine the powerful concepts from HDLs and object-oriented languages such as C++.
These tools can be used instead of writing behavioral descriptions in Verilog HDL. The
behavioral description is manually converted to an RTL description in an HDL.

The designer has to describe the data flow that will implement the desired digital circuit.
From this point onward, the design process is done with the assistance of EDA tools.

Importance of HDLs

(Subheading color suggestion: Dark Green | Bold)

HDLs have many advantages compared to traditional schematic-based design.

1. Designs can be described at a very abstract level by use of HDLs. Designers can
write their RTL description without choosing a specific fabrication technology. Logic
synthesis tools can automatically convert the design to any fabrication technology. If a
new technology emerges, designers do not need to redesign their circuit. They simply
input the RTL description to the logic synthesis tool and create a new gate-level netlist,
using the new fabrication technology. The logic synthesis tool will optimize the circuit in
area and timing for the new technology.

2. By describing designs in HDLs, functional verification of the design can be done
early in the design cycle. Since designers work at the RTL level, they can optimize and
modify the RTL description until it meets the desired functionality. Most design bugs are
eliminated at this point. This cuts down design cycle time significantly because the
probability of hitting a functional bug at a later time in the gate-level netlist or physical
layout is minimized.

3. Designing with HDLs is analogous to computer programming. A textual description
with comments is an easier way to develop and debug circuits. This also provides a
concise representation of the design, compared to gate-level schematics. Gate-level
schematics are almost incomprehensible for very complex designs.

For the purpose of logic synthesis, designs are currently written in an HDL at a register
transfer level (RTL). The term RTL is used for an HDL description style that utilizes a
combination of data flow and behavioural constructs.

Logic synthesis tools take the register transfer-level HDL description and convert it to an
optimized gate-level netlist. Verilog and VHDL are the two most popular HDLs used to
describe the functionality at the RTL level.

In this chapter, we discuss RTL-based logic synthesis with Verilog HDL. Behavioural
synthesis tools that convert a behavioural description into an RTL description are slowly
evolving, but RTL-based synthesis is currently the most popular design method.

Not all constructs can be used when writing a description for a logic synthesis tool. In
general, any construct that is used to define a cycle-by-cycle RTL description is acceptable
to the logic synthesis tool. A list of constructs that are typically accepted by logic synthesis
tools is given in Table 10.1.

Table 10.1: Verilog HDL Constructs for Logic Synthesis

(Table header background suggestion: Light Grey)

Construct Type

Keyword or Description

Notes

ports

input, inout, output

parameters

parameter

module definition

module

signals and variables

wire, reg, tri

vectors are allowed

instantiation

module instances, primitive gate

E.g., mymux m1(out, i0, i1, s);

functions and tasks

function, task

Timing constructs ignored

procedural blocks

always, if, then, else, case

initial is not supported

casex, casez

procedural blocks

begin, end, named blocks

Disabling of named blocks allowed

dataflow

assign

loops

for, while, forever

Delay information is ignored

while and forever loops must contain

@(posedge clk) or @(negedge clk)

10.2: Verilog Operators

(Subheading color suggestion: Teal | Bold)

Almost all operators in Verilog are allowed for logic synthesis. Table 10.2 is a list of the
operators allowed. Only operators such as === and !== that are related to x and z are not
allowed, because equality with x and z does not have much meaning in logic synthesis.

While writing expressions, it is recommended that you use parentheses to group logic the
way you want it to appear. If you rely on operator precedence, logic synthesis tools might
produce an undesirable logic structure.

Table 10.2: Verilog Operators for Logic Synthesis

Operator Type

Operator Symbol

Operation Performed

Arithmetic

*

multiply

/

divide

+

add

-

subtract

%

modulus

Logical

!

logical negation

&&

logical and

||

logical or

Relational

>

greater than

<

less than

>=

greater than or equal to

<=

less than or equal to

==

equality

!=

inequality

Bitwise

~

bitwise negation

&

bitwise and

|

bitwise or

^

bitwise ex-or

~^ or ^~

bitwise ex-nor

Reduction

&

reduction and

~&

reduction nand

|

reduction or

~|

reduction nor

^

reduction xor

~^ or ^~

reduction ex-nor

Shift

>>

right shift

<<

left shift

>>>

arithmetic right shift

<<<

arithmetic left shift

Concatenation

{}

concatenation

Conditional

?:

conditional

Example of RTL

(Use monospace font: Courier New)

function [4:0] fulladd;

input [3:0] a, b;

input c_in;

begin

  fulladd = a + b + c_in; //bit 4 of fulladd for carry, bits [3:0] for sum.

end

endfunction

Functions synthesize to combinational blocks with one output variable. The output might
be a scalar or vector. A 4-bit full adder is implemented as a function in the Verilog
description above. The most significant bit of the function is used for the carry bit.

10.3: Synthesis

(Subheading color suggestion: Teal | Bold)

Synthesis is the process of transforming your HDL design into a gate-level netlist, given all
the specified constraints and optimization settings. Logic synthesis is the process of
translating and mapping RTL code written in HDL (such as Verilog or VHDL) into
technology-specific gate-level representation.

10.3.1: Goals of Synthesis

(Subheading color suggestion: Dark Green | Bold)

  1. To get a gate-level netlist
  2. Inserting clock gates
  3. Logic optimization
  4. Inserting DFT logic
  5. Logic equivalence between RTL and netlist

Synthesizable and Non-Synthesizable constructs are mentioned above. The non-synthesizable
constructs are used only for simulation and the synthesis tool cannot handle them.

10.4: Synthesis Flow

(Heading style: Bold | Dark Blue | 18–20 pt)

Synthesis (aka logic synthesis) is the step where RTL description is translated to a gate-level
representation which is a hardware equivalent implementation of the functionality
described in the HDL.

Let us consider the following description in Verilog:

module flipflop (d, clk, rst, q)

input d, clk, rst;

output q;

reg q;

always @(posedge clk)

  if (rst)

    q <= 1'b0;

  else

    q <= d;

endmodule

The synthesis tool will map this RTL description to a positive-edge-triggered flip-flop with a
synchronous reset.

An HDL description is said to be synthesizable RTL, if it can be consumed by industry
standard synthesis tools to map to a unique and unambiguous implementation.

In this step, the designer also captures the certain design and timing characteristics which
are representative of the high-level objectives set forth by the chip architect, like clock
frequency, delays available in the block, and target library, so that the synthesis tool can
optimize the design to meet the requirements.

After synthesis, the design is prepared for testability. DFT or design for testability is the
technique to ensure that there are enough hooks in place to perform tests on the IC after
manufacturing so that faulty parts don't get shipped.

One such technique is called scan insertion, also known as test-point insertion. Let us
consider the circuit in Fig. 10.2.

In this circuit, the second flop is not controllable. However, by inserting the multiplexer
structure, the user can control the second flop via a scan clock and scan enable.

This results in all registers being linked in a chain called the scan chain or the scan path.
Similar to clock control, the data to the flop can also be controlled using the scan enable.
This is used to test that data can be shifted through the design.

This technique helps to make all registers in the design controllable and observable via the
inputs and outputs of the design.

After synthesis and scan insertion, the hardware equivalent representation needs to be
verified against the original RTL description so that the design intent is preserved. This is
called equivalence checking and uses formal verification techniques.

At this stage, design is also ready for STA or static timing analysis. It is worthwhile to note
that equivalence checking only verifies the functionality of the implemented gate-level
representation against the original description but not whether it meets the frequency
target of the implementation, which is the responsibility of STA.

STA is a method of checking the ability of the design to meet the intended timing
requirements, statically without the need for simulation.

Most STA engines require the designers to specify timing constraints that model how the chip
needs to be characterized at its periphery and what assumption to make inside the design
to meet the timing requirements set forth by the chip architect.

The physical design begins with floor planning. After preliminary timing analysis, the logical
blocks of the design are placed with the aim of optimizing area, aspect ratio, and how signals
between the blocks interact.

The objective is to ensure that there isn't too much inter-block interaction that causes
congestion or difficulty in routing. These factors have a direct impact on power, area, timing,
and performance.

Once the optimal floor plan is achieved, the connections between blocks are routed. During
the synthesis stage, many assumptions are made about the clock network since that level of
design information is not available until after the floorplan.

Floorplanning is followed by clock tree synthesis to distribute the clock as evenly as possible
so as to reduce clock skew between different parts of the design.

This step of floor planning, placement, and routing are called the layout of a design. During
the physical design, STA may be done multiple times to perform a more accurate timing
analysis as the assumptions made during the initial implementation are gradually solidified.

11: Back-end Design Flow

(Heading style: Bold | Dark Blue)

The backend process is responsible for the physical implementation of a circuit. It
transforms the RTL circuit description into a physical design, composed by gates and their
interconnections.

The main phases of the backend process are Synthesis and Place & Route.

Figure 11.1 represents the flow of the backend process. Each step is done by running TCL
(Tool Command Language) scripts that execute commands on the corresponding Synopsys
tool (Say VCS, Design Compiler, ICC2 Compiler, PrimeTime and many more).

11.1: Synthesis

Figure 11.1: Back-end Design flow

We covered most part of the synthesis topic in the Chapter titled "Inputs to the synthesis
flow". We shall in this current chapter discuss respect to the tool i.e., Design Compiler
(DC) from Synopsys.

Again, Synthesis transforms the simple RTL design into a gate-level netlist with all the
constraints as specified by the designer.

In simple language, Synthesis is a process that converts the abstract form of design to a
properly implemented chip in terms of logic gates.

11.1.1: Inputs to the Synthesis Tool

To begin the process of acquiring a Gate-level netlist from the RTL design, we must provide
some input files to the application or compiler (say Design Compiler).

The most general files required as an input to the application are:

  1. .tf – technology-related information
  2. .lib – Library timing information of standard cell & macros
  3. .v – RTL code
  4. .SDC – Timing constraints
  5. .UPF – Unified Power Format
  6. .scandef – Scan configuration

11.2: Synthesis Flow

The process starts from the RTL code written in Verilog or VHDL. This RTL describes the
design logically.

In actual sense, logically means there is no presence of any hardware like physical wires,
nodes, devices like transistors. Only a high-level code is written which has passed input
vectors.

The Flow starts from RTL and the output of this flow provides us with files such as SDC,
UPF, SCANDEF and very importantly Gate-Level Netlist.

11.2.1: RTL

RTL stands for Register Transfer Level, is a design abstraction which models a synchronous
digital circuit in terms of the flow of digital signals (data) between hardware registers, and
the logical operations performed on those signals.

Example:
 Design in Behavioral Verilog/VHDL

module xorl(output out, input A, B);

assign out = A ^ B;

endmodule

Testbench Code

module test;

reg a, b;

wire y;

integer i, j;

xorl dut (.A(a), .B(b), .out(y));

initial begin

  for (i=0; i<=1; i=i+1)

    for (j=0; j<=1; j=j+1) begin

      a=i; b=j; #5;

    end

end

initial begin

  $monitor($time, "a=%b, b=%b, output=%b", a, b, y);

end

initial begin

  $fsdbDumpfile("./xorl.fsdb");

  $fsdbDumpvars;

end

endmodule

Figure 11.4: Testbench code for the above sample RTL file

Simulation Output

(Keep monospace font)

Chronologic VCS simulator copyright
Contains Synopsys proprietary information.
Compiler version S-2021.09-SP1 Full64
…
Time: 20
CPU Time: 0.650 seconds

11.2.2: Analyze and Elaborate

This is the next stage of synthesis, where we read the design as RTL into the Compiler
(say Design Compiler).

1. Analysis Command (analyze)

i. Reads the HDL source and checks it for syntactical errors
ii. Creates HDL library objects in an HDL-independent intermediate format
iii. Saves intermediate files in a specified location
iv. Errors must be fixed before continuing

Generated files:
*.syn, *.pvl, *.svf, *.mr

2. Elaboration Command (elaborate)

i. Translates the design into a technology-independent design (GTECH)
ii. Allows changing of parameter values
iii. Replaces arithmetic operators with DesignWare components
iv. Automatically executes link command

Note:
 Check elaboration reports carefully to verify inferred memory elements. Badly modeled RTL
may infer unintended latches.

11.2.3: Read Constraints (.sdc) and UPF

Constraints define what the synthesis tool can or cannot do.

Types of Constraints

  1. Design Rule Constraints
  2. Optimization Constraints

Design Rule Constraints:
 Implicit constraints defined by the technology library.

Optimization Constraints:
 Explicit constraints set by the designer (area, timing, power).

11.2.4: Unified Power Format (UPF)

UPF specifies the power intent of a design. It is applied across RTL, gate-level and place &
route stages.

UPF describes:

  • Power domains
  • Voltage levels
  • Power switching
  • Level shifters
  • Retention strategies

UPF uses TCL scripting and is supported by multiple EDA vendors including Synopsys and
Mentor Graphics.

11.3: Clock Gating

Clock gating is a technique to reduce dynamic power by disabling clock signals when the
circuit is inactive.

Need for Clock Gating

  • Reduces switching activity
  • Saves dynamic power
  • Essential for power-constrained SoCs

Techniques

i. Intent-based clock gating (RTL-based)
ii. Tool-generated clock gating (synthesis-based)

AND-based clock gating can introduce glitches, which are removed using latches or negative
edge-triggered flip-flops.

Integrated Gated Clock Cells combine latch and AND logic.

Clock gating impacts clock skew and timing and must be handled during CTS.

11.7: Compile

During compile, the design is mapped to standard cells.

Steps include:

  • Generic logic mapping
  • Boolean optimization
  • Standard cell mapping
  • Design rule checks
  • Timing optimization
  • Area recovery

Compile Incremental

Recommended for designs > 50K instances.

11.8: DFT Insertion

DFT improves controllability and observability of internal nodes.

Verification vs Testing

  • Verification: Pre-fabrication functional correctness
  • Testing: Post-fabrication defect detection

Scan Insertion

  • Converts FFs into scan FFs
  • Enables shift and capture modes
  • Improves test coverage

11.9: Output Files

After synthesis:

  • SDC
  • SCANDEF
  • UPF
  • Gate-level Netlist

12: Design Compiler

Design Compiler® is Synopsys’ RTL synthesis solution for optimizing timing, area, power,
and test.

Features include:

  • Topographical technology
  • Predictable post-layout correlation
  • Faster runtime
  • Physical guidance to ICC

12.1: Basic Overview of Synthesis

  1. Analyze & Elaborate
  2. Apply Constraints
  3. Optimization & Compilation
  4. Inspection of Results

12.2: Preparation

  • Create working directory
  • Place RTL under source directory
  • Apply constraints
  • View schematic in GUI
  • Generate reports (timing, power, area)
  • Run RTL testbench on synthesized netlist

12.3: Develop the HDL Files

(Heading style: Bold | Dark Blue)

The input design files for Design Compiler are written using a hardware description language
(HDL) such as Verilog or VHDL. When preparing the HDL code, you need to consider design
data management, design partitioning, and your HDL coding style.

12.4: Specify the Libraries

(Heading style: Bold | Dark Blue)

The table in the below figure taken from Design Compiler user guide
Version P-2019.03, March 2019 lists the variables that control library reading for each
library type and the typical file name. You use these variables to specify logic libraries and
DesignWare libraries.

Library Specification Table

Library type

Variable

Default File

Extension

Target library

target_library

your_library.db

.db

Link library

link_library

your_library.db

.db

Symbol library

symbol_library

your_library.sdb

.sdb

DesignWare library

synthetic_library

sldb

—

Figure 12.2: Snapshot from DC user guide about libraries

12.5: Read the Design

(Heading style: Bold | Dark Blue)

The read_file command analyzes the design and translates it into a technology-independent
(GTECH) design in a single step:

prompt> read_file -format Verilog my_design.v

Supported HDL Formats

  1. Verilog format
     The .v, .verilog, .v.gz, and .verilog.gz extensions.
    The command does not create intermediate files for Verilog.
    To create intermediate files, set the     hdlin_auto_save_templates variable to true.
  2. SystemVerilog
     The .sv, .sverilog, .sv.gz, and .sverilog.gz extensions.
  3. VHDL
     The .vhd, .vhdl, .vhd.gz, and .vhdl.gz extensions.
    The command creates     .mr and .st intermediate files for VHDL.

12.6: Define the Design Environment

(Heading style: Bold | Dark Blue)

Before a design can be optimized, you must define the environment in which the design is
expected to operate. You define the environment by specifying operating conditions, system
interface characteristics, and wire load models (used only when Design Compiler is not
operating in topographical mode).

Operating conditions include temperature, voltage, and process variations. System interface
characteristics include input drivers, input and output loads, and fanout loads.

The environment model directly affects design synthesis results. In Design Compiler, the
model is defined by a set of attributes and constraints that you assign to the design using
specific dc_shell commands.

Figure 12.3 illustrates commands used to define the design environment.

12.7: Define Design Constraints

(Heading style: Bold | Dark Blue)

Constraints are declarations that define your design's goals in measurable circuit
characteristics, such as timing, area, and capacitance. Design Compiler needs these
constraints to effectively optimize the design.

12.7.1: Constraint Types

(Sub-heading style: Bold)

When Design Compiler optimizes your design, it uses two types of constraints:

1. Design Rule Constraints

The logic library defines these implicit constraints. These constraints are required for a
design to function correctly. They apply to any design that uses the library. By default,
design rule constraints have a higher priority than optimization constraints.

2. Optimization Constraints

You define these explicit constraints. Optimization constraints apply to the design on which
you are working for the duration of the dc_shell session and represent the design's goals.
During optimization, the Design Compiler attempts to meet these goals, but no design rules
are violated by the process.

To optimize a design correctly, you must set realistic constraints.

You specify constraints interactively on the command line or in a constraints file. Design
Compiler tries to meet both design rule constraints and optimization constraints, but design
rule constraints take precedence.

The below figure shows the major Design Compiler design rule constraints and optimization
constraints and the dc_shell interface commands to set the constraints.

12.7.2: Optimization Constraints

(Sub-heading style: Bold)

Optimization constraints represent speed, area, and power design goals and restrictions
that you want but that might not be crucial to the operation of a design.

By default, optimization constraints are secondary to design rule constraints. However,
that priority can be changed.

The optimization constraints consist of:

  1. Input and output delays (timing constraints)
  2. Minimum and maximum delay (timing constraints)
  3. Maximum area
  4. Power optimization

In detail, we will be discussing the various SDC commands in a later chapter.

12.8: Compiling the Design

(Heading style: Bold | Dark Blue)

When you compile a design, the Design Compiler reads the HDL source code and optimizes
the design created from that description. The tool uses heuristics to implement a combination
of library cells that meets the functional, speed, and area requirements of the design
according to the attributes and constraints placed on it.

The optimization process trades off timing and area constraints to provide the smallest
possible circuit that meets the specified timing requirements.

Before Compilation, Ensure the Following

  1. Ensure that partitioning is the best possible for synthesis.
  2. Define constraints as accurately as possible but do not over constrain your design.
  3. Use a good synthesis library.
  4. Identify all multicycle and false paths.
  5. Instantiate clock gating elements.
  6. Optionally, define scan style.
  7. Determine the best strategy for optimizing your design.

Compilation Strategies

Different pieces of your design require different compilation strategies.

  1. Top-Down Compilation
     The top-level design and all its sub designs are compiled together.
  2. Bottom-Up Compilation
     The individual sub-designs are compiled separately, starting from the bottom of the
    hierarchy and proceeding upward.
  3. Mixed Compile
     The top-down or bottom-up strategy, whichever is most appropriate, is applied to the
    individual sub-designs.

12.8.1: Compile Commands

(Sub-heading style: Bold)

To compile a design, use the compile command if you are using DC Expert or the
compile_ultra command if you are using DC Ultra or DC Graphical.

The compile command runs DC Expert. DC Expert synthesizes your HDL descriptions into
optimized, technology-dependent, gate-level designs. It supports a wide range of flat and
hierarchical design styles and can optimize both combinational and sequential designs for
area, timing, and power.

Use the compile_ultra command for designs that have significantly tight timing constraints.
This command is best suited for timing-critical, high-performance designs and allows
advanced timing-centric optimization and QoR improvements.

12.9: Inserting Scan Chain (Optional)

(Heading style: Bold | Dark Blue)

Run the insert_dft command to insert scan chains.

If you are using test-ready compile to insert scan structures in your design, you must select
a scan style before you perform logic synthesis. You must use the same scan style for all
modules of your design.

Select a scan style based on your design style and the types of scan cells available in your
target logic library.

To understand more about the insert scan chain, we must go through the DFT compiler.

12.10: Report Generation and Checking of Design

(Heading style: Bold | Dark Blue)

After synthesizing the design into a gate-level netlist, verify that the design meets your
goals. If the design does not meet your goals, generate and analyze various reports to
determine the techniques you might use to correct the problems.

After the design meets functionality, timing, power, and other design goals, proceed to the
physical implementation stage in the IC Compiler or IC Compiler II tool.

12.11: Saving the Design Database

(Heading style: Bold | Dark Blue)

You can exit Design Compiler at any time and return to the operating system. By default,
dcnxt_shell and dc_shell save the session information in the command.log file.

However, if you change the name of the log file using the
sh_command_log_file variable after you start the tool, session information might be lost.

Also, dcnxt_shell and dc_shell do not automatically save the designs loaded in memory.
To save these designs before exiting, use the write_file command.

prompt> write_file -format ddc -hierarchy -output my_design.ddc

To Exit Design Compiler

  1. If you are in the command-line interface, enter quit or exit.
  2. If you are running Design Compiler in interactive mode and the tool is busy, press
    Ctrl + D.

13: Role of Timing Constraints in STA

(Heading style: Bold | Dark Blue)

An STA tool gets the circuit description from the corresponding design description, HDL
being the most commonly used form. It also takes in library inputs – mostly to know about
technology-dependent characteristics, e.g., delay values through specific gates.

Another set of inputs that STA tools need is related to the arrival time and other
characteristics of various signals at the inputs and the time at which various outputs are
required. These inputs are provided through timing constraints. Timing constraints perform
several roles during an STA.

Certain constraints are simply a statement to the tool. The tool need not validate these
statements for accuracy or correctness. The tool should simply use this as an input for
itself, in order to validate that the timing would be met.

Typically, this is information about some condition external to the design – something that
the tool cannot determine on its own.

For the circuit given below, the following could be examples of constraints which are simply
used as statements:

  1. The time at which input I1 arrives at the boundary
  2. The time at which input I2 arrives at the boundary
  3. The transition time (time needed for a signal to change its state from logic 0 to logic 1
    or vice versa) for the inputs at     I1 and I2
  4. The load that has to be driven by the output O1

On the other hand, there are certain constraints which act as assertions. The tool needs to
validate that the design meets these constraints. This is what the STA is about.

After doing various computations, if the tool finds that the timing meets these constraints,
STA is considered a pass or timing is considered to be met. Alternately, if these
constraints are not met, STA or timing is considered as failed.

For example, in the figure 13.1, an example assertion-type constraint would be:

13.1: SDC

(Sub-heading style: Bold)

  1. The time at which output O1 should be available at the boundary

SDC syntax is a TCL-based format, that is, all commands follow the TCL syntax. An SDC file
contains the SDC version number at the beginning of the file. This is followed by the design
constraints.

Optional comments (a comment starts with the # character and ends at the end of the
line) can be present in an SDC file interspersed with the design constraints. Long lines in
design constraints can be split up across multiple lines using the backslash (\)
character.

The constraints in the SDC format can be broadly categorized as:

  1. Constraints for timing
  2. Constraints for area and power
  3. Constraints for design rules
  4. Constraints for interfaces
  5. Constraints for specific modes and configurations
  6. Exceptions to design requirements
  7. Miscellaneous commands

Some of the constraints can fall into more than one category.

13.2: Constraints for Timing

(Heading style: Bold | Dark Blue)

Constraints for timing provide guidance on design parameters that affect operational
frequency. It includes commands to specify clock characteristics, delays on the port, and
pins and paths.

Table 13.1: Constraints for Timing

Command name

Description

create_clock

Creates a clock object and defines its waveform in the current design

set_clock_latency

Specifies the clock latency for clocks, ports, or pins

create_generated_clock

Creates a generated clock object

set_propagated_clock

Specifies propagated (rather than ideal) clock latency

set_clock_gating_check

Puts setup and hold checks on clock-gating cells

set_ideal_transition

Specifies ideal transition for the ideal network and ideal nets

set_timing_derate

Sets a derating factor on the design or specified objects

set_clock_uncertainty

Specifies uncertainty (skew + jitter + margin)

set_input_delay

Sets input delay on pins or input ports

set_output_delay

Sets output delay on pins or output ports

set_ideal_latency

Sets ideal latency on ports and leaf cell pins

set_clock_transition

Sets transition time at clock pins of sequential devices

set_drive

Sets rise or fall drive attributes

set_fanout_load

Sets fanout load on output ports

set_input_transition

Sets transition values on input and inout ports

set_clock_groups

Defines mutually exclusive or asynchronous clocks

set_max_time_borrow

Limits time borrowing in latches

set_data_check

Sets data-to-data setup and hold checks

set_clock_sense

Specifies clock sense

set_ideal_network

Marks ideal networks

set_resistance

Sets resistance on nets

group_path

Groups paths for cost calculations

set_load

Sets load on ports and nets

13.3: Constraints for Area and Power

(Heading style: Bold | Dark Blue)

Constraints for area and power include commands that provide guidance on the area design
must fit within and power requirements for optimization.

Table 13.2: Constraints for Area and Power

Command name

Description

set_max_area

Sets max area attribute

set_level_shifter_threshold

UPF command

set_level_shifter_strategy

Sets level shifting strategy

create_voltage_area

Creates voltage areas

set_max_dynamic_power

Sets target dynamic power

set_max_leakage_power

Sets target leakage power

13.4: Constraints for Design Rules

(Heading style: Bold | Dark Blue)

Constraints for design rules include commands that provide guidance on some of the
requirements of the target technology.

Table 13.3: Constraints for Design Rules

Command name

Description

set_max_capacitance

Sets max capacitance

set_max_transition

Sets max transition

set_min_capacitance

Sets min capacitance

set_max_fanout

Sets max fanout

13.5: Constraints for Interfaces

(Heading style: Bold | Dark Blue)

Constraints for interfaces include commands that provide guidance on the assumptions design
needs to make about blocks it will be connected to.

Table 13.4: Constraints for Interfaces

Command name

Description

set_drive

Sets drive strength

set_driving_cell

Specifies driving library cell

set_input_transition

Sets input transition

set_load

Sets load

set_fanout_load

Sets fanout load

set_port_fanout_number

Sets external fanout

set_input_delay

Sets input delay

set_output_delay

Sets output delay

13.6: Constraints for Specific Modes and Configurations

(Heading style: Bold | Dark Blue)

Constraints for what-if analysis include commands that help designers make assumptions on
allowed values on ports and pins.

Table 13.5: Constraints for Specific Modes and Configurations

Command name

Description

set_case_analysis

Sets constant logic value

set_logic_dc

Specifies don't-care inputs

set_logic_zero

Specifies logic zero

set_logic_one

Specifies logic one

13.7: Exceptions to Design Constraints

(Heading style: Bold | Dark Blue)

This category includes commands that help designers relax requirements set by other
constraints.

Table 13.6: Exceptions to Design Constraints

Command name

Description

set_false_path

Removes timing constraints

set_multi_cycle_path

Modifies cycle relationship

set_disable_timing

Disables timing arcs

set_max_delay*

Sets max delay

set_min_delay*

Sets min delay

13.8: Miscellaneous Commands

(Heading style: Bold | Dark Blue)

These commands provide guidance on operating conditions, wire load model, units, and SDC
version.

Table 13.7: Miscellaneous Commands

Command name

Description

set_wire_load_model

Sets wire load model

set_wire_load_mode

Sets wire load mode

set_wire_load_selection_group

Sets selection group

set_wire_load_min_block_size

Sets min block size

set_units

Sets units

set_operating_conditions

Defines operating conditions

sdc_version

Shows SDC version

13.9: Design Query in SDC

(Heading style: Bold | Dark Blue)

SDC provides commands to access design information for query and traversal.

Table 13.8: Query Commands in SDC

Command name

Description

get_cells

Gets cells

get_ports

Gets ports

get_pins

Gets pins

get_nets

Gets nets

get_clocks

Gets clocks

all_inputs

Gets all inputs

all_outputs

Gets all outputs

all_registers

Gets registers

all_clocks

Gets all clocks

get_libs

Gets libraries

get_lib_cells

Gets library cells

get_lib_pins

Gets library pins

current_design

Sets current design

13.10: Clocks (Please refer to chapter 4)

(Heading style: Bold | Dark Blue)

A synchronous design is one where a control signal triggers the circuit to transition from one
state to another. Such a control signal is called a clock, and the edge on which the design
triggers is called the active edge of the clock.

Clock characteristics include:

  1. Period
  2. Active edge
  3. Duty cycle

The period of a clock indicates the time after which a clock repeats its behaviour.

If the period is T, frequency is 1/T, represented in Hz, MHz, or GHz.

Clock frequency alone does not define performance. Architecture and pipelining also play a
major role. This misconception is known as the “megahertz myth”, a term coined by
Steve Jobs in 2001.

13.10.1: Clock Edge and Duty Cycle

A clock will have positive and negative edges. A positive edge is when the clock transitions from state 0 to state 1. Let us consider the waveform of the clock as shown in Fig. 13.3. The clock has a positive edge at time = {0, 10, 20, 30 ....). A negative edge is when the clock transitions from state 1 to state 0. In Fig. 13.3, the clock has a negative edge at time = {7, 17, 27 ...).

A clock is said to be in high transition as it changes state from 0 to 1 and is said to be in a positive phase, while it holds this value until the next change. The clock shown in Fig. 13.3 is in a positive phase from t = 0 to t = 7 ns.

When a clock changes value from 1 to 0, it is said to be in low transition and a negative phase, while it holds this value until the next change.

For the circuit in Fig. 13.4, the positive edge of the clock at time {t = 0, t = 10 ...} will trigger the circuit. However, for the circuit in Fig. 13.5, the negative edge of the clock at time {t = 7, t = 17 ...) will trigger the circuit.

The duty cycle is defined as the percentage of the time clock spends in the positive phase as a fraction of its total time period.

Therefore, for the positive edge-triggered design, the clock has a duty cycle of 70%, since it spends 70% of its time (7 out of the 10ns) in its positive phase. The amount of time also has a direct implication on how long a circuit is ON, thereby affecting its power consumption.

The difference in the time interval between the positive and negative edge of a clock is referred to as the high pulse width, which is the same as the time the design is in the high state. This is also applicable to latch-based designs.

13.10.2: Generated Clocks

Most complex designs require more than one clock for its functioning. When there are multiple clocks in a design, they would need to interact or share a relationship.

Asynchronous clocks are clock signals that don't share a fixed phase relationship. Having only asynchronous clocks in the design makes it really hard to meet setup and hold requirements when multiple clock domains are interacting.

Synchronous clocks share a fixed phase relationship. More often than not synchronous clocks originate from the same source.

Today's SoCs (System on Chip) contain heterogeneous devices within the same chip. This could include very high-speed processors as well as low-speed memories all on the same chip. These elements working at different speeds are usually triggered by different clocks.

Each portion operating on its own clock could bring in asynchronicity in the design. This may result in several clocks being derived from one master clock. Such clocks are referred to as generated clocks or derived clocks.

These clocks can be generated in multiple ways:

  1. Clock dividers
  2. Clock multipliers
  3. Clock gating

13.10.3: Clock Divider

A clock divider generates a clock of higher period and lower frequency compared to the original source clock. A typical example of a clock divider is a 2-bit ripple counter.

Figure 13.6 shows the circuit of a ripple counter. For this circuit, if the period of the clock at the input of the first flop is 10ns, then the waveform generated at the LSB (least significant bit) is divided by 2, which means it has a period of 20ns.

For the same design, the waveform at the MSB (most significant bit) is divided by 4, which means it has a period of 40ns.

13.10.4: Clock Multiplier

Figure 13.6: 2-bit Ripple Counter

A clock multiplier is a circuit where the frequency is increased, and the clock period is decreased for a faster clocking rate. This technique is typically used in microprocessors and on internal buses so as to improve the overall throughput of the processor and is generally used in conjunction with internal cache memories.

Figure 13.7 shows the circuit of a simple clock multiplier, where the clock frequency is doubled. The circuit is a simple implementation of the clock and its delayed version.

The delay can be introduced in the line by the use of buffers and invertors. It is more common to use PLLs (phase-locked loops) to achieve frequency multiplication.

13.10.5: Clock Gating

Figure 13.7: A simple Clock Multiplier

Clock-gating technique has become very popular since the mid-1990s to reduce power consumption. Power in a circuit is consumed when a flop or register in the design switches state due to a clock trigger.

However, in a design portions of the logic may not be getting used at certain times. During that stage, disabling the clock to those portions of the design reduces the switching power. This is achieved by having to enable logic before the clock and such a clock is called a gated clock.

Figure 13.8 shows the example of a gated clock. We can also use clock gating to obtain divided clocks with waveforms similar to those shown in Fig. 13.6.

The concept of clock gating can be extended to create clock pulses. Let us consider Fig. 13.9 where the clock is gated via a chain of odd numbers of inverters. Depending on the delay in the chain of inverters, a pulse will be generated.

This method is used to improve performance and reduce power as well. It is important to ensure that clock gating is typically done with only one clock.

13.10.6: Clock Groups

When a design has more than one clock, the timing of such a design depends not just on the frequency of clocks but also on the relation the clocks share with each other.

Synchronous clocks are clocks which share a deterministic phase relationship. More often than not, synchronous clocks share the same source.

On the other hand, asynchronous clocks are clocks which don't share a fixed phase relationship. Let us consider Fig. 13.10 — if the two clocks C1 and C2 are generated from different sources, then they are treated as asynchronous.

The section of the design driven by each of these clocks forms a clock domain. The signals that interface between these clock domains driven by asynchronous clocks are called asynchronous clock domain crossings, or abbreviated as CDC.

13.10.7: Setup and Hold Timing Check

Let us consider Fig. 13.10. In this simple circuit, there is a launch flop (F1) that launches data that is captured by the capture flop (F2).

As described earlier, Setup is defined as the time by which data needs to be available before the active edge of the clock, and hold is the time for which the data must remain stable after the active edge of the clock so that data is properly registered by the flip-flop.

The design would need to ensure that data on the active edge of the launch flop (F1) is captured by the closest following active edge of the capture flop (F2). This is called the setup timing check.

Figure 13.11 shows the waveform of the clocks for the design.

Let us assume that tF is the delay from Clock to Q pin of launch flop (F1) and tc is the delay within the combination cloud. This means data arrives at flop F2 at time tF + tc.

Let us also assume that the edges of clocks C1 and C2 are perfectly aligned and the setup requirement of capture flop (F2) is tsetup.

Since the next clock arrives at F2 at the next edge, which is tperiod (period of clock C2), then for data from flop F1 to be captured by F2, the data must arrive at least tsetup time before the next active edge of F2.

This setup timing check can be represented as:

tF + tc < tperiod − tsetup

Once the setup requirement is met, the hold requirements have to be met as well.

The hold timing check ensures the hold timing is met between the active edge of the launch clock and the same edge of the capture clock.

This hold timing check can be represented as:

tF + tc > tHold

If the edges are not aligned, and t1 is the time for the clock to reach the launch flop and t2 is the time for the clock to reach the capture flop, then the equations become:

t1 + tF + tc < t2 + tperiod − tSetup

t1 + tF + tc > t2 + tHold

13.10.8: Fast to Slow Clocks

Let us consider the case when the period of the launch clock is less than the period of the capture clock.

Let C1 have a period of 10ns (50% duty cycle) and C2 have a period of 15ns (50% duty cycle).

The clocks can be represented as:

create_clock -period 10 -name C1 -waveform {0 5} [get_pins F1/CK]

create_clock -period 15 -name C2 -waveform {0 7.5} [get_pins F2/CK]

The waveforms repeat after 30ns. The most restrictive setup check occurs for launch at 10ns and capture at 15ns. The most restrictive hold check occurs when both edges are at 0.

13.10.9: Slow to Fast Clocks

If the periods of clocks C1 (15ns) and C2 (10ns) are reversed, the most restrictive setup timing check occurs between the launch edge at 15ns and the capture edge at 20ns.

The most restrictive hold check remains at time 0 for both edges.

13.10.10: Multiple Clocks Where Periods Synchronize in More than Two Cycles

Let the period of clock C1 be 6ns and the period of clock C2 be 10ns. The clocks realign at t = 30ns, which is the LCM of the two periods.

The most restrictive setup check occurs when the launch is at 18ns and capture is at 20ns. The most restrictive hold check occurs when both edges are at 0.

13.11: Asynchronous Clocks

If the clocks don't share a phase relationship, the arrival of the launch clock and capture clock will not be deterministic relative to each other. This means setup and hold timing requirements could vary in every cycle.

This becomes a serious timing problem when analyzing asynchronous clocks, especially when signals interact across clock domain crossings.

If the input of flip-flop F2 changes while it is being captured, the output of F2 could be unstable for a certain period of time. This phenomenon is called metastability and needs to be resolved using synchronizers.

Since timing checks must be done on the most restrictive edge pair, at least one edge pair will violate timing.

To prevent implementation tools from unnecessarily trying to meet timing on such paths, these crossings should be identified using set_clock_groups.

17: Synthesis Reports

We already discussed about synthesis flow, simply we can formulate the synthesis process as:

Synthesis = Translation + Logic Optimization + Mapping

HDL → Generic Boolean Logic → Optimize → Map → Netlist

17.1: Synthesis Output Files

Below are the various output files that can be generated after the Synthesis process:

  1. Design.v – Verilog structural netlist
  2. Design.sdf – Standard delay file for timing simulation
  3. Design.rep – Synthesis report (timing, area, etc.)
  4. Design.ddc – Synopsys database format (to view in DV)
  5. Design.sdc – Constraints for Encounter / ICC2 place & route
  6. Design.pow – Power estimate

17.2: Synthesis Report Generation

  1. check_timing
     This command checks for constraint problems such as undefined clocking, undefined input arrival times, and undefined output constraints.
  2. report_qor
     This command reports timing-path group and cell count details, along with current design statistics such as combinational, non-combinational, and total area.
    The command also reports static power, design rule violations, and compile-time details.
  3. report_constraint
     This command tells you whether the design meets the timing, area, power, and design rule constraints.
  4. report_timing
     This command provides detailed, point-by-point information on the clock paths and data paths that have the worst slack, along with the delay and slack calculation.
    We can control the scope of the design that is reported, the number of paths to report, and the types of path information that are included in the report.
    This information is very helpful in determining the cause of timing violations and how to fix them.

17.2.1: Additional Timing Report Options Available

i. -to {list of signals}
     Inputs / flip-flop outputs to these signals

ii. -from {list of signals}
     Flip-flop outputs / inputs to these signals

iii. -through {list of pins}
     Paths that go through these pins

iv. -max_paths N
     Number of paths to report

v. -loops {timing loops}
     Timing loops (combinational logic feedback)

  1. check_design
