     This check is to report problems like undriven input ports, unloaded output ports, nets/ports with multiple drivers, unloaded nets, pin mismatch, cells or instances without I/O pins/ports, etc.
  2. check_mv_design
     This command checks the design’s multivoltage constraints, electrical isolation requirements, and connection rules.
  3. report_clock_timing
     It reports the clock latency, transition time, and skew characteristics at specified clock pins of sequential elements in the network.

17.3: Writing Files

Once the design is successfully compiled and verified after analyses, the next and final step is to write out the files.

The write command is used for writing out the required files such as the netlist.

Syntax:

write -format [Verilog | ddc | VHDL] -hierarchy -output [name]

18: Process, Voltage, Temperature Corners

18.1: Corner

The corner characterizes the physical environment for Timing Analysis, it is an extreme point in the PVT/RC space where cell and net delays have extreme values. A particular one cell library and RC model are specified for STA run. Corners are meant to capture variations in the manufacturing process, along with expected variations in the Voltage and Temperature of the environment in which the chip will operate. Corners are independent of functional settings.

As technology shrinks, variations increase, since smaller geometries have had a higher variability. As a result, the number of Corners and Derates also grows. It is important to find the minimum number of Corners, because run-time and Turn Around Time increases with an increased number of Corners.
E.g., run only slow metal at SS for Maximum Frequency.

Also, each Corner needs its own OCV timing margins. The more Corners are used, the more pessimistic the timing signoff will be.

Table 18.1: Corners and their parameters with respect to RC

Parameters

Resistance

Capacitance

Remark

Surface

↑

↑

Coupling

Temperature

↑

↑

—

Width

↓

↑

—

Thickness

↓

↓

—

Space

No effect

No effect

Space between the same metal plate

At each global Corner the Die experiences

  1. External Voltage (like Minimum, Maximum, Typical)
  2. Temperature (like Minimum, Typical, Maximum)
  3. Process Shifts in (independent):
     i. Transistors
  • Slow: SS
  • Typical: TT
  • Fast: FF or mixed SF & FS
  1. ii. Interconnects
  • 4 RC-extremes
  • RC-typical
  • Via Minimum, Maximum, Typical Capacitance / Resistance

Vias are independent and not practically correlated with RC-wire models. Possible Vias models are:
VRCBEST, VCBEST, VRCWORST, VCWORST, VRCTYP

Total number of Corners

Total number of Corners =

{P: SS & FF & TT} ×

{V: Min. & Max. & Typ.} ×

{T: Min. & Max. & Typ.} ×

{RC: RCBEST, CBEST, RCWORST, CWORST, RCTYP}

Example:
 3 × 3 × 3 × 5 = 135 PVT/RC Corners

Aging Degradation Corners

By considering Aging Degradation, two more corners will come into the picture:

  1. Beginning-Of-Life (BOL)
  2. End-Of-Life (EOL)

Additional Reasons for More Corners in Advanced Nodes

Even more, Corners are needed for advanced nodes due to:

  1. Temperature Inversion
  2. Non-Linearity in Voltage
  3. Designs with multi-voltage domains
  4. Additional voltages for over- and under-drive design modes
  5. DPT (Double Patterning Technology) may add new corners
  6. Via Capacitance Corners (additional to resistance corners) due to using wide vias
  7. Using FinFET and 3D structures may also contribute to Corner numbers and may decrease model accuracy

Design Impact

Using so many PVT / RC / Via corners will be not acceptable from the design time and cost considerations. Additionally, the number of Signoff Scenarios is a product of Corners × Modes (functional, test, etc.) and becomes too big to be handled by the tools.

18.3 PVT Variations

(Google Docs → Heading 1 | Suggested color: Dark Blue)

PVT variations refer to Process, Voltage, and Temperature variations. These variations significantly impact propagation delay, performance, power, and reliability of digital circuits.

Process Variations

(Heading 2 | Dark Green)

Process variations arise due to manufacturing imperfections and cause differences in transistor characteristics across a chip.

Key Points

  1. Process variation causes variation in propagation delay.
  2. Smaller transistors are generally faster and have lower propagation delay.
  3. Variations occur in process parameters such as:
  • Threshold voltage (Vt)
  • Channel length
  • Oxide thickness
  • Width-to-length ratio (W/L)

Die Location Effect

  • Dies at the center of the wafer tend to have more accurate process values.
  • Dies near the periphery deviate more from nominal process values.

These variations directly influence current drive capability and hence timing delay.

Voltage Variations

(Heading 2 | Dark Green)

Supply voltage variations significantly affect circuit speed.

Key Points

  1. Higher voltage → higher current → lower propagation delay
  2. Voltage drops occur due to:
  • Non-zero resistance in supply wires
  • IR drop across power grids
  1. Voltage is not constant over time and varies across the chip.

Voltage variation across the chip leads to non-uniform delay behavior.

Temperature Variations

(Heading 2 | Dark Green)

Temperature affects transistor mobility and threshold voltage.

Key Points

  1. Higher temperature reduces threshold voltage, increasing current and reducing delay.
  2. Higher temperature also:
  • Reduces carrier mobility due to electron scattering
  • Increases sheet resistance
  • Increases diffusion depths
  1. Oxide thickness variation and increased voltage drop contribute to delay.
  2. Increased temperature causes increased leakage and power dissipation.
  3. Temperature varies dynamically due to:
  • Switching activity
  • Short-circuit power
  • Leakage power

Why PVT Impact is Increasing

(Heading 2 | Dark Green)

With each new technology node, PVT variations are increasing.

  • Traditional corner-based analysis introduces pessimism.
  • Pessimism leads to:
  • Increased design effort
  • Longer time-to-market
  • Possible loss of revenue
  • Worst-case conditions do not always occur at single corners.

Limitation of Corner Models

  • Cannot simultaneously model fast and slow interconnects.
  • Requires multiple simulations → inefficient and unreliable.

Statistical Timing Analysis (STA)

(Heading 2 | Dark Green)

Statistical analysis:

  • Captures all parameter variations simultaneously
  • Accounts for correlation between parameters
  • Improves yield prediction

Example:

  • Gate length variation increased from 35% at 130nm to ~60% at 70nm

18.4 Sources of Variations

(Heading 1 | Dark Blue)

Margins are used to cover variations, but as clock frequencies increase, margins consume a large portion of the clock cycle.

Variations arise from multiple physical sources, not a single factor.

18.4.1 Process Variations

(Heading 2 | Dark Green)

  • Occur after fabrication
  • Affect devices and interconnects
  • Lead to parametric yield, defined as the fraction of chips meeting performance specs

Types of Yield

  • Manufacturing Yield: No catastrophic failures
  • Parametric Yield: Meets performance constraints
  • Timing Yield: Meets timing constraints
  • Power Yield: Meets power constraints

Frequency and Leakage Variation

(Heading 2 | Dark Green)

  • ~20× leakage variation
  • ~30% frequency variation
  • Leads to frequency binning
  • High-frequency chips may have high leakage → discarded
  • Low-frequency chips with high leakage also discarded

Vt Variation Effects

(Heading 2 | Dark Green)

  • Vt is normally distributed
  • σ ≈ 30mV (180nm technology)
  • Leads to:
  • Leakage variation
  • Performance variation
  • Different critical paths per chip

18.4.2 Environmental Variations

(Heading 2 | Dark Green)

Includes:

  • Temperature
  • Supply voltage
  • Switching activity (input vectors)

Key Characteristics

  • Time-dependent
  • Workload-dependent
  • Not treated statistically
  • Worst-case conditions assumed during verification

Designers limit:

  • Voltage drop to 5–10%
  • Temperature hot-spots

Leakage Feedback Loop

  • Higher temperature → higher leakage
  • Higher leakage → higher temperature
  • Can lead to thermal runaway

18.4.3 Modeling Variations

(Heading 2 | Dark Green)

Arise due to inaccuracies in delay and power models.

  • Conservative models → pessimism
  • Aggressive models → yield loss
  • Affect all fabricated samples

Designers generally choose conservative margins.

Time-Dependent Degradation Effects

(Heading 3)

Includes:

  • Hot carrier effects
  • NBTI
  • Electromigration

Inter-Die vs Intra-Die Variations

(Heading 1 | Dark Blue)

18.4.4 Inter-Die Variations

(Heading 2 | Dark Green)

  • Same variation across entire die
  • Occurs die-to-die, wafer-to-wafer, lot-to-lot
  • Represented using a single random variable

Mathematical Model
 P = Pnom + ΔPinter

  • ΔPinter is zero-mean
  • Typically Gaussian distributed

Corner models capture inter-die variation but fail when multiple correlated parameters exist.

18.4.5 Intra-Die Variations

(Heading 2 | Dark Green)

  • Variation across locations within a single die
  • Requires separate random variables per device
  • Can be:
  • Spatially correlated
  • Spatially uncorrelated (random)

Complete Model
 P = Pnom + ΔPinter + ΔPspatial(x, y) + ΔPrandom

Intra-die variations increase computational complexity and make Monte Carlo methods impractical.

18.4.6 Supply Voltage Variations

(Heading 1 | Dark Blue)

  • Caused by uneven switching activity
  • Results in:
  • Voltage droop
  • Temperature hot-spots
  • Leakage variation

Supply voltage scaling is limited to ~15% due to:

  1. Threshold voltage scaling difficulty
  2. Performance constraints

Power delivery impedance does not scale → voltage drop becomes significant.

18.4.7 Temperature Variations

(Heading 1 | Dark Blue)

  • Affects both device and interconnect performance
  • Causes mismatch between blocks
  • Leads to functional failures

18.5 Temperature Inversion

(Heading 1 | Dark Blue)

Normally:

  • Higher temperature → higher delay

But for <65nm technologies:

  • Delay increases at low temperature
  • Maximum delay at −40°C
  • This phenomenon is called Temperature Inversion

Why Temperature Inversion Occurs

(Heading 2 | Dark Green)

MOSFET current equation:
Ip = (1/2) μnCox (W/L)(VGS − VTH)²

  • At higher nodes (>65nm): mobility dominates
  • At lower nodes (<65nm): VGS − VTH is small
  • Reduced current → increased delay at low temperature

18.6 Corner Analysis

(Heading 1 | Dark Blue)

Corners combine:

  • Process
  • Voltage
  • Temperature
  • RC variations

18.6.1 RC Corners

(Heading 2 | Dark Green)

  1. CBEST (CMIN) – Minimum capacitance → min path
  2. CWORST (CMAX) – Maximum capacitance → max path
  3. RC-BEST (RCMIN) – Min RC product → long nets min path
  4. RC-WORST (RCMAX) – Max RC product → long nets max path
  5. Typical – Nominal values

18.6.2 Cross Corner Analysis

(Heading 2 | Dark Green)

Due to temperature inversion:

  • Worst delay may not occur at highest temperature
  • Designers must consider low-temperature corners

18.6.3 Two Corner Analysis

(Heading 2 | Dark Green)

  1. Setup: Weak process, low voltage, high temperature
  2. Hold: Strong process, high voltage, low temperature

18.7 Modes of Analysis

(Heading 1 | Dark Blue)

Mode = Operational state with unique timing constraints
Defined in .sdc

Examples:

  • Functional
  • Sleep
  • Scan shift
  • LBIST
  • MBIST
  • JTAG

18.8 MC/MM Analysis

(Heading 1 | Dark Blue)

MCMM = Multi-Corner Multi-Mode Analysis

Purpose:

  • Avoid setup/hold fixes breaking other modes
  • Analyze multiple scenarios concurrently

Advantages:

  1. Fewer hold buffers
  2. Fewer ECOs
  3. Faster closure
  4. Reduced pessimism (DFV)

Synopsys: MCMM
Cadence: MMMC

19 Liberty File

(Heading 1 | Dark Blue)

Liberty (.lib) files define:

  • Timing
  • Power
  • Noise
  • PVT behavior

Generated by cell library characterization.

19.1 Cell Library Characterization

(Heading 1 | Dark Blue)

Purpose:

  • Model timing, power, and noise behavior
  • Required for RTL-to-GDSII flow

Uses:

  • SPICE simulations
  • PVT corners
  • Input slew and output load grids

19.7 Rise Time, Fall Time, and Delay

(Heading 1 | Dark Blue)

Definitions:

  • Rise time: 10% → 90%
  • Fall time: 90% → 10%
  • tPLH, tPHL measured at 50%

19.8 First-Order RC Model

(Heading 1 | Dark Blue)

Propagation delay:
tp = 0.69RC

CMOS inverter modeled as:

  • R = ON resistance
  • C = Load capacitance

19.11 NLDM Timing Model

(Heading 1 | Dark Blue)

NLDM uses:

  • Input transition
  • Output capacitance
  • 2D lookup tables

Tables include:

  1. Rise delay
  2. Fall delay
  3. Rise transition
  4. Fall transition

19.12 CCS Timing Model

(Heading 1 | Dark Blue)

CCS uses:

  • Output current waveforms
  • Higher accuracy
  • Better modeling for deep sub-micron nodes

CMOS Latch and Flip-Flop

(Google Docs → Heading 1 | Color: Dark Blue)

This chapter explains CMOS technology, transmission gates, latches, flip-flops, and timing concepts such as setup time and clock-to-Q delay, which are fundamental building blocks of sequential digital circuits.

21.1 CMOS (Complementary Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor)

(Heading 2 | Dark Green)

CMOS is a highly successful technology that combines p-type and n-type MOSFETs in a complementary and symmetrical manner to implement logic functions.

Key Features of CMOS

  • Uses one pMOS and one nMOS transistor
  • Forms the basic building block of integrated circuits
  • Widely used in:
  • Digital logic
  • Image sensors
  • Data converters
  • Transceivers
  • Analog and mixed-signal circuits (e.g., op-amps)

Operation of CMOS Transistor Pair

(Heading 3)

  • pMOS:
  • Conducts when gate voltage is LOW
  • Low resistance between source and drain for LOW gate voltage
  • nMOS:
  • Conducts when gate voltage is HIGH
  • Low resistance between source and drain for HIGH gate voltage

Both gates are connected together, and both drains are connected together.

Working Principle

  • Input = HIGH → nMOS ON, pMOS OFF
  • Input = LOW → pMOS ON, nMOS OFF

 One transistor is always OFF, which leads to low power consumption.

Power and Switching Characteristics

(Heading 3)

  • Transistors are:
  • Series with respect to power supply
  • Parallel with respect to signal
  • During switching:
  • Brief high-intensity current spike
  • Can cause:
  • Power quality issues
  • RF interference
  • Despite this, CMOS provides:
  • Low average power
  • Low heat dissipation
  • High integration density

CMOS Physical Structure

(Heading 3)

  • Typically implemented using n-well technology
  • Body is tied to source in discrete devices
  • Body connection may vary in ICs

21.2 CMOS Transmission Gate (TG)

(Heading 2 | Dark Green)

A CMOS transmission gate consists of:

  • One nMOS
  • One pMOS
  • Connected in parallel
  • Controlled by complementary gate signals

It acts as a bidirectional switch between nodes A and B, controlled by signal C.

Operation of Transmission Gate

(Heading 3)

  • C = HIGH (VDD):
  • Both transistors ON
  • Low resistance path between A and B
  • C = LOW:
  • Both transistors OFF
  • Output in High-Impedance (Hi-Z) state

Signal Passing Characteristics

(Heading 3)

  • nMOS → good at passing logic 0, poor at logic 1
  • pMOS → good at passing logic 1, poor at logic 0
  • Combined → full logic swing

Applications of Transmission Gates

(Heading 3)

  • Multiplexers
  • XOR / XNOR
  • Latches
  • Flip-Flops

21.3 Memory Elements in Sequential Circuits

(Heading 2 | Dark Green)

Memory elements store logic values and are essential in digital ICs.

Types of Memory Elements

  1. Latches – Level sensitive
  2. Flip-Flops – Edge triggered

21.4 CMOS Latch

(Heading 2 | Dark Green)

A latch is a level-sensitive device.

  • Positive latch → transparent when CLK = HIGH
  • Negative latch → transparent when CLK = LOW

Output Q depends on:

  • Clock level
  • Input D

21.4.1 Positive D-Latch Using Transmission Gate

(Heading 3)

Structure

  • Two transmission gates
  • Two inverters

Operation

  • CLK = HIGH
  • T1 ON, T2 OFF
  • Q follows D
  • CLK = LOW
  • T1 OFF, T2 ON
  • Output holds previous value

21.4.2 Negative D-Latch Using Transmission Gate

(Heading 3)

Operation

  • CLK = LOW
  • T1 ON, T2 OFF
  • Q follows D
  • CLK = HIGH
  • T1 OFF, T2 ON
  • Output holds stored value

21.5 CMOS Flip-Flop

(Heading 2 | Dark Green)

A D Flip-Flop is an edge-triggered device.

  • Output changes only at:
  • Rising edge (positive edge-triggered)
  • Falling edge (negative edge-triggered)
  • Output remains constant between clock edges

21.5.1 D Flip-Flop Using Transmission Gates

(Heading 3)

A flip-flop is formed by:

  • Negative level-sensitive latch (Master)
  • Positive level-sensitive latch (Slave)

Operation of Positive Edge-Triggered D-FF

(Heading 3)

CLK = LOW

  • T1, T4 ON
  • T2, T3 OFF
  • Master latch active
  • Data stored at internal node (node 4)
  • Slave latch holds previous output

CLK = HIGH

  • T2, T3 ON
  • T1, T4 OFF
  • Stored data transferred to output Q
  • Output changes only at rising edge

 Any input change appears at output only at the clock edge

21.6 Setup Time in a Flip-Flop

(Heading 2 | Dark Green)

Setup Time Definition

The minimum time before the active clock edge during which data must be stable to be correctly captured.

  • Measured with respect to active clock edge
  • Violation → Setup violation

Explanation

  • Data travels from D → node 4
  • Must reach node 4 before CLK rises
  • Time taken is called setup time

21.7 Clock-to-Q Delay

(Heading 2 | Dark Green)

Definition

The delay between:

  • Clock active edge
  • Output Q transition

Explanation

  • When CLK = HIGH, T2 and T3 turn ON
  • Data propagates from node 2 → node 6 (Q)
  • Delay includes:
  • One transmission gate delay
  • One inverter delay

Timing Notes

(Heading 2 | Dark Green)

  • Setup and hold times are measured only at the active clock edge
  • Hold time will be discussed in the next chapter

22. Clock Variations

(Google Docs → Heading 1 | Color: Dark Blue)

Closing timing with correct timing constraints is critical in digital design. If timing is not properly closed, the design may violate setup or hold constraints, eventually leading to metastability.

The clock acts as the reference signal in digital systems for sampling inputs and outputs.

Clock Basics

(Heading 2 | Dark Green)

  • Clock period: Time between two adjacent active edges or inactive edges
  • Ideal clock:
  • Zero rise time
  • Zero fall time
  • Real clock:
  • Non-zero rise and fall times
  • Transition is gradual, not instantaneous

Clock Slew

(Heading 3)

  • Rise/fall transition rate is measured in volts/second
  • This transition uncertainty is called slew rate
  • Defined between:
  • 30% → 70% (or sometimes 10% → 90%) of voltage swing

Types of Clocks in Digital Design

(Heading 2 | Dark Green)

There are three types of clocks used in digital designs:

  1. Gated clocks
  2. Virtual clocks
  3. Generated clocks

Clock Grouping in Timing Analysis

(Heading 3)

  • Clocks sharing the same source → Synchronous clocks
  • Clocks with different sources → Asynchronous clocks
  • Timing tools group clocks automatically based on constraints

Clock Variations and Uncertainties

(Heading 2 | Dark Green)

When a clock propagates through a design, it experiences uncertainties, mainly:

  1. Clock Skew
  2. Clock Jitter
  3. Clock Latency

All of these affect timing analysis and must be modeled accurately.

22.1 Clock Skew

(Heading 2 | Dark Green)

Clock skew is the difference in arrival time of the same clock edge at two different registers.

Cause

  • Uneven delay in the clock distribution path

Types of Clock Skew

(Heading 3)

  • Positive Clock Skew:
  • Clock and data move in the same direction
  • Negative Clock Skew:
  • Clock and data move in opposite directions

Clock skew can either help or hurt timing, depending on direction and path.

22.2 Clock Jitter

(Heading 2 | Dark Green)

Clock jitter refers to variation in the position of a clock edge relative to its ideal position.

Characteristics

  • Clock edge may arrive early or late
  • Clock period becomes non-uniform
  • Can increase or decrease available timing margin

Sources of Clock Jitter

(Heading 3)

  1. Internal PLL circuitry
  2. Thermal noise in crystal oscillators
  3. Transmitters and receivers of resonating devices

22.3 Clock Latency

(Heading 2 | Dark Green)

Clock latency is the time taken by the clock signal to travel from its source to the clock pin of a register.

Types of Clock Latency

(Heading 3)

  1. Source clock latency
  2. Network clock latency

22.4 Clock Uncertainty

(Heading 2 | Dark Green)

Clock uncertainty represents the difference between clock arrivals at registers within the same domain or across domains.

Purpose of Clock Uncertainty

(Heading 3)

  • Models pessimism due to:
  • Skew
  • Jitter
  • OCV
  • Crosstalk
  • Margins
  • Specified in SDC constraints
  • Reserved during synthesis stage
  • Applied for both:
  • Setup
  • Hold

Effect

  • Setup uncertainty → reduces effective clock period
  • Hold uncertainty → adds extra safety margin

22.5 Static Clock Uncertainty

(Heading 2 | Dark Green)

Static uncertainty does not vary or varies very slowly with time.

Example

  • Clock skew

22.5.1 Sources of Static Clock Uncertainty

(Heading 3)

  1. Intentional and unintentional design mismatch
  2. On-chip variation (OCV)
  3. Load variation in clock distribution network

22.6 Dynamic Clock Uncertainty

(Heading 2 | Dark Green)

Dynamic uncertainty varies with time.

Examples

  • Clock jitter
  • Dynamic power supply variations

22.6.1 Sources of Dynamic Clock Uncertainty

(Heading 3)

  1. Voltage droop
  2. Temperature variation
  3. Clock generator jitter

Clock Usage Phases in Design Flow

(Heading 2 | Dark Green)

Phase 1: Ideal Clock

(Heading 3)

  • RTL design
  • Synthesis
  • Placement
  • Clock connected simultaneously to all flip-flops
  • No clock tree exists

Phase 2: Propagated Clock

(Heading 3)

  • Clock Tree Synthesis (CTS) inserts buffers
  • Real clock distribution path exists
  • Clock is now called a propagated clock

Clock Latency vs Insertion Delay

(Heading 2 | Dark Green)

  • Clock Latency:
  • Used when clock is ideal
  • User-defined
  • Not a measured value
  • Insertion Delay:
  • Used after CTS
  • Actual measured delay
  • Traverses real clock tree

Sometimes clock latency is treated as the target value for insertion delay.

22.7 Clock Duty Cycle Distortion

(Heading 2 | Dark Green)

Duty Cycle

  • Ratio of HIGH phase time to LOW phase time
  • Ideal value: 50%

Impact

  • Critical for:
  • Latch-based designs
  • Memory circuits
  • Phase-path designs
  • Edge-triggered FFs are more immune

Effect of Distortion

  • Time loss directly subtracts from phase time
  • Penalizes high-performance designs

Mathematical Analysis of Duty Cycle Distortion

(Heading 3)

For clock period Tper and duty cycle error Q%:

  • High phase time:
    TphH = Tper × (50 + Q) / 100
  • Low phase time:
    TphL = Tper × (50 − Q) / 100

Maximum frequency impact:

  • FmaxH = F50% × 100 / (100 + 2Q)
  • FmaxL = F50% × 100 / (100 − 2Q)

 Effective frequency penalty ≈ 2 × duty cycle offset

Clock Distribution and Duty Cycle Fidelity

(Heading 2 | Dark Green)

  • Buffer-based clock trees:
  • Asymmetric rise/fall delays
  • More duty cycle distortion
  • Inverter-based clock trees:
  • Symmetric loading
  • Better duty cycle control

Advanced Solutions

(Heading 3)

  • Differential clock distribution
  • Cross-coupled stages
  • Active duty-cycle correction

22.8 Setup Slack

(Heading 2 | Dark Green)

Setup Slack Condition

Required Time > Arrival Time

Expanded Equation

Tcapture + Tcycle − Tsetup − Uncertainty

> Tlaunch + Tck→q + Tcombo + Twire

Uncertainty includes:

  • Skew
  • Jitter
  • Slack margin

22.9 Hold Slack

(Heading 2 | Dark Green)

Hold Slack Condition

Required Time < Arrival Time

Expanded Equation

Tcapture + Thold

< Tlaunch + Tck→q + Tcombo + Twire + Uncertainty

Important Notes

(Heading 3)

  • Negative hold slack → data propagates too fast
  • Clock frequency does not affect hold timing
  • Hold violations must be fixed before setup violations

23. Hold Time

Introduction

In earlier sections, we discussed setup time. Let us briefly recap and then extend the discussion to hold time, which is equally important for correct register operation.

The data input of a register, commonly referred to as the D input, must receive incoming data at least Tsetup units before the onset of the latching edge of the clock. After this latching edge, the data becomes available at the output node Q.

In addition to setup time, the input D must be kept stable for a certain duration after the active clock edge. This duration is known as hold time (Thold).

Key Timing Parameters of a Latch

  • Setup Time (Tsetup):
    Minimum time before the active clock edge during which data must be stable.
  • Hold Time (Thold):
    Minimum time after the active clock edge during which data must remain stable.
  • Clock-to-Q Delay (Tclk-q):
    Delay between the time when data and clock are available at the latch input and when the data appears at the output Q.

These timing parameters together ensure correct data storage inside the latch.

Figure 23.1: Positive Level D-Latch Circuit

Clocking Parameters of a Register

Figure 23.2 illustrates the timing parameters associated with a register:

  • Setup Time
  • Hold Time
  • Clock-to-Q Delay
  • Cycle Time (Clock Period)

The clock is a periodic waveform that repeats every P units of time, where P is called the clock period or cycle time.

For a negative edge-triggered register, setup and hold requirements are defined with respect to the falling edge of the clock, as shown in Figure 23.2.

23.1 Hold Time in a Flip-Flop

Figure 23.3: Positive Edge-Triggered D Flip-Flop using Transmission Gates

This flip-flop consists of:

  • Master Stage: Negative level-sensitive latch
  • Slave Stage: Positive level-sensitive latch

Understanding Hold Time Inside a Flip-Flop

Hold time is measured only with respect to the active clock edge.

In Figure 23.3:

  • Input data D passes through an inverter or combinational logic before reaching transmission gate T1.
  • This logic is considered part of the flip-flop itself.
  • CLK and CLK BAR control the switching of the transmission gates.

The CLK and CLK BAR signals are delayed versions of the clock, as they pass through buffers and inverters. Therefore:

  • There is a finite delay between CLK and CLK BAR.
  • Transmission gates do not switch ON or OFF instantaneously.

During this switching interval:

  • The input data must remain stable
  • This stability ensures a stable value at the internal node W
  • A stable node W ensures a correct value at the output Q

This requirement explains why hold time exists inside a flip-flop.

Nature of Hold Time

  • A finite positive setup time always exists
  • Hold time can be:
  • Positive
  • Zero
  • Negative

The reason lies in the delay of the data path before the transmission gate.

If combinational logic exists before the transmission gate (for scan-enable, reset, set, enable, etc.):

  • This logic introduces extra delay
  • This delay determines whether hold time becomes positive, zero, or negative

23.2 Hold Time Analysis in a Register-to-Register Timing Path

Definition

Hold Time:
The minimum time for which the data (D) must remain stable at the input of the capture flip-flop after the active clock edge has arrived.

Register-to-Register Timing Path

Figure 23.4: Register-to-Register Timing Path

Path Definitions

Launch Path (Arrival Path / Data Path)

  • Data is launched from Launch Flip-Flop (FF1)
  • Data passes through:
  • Clock-to-Q delay (Tclk-Q)
  • Combinational logic delay (Tcombo)

Capture Path (Required Path / Clock Path)

  • Data is captured at Capture Flip-Flop (FF2)
  • Capture is governed by:
  • Capture clock
  • Hold time requirement of FF2

Timing Waveform Explanation

Figure 23.5: Timing Waveform for Register-to-Register Path

  • Data is launched from FF1 at time 0 sec
  • This data is intended to be captured by FF2 after one clock period (T)

Why Hold Time is Checked at the Same Clock Edge

At the same clock edge:

  • FF2 captures the current data (n)
  • FF1 launches the next data (n+1)

The purpose of the hold check is to ensure:

  • The newly launched data (n+1) does not arrive too early
  • It must not corrupt the current data (n) being captured by FF2

Therefore:

  • Data (n) must remain stable at FF2 input
  • Stability duration must be at least Thold

This required stability duration is called Hold Time.

Hold Time Equations

Basic Hold Condition

Data Arrival Time > Data Required Time

Mathematically:

Tclk-Q + Tcombo_delay > Thold

Hold Slack

Hold Slack = Data Arrival Time − Data Required Time

  • Positive Hold Slack: No hold violation
  • Negative Hold Slack: Hold violation

If arrival time is less:

  • Data is arriving too fast (early)
  • This causes a hold violation

Effect of Clock Skew on Hold Time

Positive Clock Skew

Figure 23.6: Positive Skew

  • Launch clock arrives earlier at FF1
  • Capture clock arrives later at FF2

Effects:

  • New data (n+1) is launched early
  • Capture clock has not yet arrived
  • Current data (n) must remain stable for:
  • Tskew + Thold duration

If this condition is not met:

  • Data (n) gets corrupted

 Positive skew is BAD for hold time

Negative Clock Skew

Figure 23.7: Negative Skew

  • Capture clock arrives earlier at FF2
  • Launch clock arrives later at FF1

Effects:

  • Data (n) is captured early
  • New data (n+1) has not yet been launched
  • Data (n) remains stable long enough

 Negative skew is GOOD for hold time

Hold Condition with Clock Skew

Final hold condition including skew:

Tclk-Q + Tcombo_logic > Thold + Tskew

  • Positive Tskew: Reduces hold slack (bad)
  • Negative Tskew: Increases hold slack (good)

Refer back to the Hold Slack Equation for a complete understanding of how skew impacts hold timing.

23. Hold Time

Introduction

In earlier sections, we discussed setup time. Let us briefly recap and then extend the discussion to hold time, which is equally important for correct register operation.

The data input of a register, commonly referred to as the D input, must receive incoming data at least Tsetup units before the onset of the latching edge of the clock. After this latching edge, the data becomes available at the output node Q.

In addition to setup time, the input D must be kept stable for a certain duration after the active clock edge. This duration is known as hold time (Thold).

Key Timing Parameters of a Latch

  • Setup Time (Tsetup):
    Minimum time before the active clock edge during which data must be stable.
  • Hold Time (Thold):
    Minimum time after the active clock edge during which data must remain stable.
  • Clock-to-Q Delay (Tclk-q):
    Delay between the time when data and clock are available at the latch input and when the data appears at the output Q.

These timing parameters together ensure correct data storage inside the latch.

Figure 23.1: Positive Level D-Latch Circuit

Clocking Parameters of a Register

Figure 23.2 illustrates the timing parameters associated with a register:

  • Setup Time
  • Hold Time
  • Clock-to-Q Delay
  • Cycle Time (Clock Period)

The clock is a periodic waveform that repeats every P units of time, where P is called the clock period or cycle time.

For a negative edge-triggered register, setup and hold requirements are defined with respect to the falling edge of the clock, as shown in Figure 23.2.

23.1 Hold Time in a Flip-Flop

Figure 23.3: Positive Edge-Triggered D Flip-Flop using Transmission Gates

This flip-flop consists of:

  • Master Stage: Negative level-sensitive latch
  • Slave Stage: Positive level-sensitive latch

Understanding Hold Time Inside a Flip-Flop

Hold time is measured only with respect to the active clock edge.

In Figure 23.3:

  • Input data D passes through an inverter or combinational logic before reaching transmission gate T1.
  • This logic is considered part of the flip-flop itself.
  • CLK and CLK BAR control the switching of the transmission gates.

The CLK and CLK BAR signals are delayed versions of the clock, as they pass through buffers and inverters. Therefore:

  • There is a finite delay between CLK and CLK BAR.
  • Transmission gates do not switch ON or OFF instantaneously.

During this switching interval:

  • The input data must remain stable
  • This stability ensures a stable value at the internal node W
  • A stable node W ensures a correct value at the output Q

This requirement explains why hold time exists inside a flip-flop.

Nature of Hold Time

  • A finite positive setup time always exists
  • Hold time can be:
  • Positive
  • Zero
  • Negative

The reason lies in the delay of the data path before the transmission gate.

If combinational logic exists before the transmission gate (for scan-enable, reset, set, enable, etc.):

  • This logic introduces extra delay
  • This delay determines whether hold time becomes positive, zero, or negative

23.2 Hold Time Analysis in a Register-to-Register Timing Path

Definition

Hold Time:
The minimum time for which the data (D) must remain stable at the input of the capture flip-flop after the active clock edge has arrived.

Register-to-Register Timing Path

Figure 23.4: Register-to-Register Timing Path

Path Definitions

Launch Path (Arrival Path / Data Path)

  • Data is launched from Launch Flip-Flop (FF1)
  • Data passes through:
  • Clock-to-Q delay (Tclk-Q)
  • Combinational logic delay (Tcombo)

Capture Path (Required Path / Clock Path)

  • Data is captured at Capture Flip-Flop (FF2)
  • Capture is governed by:
  • Capture clock
  • Hold time requirement of FF2

Timing Waveform Explanation

Figure 23.5: Timing Waveform for Register-to-Register Path

  • Data is launched from FF1 at time 0 sec
  • This data is intended to be captured by FF2 after one clock period (T)

Why Hold Time is Checked at the Same Clock Edge

At the same clock edge:

  • FF2 captures the current data (n)
  • FF1 launches the next data (n+1)

The purpose of the hold check is to ensure:

  • The newly launched data (n+1) does not arrive too early
  • It must not corrupt the current data (n) being captured by FF2

Therefore:

  • Data (n) must remain stable at FF2 input
  • Stability duration must be at least Thold

This required stability duration is called Hold Time.

Hold Time Equations

Basic Hold Condition

Data Arrival Time > Data Required Time

Mathematically:

Tclk-Q + Tcombo_delay > Thold

Hold Slack

Hold Slack = Data Arrival Time − Data Required Time

  • Positive Hold Slack: No hold violation
  • Negative Hold Slack: Hold violation

If arrival time is less:

  • Data is arriving too fast (early)
  • This causes a hold violation

Effect of Clock Skew on Hold Time

Positive Clock Skew

Figure 23.6: Positive Skew

  • Launch clock arrives earlier at FF1
  • Capture clock arrives later at FF2

Effects:

  • New data (n+1) is launched early
  • Capture clock has not yet arrived
  • Current data (n) must remain stable for:
  • Tskew + Thold duration

If this condition is not met:

  • Data (n) gets corrupted

 Positive skew is BAD for hold time

Negative Clock Skew

Figure 23.7: Negative Skew

  • Capture clock arrives earlier at FF2
  • Launch clock arrives later at FF1

Effects:

  • Data (n) is captured early
  • New data (n+1) has not yet been launched
  • Data (n) remains stable long enough

 Negative skew is GOOD for hold time

Hold Condition with Clock Skew

Final hold condition including skew:

Tclk-Q + Tcombo_logic > Thold + Tskew

  • Positive Tskew: Reduces hold slack (bad)
  • Negative Tskew: Increases hold slack (good)

Refer back to the Hold Slack Equation for a complete understanding of how skew impacts hold timing.

24: Hold Slack – Worked Example

Objective of the Example

To clearly understand how Hold Slack is calculated using a practical register-to-register timing path.

Given Circuit Description (Figure 24.1)

The circuit consists of:

  • A clock source
  • Clock buffers in the clock path
  • Three flip-flops: F1 (launch), F2, F3 (capture)
  • A data path buffer and an AND gate in the data path

Given Timing Parameters

Flip-Flop Parameters

  • Clock-to-Q delay (Tck→q) = 50 ps
  • Hold requirement (Thold) = 50 ps

Data Path Delays

  • Data path buffer delay = 200 ps
  • AND gate delay = 50 ps

Clock Path Delays

  • Clock buffer delay = 100 ps per buffer

Clock and Uncertainty

  • Clock period = 1 ns
  • Hold uncertainty = 50 ps

Hold Slack Condition

For a path to meet the hold requirement:

Data Arrival Time (DAT) > Data Required Time (DRT)

Hold Slack is defined as:

Hold Slack = Data Arrival Time − Data Required Time

 Important Rule: While calculating hold slack, always consider the minimum (fastest) data path.

Step 1: Data Arrival Time (DAT)

Data Arrival Time is calculated from launch flip-flop F1 to capture flip-flop F3.

Components contributing to DAT:

  • Launch clock latency
  • Clock-to-Q delay of launch flip-flop
  • Minimum data path delay

Calculation

  • Launch clock latency = 6 × 100 ps = 600 ps
  • Clock-to-Q delay = 50 ps
  • AND gate delay (minimum data path) = 50 ps

DAT = 600 + 50 + 50 = 700 ps

Step 2: Data Required Time (DRT)

Data Required Time depends on when the capture flip-flop requires stable data.

Components contributing to DRT:

  • Capture clock latency
  • Hold uncertainty
  • Hold requirement of capture flip-flop

Calculation

  • Capture clock latency = 5 × 100 ps = 500 ps
  • Hold uncertainty = 50 ps
  • Hold requirement = 50 ps

DRT = 500 + 50 + 50 = 600 ps

Step 3: Hold Slack Calculation

Hold Slack = DAT − DRT

= 700 ps − 600 ps

= +100 ps

Result

 Hold timing is MET (Positive slack)

Timing Interpretation (Figure 24.2)

  • Clock at F1 starts at 600 ps
  • Clock at F3 starts at 500 ps
  • Data arrives at 700 ps
  • Data is required at 600 ps

Since the data arrives 100 ps later than required, the hold condition is safely satisfied.

Key Student Takeaways

  • Hold analysis always uses the fastest data path
  • Clock frequency does not affect hold timing
  • Positive hold slack → Safe design
  • Negative hold slack → Hold violation (data too fast)
  • Hold fixes must be done before setup fixes

End of Chapter 24 – Hold Slack Example

23. Hold Time

Introduction

In earlier sections, we discussed setup time. Let us briefly recap and then extend the discussion to hold time, which is equally important for correct register operation.

The data input of a register, commonly referred to as the D input, must receive incoming data at least Tsetup units before the onset of the latching edge of the clock. After this latching edge, the data becomes available at the output node Q.

In addition to setup time, the input D must be kept stable for a certain duration after the active clock edge. This duration is known as hold time (Thold).

Key Timing Parameters of a Latch

  • Setup Time (Tsetup):
    Minimum time before the active clock edge during which data must be stable.
  • Hold Time (Thold):
    Minimum time after the active clock edge during which data must remain stable.
  • Clock-to-Q Delay (Tclk-q):
    Delay between the time when data and clock are available at the latch input and when the data appears at the output Q.

These timing parameters together ensure correct data storage inside the latch.

Figure 23.1: Positive Level D-Latch Circuit

Clocking Parameters of a Register

Figure 23.2 illustrates the timing parameters associated with a register:

  • Setup Time
  • Hold Time
  • Clock-to-Q Delay
  • Cycle Time (Clock Period)

The clock is a periodic waveform that repeats every P units of time, where P is called the clock period or cycle time.

For a negative edge-triggered register, setup and hold requirements are defined with respect to the falling edge of the clock, as shown in Figure 23.2.

23.1 Hold Time in a Flip-Flop

Figure 23.3: Positive Edge-Triggered D Flip-Flop using Transmission Gates

This flip-flop consists of:

  • Master Stage: Negative level-sensitive latch
  • Slave Stage: Positive level-sensitive latch

Understanding Hold Time Inside a Flip-Flop

Hold time is measured only with respect to the active clock edge.

In Figure 23.3:

  • Input data D passes through an inverter or combinational logic before reaching transmission gate T1.
  • This logic is considered part of the flip-flop itself.
  • CLK and CLK BAR control the switching of the transmission gates.

The CLK and CLK BAR signals are delayed versions of the clock, as they pass through buffers and inverters. Therefore:

  • There is a finite delay between CLK and CLK BAR.
  • Transmission gates do not switch ON or OFF instantaneously.

During this switching interval:

  • The input data must remain stable
  • This stability ensures a stable value at the internal node W
  • A stable node W ensures a correct value at the output Q

This requirement explains why hold time exists inside a flip-flop.

Nature of Hold Time

  • A finite positive setup time always exists
  • Hold time can be:
  • Positive
  • Zero
  • Negative

The reason lies in the delay of the data path before the transmission gate.

If combinational logic exists before the transmission gate (for scan-enable, reset, set, enable, etc.):

  • This logic introduces extra delay
  • This delay determines whether hold time becomes positive, zero, or negative

23.2 Hold Time Analysis in a Register-to-Register Timing Path

Definition

Hold Time:
The minimum time for which the data (D) must remain stable at the input of the capture flip-flop after the active clock edge has arrived.

Register-to-Register Timing Path

Figure 23.4: Register-to-Register Timing Path

Path Definitions

Launch Path (Arrival Path / Data Path)

  • Data is launched from Launch Flip-Flop (FF1)
  • Data passes through:
  • Clock-to-Q delay (Tclk-Q)
  • Combinational logic delay (Tcombo)

Capture Path (Required Path / Clock Path)

  • Data is captured at Capture Flip-Flop (FF2)
  • Capture is governed by:
  • Capture clock
  • Hold time requirement of FF2

Timing Waveform Explanation

Figure 23.5: Timing Waveform for Register-to-Register Path

  • Data is launched from FF1 at time 0 sec
  • This data is intended to be captured by FF2 after one clock period (T)

Why Hold Time is Checked at the Same Clock Edge

At the same clock edge:

  • FF2 captures the current data (n)
  • FF1 launches the next data (n+1)

The purpose of the hold check is to ensure:

  • The newly launched data (n+1) does not arrive too early
  • It must not corrupt the current data (n) being captured by FF2

Therefore:

  • Data (n) must remain stable at FF2 input
  • Stability duration must be at least Thold

This required stability duration is called Hold Time.

Hold Time Equations

Basic Hold Condition

Data Arrival Time > Data Required Time

Mathematically:

Tclk-Q + Tcombo_delay > Thold

Hold Slack

Hold Slack = Data Arrival Time − Data Required Time

  • Positive Hold Slack: No hold violation
  • Negative Hold Slack: Hold violation

If arrival time is less:

  • Data is arriving too fast (early)
  • This causes a hold violation

Effect of Clock Skew on Hold Time

Positive Clock Skew

Figure 23.6: Positive Skew

  • Launch clock arrives earlier at FF1
  • Capture clock arrives later at FF2

Effects:

  • New data (n+1) is launched early
  • Capture clock has not yet arrived
  • Current data (n) must remain stable for:
  • Tskew + Thold duration

If this condition is not met:

  • Data (n) gets corrupted

 Positive skew is BAD for hold time

Negative Clock Skew

Figure 23.7: Negative Skew

  • Capture clock arrives earlier at FF2
  • Launch clock arrives later at FF1

Effects:

  • Data (n) is captured early
  • New data (n+1) has not yet been launched
  • Data (n) remains stable long enough

 Negative skew is GOOD for hold time

Hold Condition with Clock Skew

Final hold condition including skew:

Tclk-Q + Tcombo_logic > Thold + Tskew

  • Positive Tskew: Reduces hold slack (bad)
  • Negative Tskew: Increases hold slack (good)

Refer back to the Hold Slack Equation for a complete understanding of how skew impacts hold timing.

24: Hold Slack – Worked Example

Objective of the Example

To clearly understand how Hold Slack is calculated using a practical register-to-register timing path.

Given Circuit Description (Figure 24.1)

The circuit consists of:

  • A clock source
  • Clock buffers in the clock path
  • Three flip-flops: F1 (launch), F2, F3 (capture)
  • A data path buffer and an AND gate in the data path

Given Timing Parameters

Flip-Flop Parameters

  • Clock-to-Q delay (Tck→q) = 50 ps
  • Hold requirement (Thold) = 50 ps

Data Path Delays

  • Data path buffer delay = 200 ps
  • AND gate delay = 50 ps

Clock Path Delays

  • Clock buffer delay = 100 ps per buffer

Clock and Uncertainty

  • Clock period = 1 ns
  • Hold uncertainty = 50 ps

Hold Slack Condition

For a path to meet the hold requirement:

Data Arrival Time (DAT) > Data Required Time (DRT)

Hold Slack is defined as:

Hold Slack = Data Arrival Time − Data Required Time

 Important Rule: While calculating hold slack, always consider the minimum (fastest) data path.

Step 1: Data Arrival Time (DAT)

Data Arrival Time is calculated from launch flip-flop F1 to capture flip-flop F3.

Components contributing to DAT:

  • Launch clock latency
  • Clock-to-Q delay of launch flip-flop
  • Minimum data path delay

Calculation

  • Launch clock latency = 6 × 100 ps = 600 ps
  • Clock-to-Q delay = 50 ps
  • AND gate delay (minimum data path) = 50 ps

DAT = 600 + 50 + 50 = 700 ps

Step 2: Data Required Time (DRT)

Data Required Time depends on when the capture flip-flop requires stable data.

Components contributing to DRT:

  • Capture clock latency
  • Hold uncertainty
  • Hold requirement of capture flip-flop

Calculation

  • Capture clock latency = 5 × 100 ps = 500 ps
  • Hold uncertainty = 50 ps
  • Hold requirement = 50 ps

DRT = 500 + 50 + 50 = 600 ps

Step 3: Hold Slack Calculation

Hold Slack = DAT − DRT

= 700 ps − 600 ps

= +100 ps

Result

 Hold timing is MET (Positive slack)

Timing Interpretation (Figure 24.2)

  • Clock at F1 starts at 600 ps
  • Clock at F3 starts at 500 ps
  • Data arrives at 700 ps
  • Data is required at 600 ps

Since the data arrives 100 ps later than required, the hold condition is safely satisfied.

Key Student Takeaways

  • Hold analysis always uses the fastest data path
  • Clock frequency does not affect hold timing
  • Positive hold slack → Safe design
  • Negative hold slack → Hold violation (data too fast)
  • Hold fixes must be done before setup fixes

End of Chapter 24 – Hold Slack Example

25: Clock Checks and Design Rule Constraints

This chapter covers clock-related integrity checks and design rule violations that are essential for reliable silicon operation. These checks are heavily used in Static Timing Analysis (STA) and are critical for both student understanding and industry practice.

25.1: Minimum Pulse Width (MPW)

Definition

The Minimum Pulse Width check ensures that the high and low durations of a clock signal are greater than the minimum required value for correct operation of sequential elements.

This requirement depends on:

  • Operating frequency
  • Technology node
  • Clock path implementation

Basic Concept

For a design operating at 1 GHz:

  • Clock period = 1 ns
  • Ideal duty cycle = 50%
  • Ideal high pulse = 0.5 ns
  • Ideal low pulse = 0.5 ns

In real designs:

  • Rise and fall delays are not equal
  • Buffers and inverters distort pulse width
  • Pulse width may shrink or even vanish in deep clock trees

This is especially critical for:

  • Half-cycle paths (+ve edge launch, –ve edge capture)
  • Latch-based designs

Effect of Rise/Fall Delay Mismatch

If a buffer has:

  • Rise delay > Fall delay → High pulse shrinks
  • Fall delay > Rise delay → Low pulse shrinks

Example (Figure 25.1):

  • Ideal pulse = 0.5 ns
  • Rise delay = 0.056 ns
  • Fall delay = 0.049 ns

Calculated pulse widths:

  • High pulse = 0.5 − 0.056 + 0.049 = 0.493 ns
  • Low pulse = 0.5 − 0.049 + 0.056 = 0.507 ns

Real-Time Clock Path Example (Figure 25.2)

A clock passes through multiple buffers, each having different rise and fall delays.

High Pulse Width calculation:
0.5 + (0.049 − 0.056) + (0.034 − 0.039) + (0.023 − 0.026) + (0.042 − 0.046) + (0.061 − 0.061) + (0.051 − 0.054)

= 0.478 ns

Low Pulse Width calculation:
0.5 + (0.056 − 0.049) + (0.038 − 0.034) + (0.026 − 0.023) + (0.046 − 0.042) + (0.061 − 0.061) + (0.054 − 0.051)

= 0.522 ns

MPW Violation Example

  • Required Min Pulse Width = 0.420 ns
  • Clock uncertainty = 80 ps

Effective high pulse width:
0.478 − 0.080 = 0.398 ns

 Violation observed (High pulse < required)

Fix

  • Insert an inverter to flip transitions and rebalance rise/fall paths
  • Optimize clock buffer chain

25.2: Minimum Period Check

Purpose

Ensures that a block is not clocked faster than its characterized limit.

Example Scenario

  • Block A characterized at 2 ns (500 MHz)
  • Top-level clock applied at 1 ns (1 GHz)

 Block A may fail functionally

min_period Constraint in .lib

pin (CP) {

  direction: input;

  capacitance: 1.2;

  min_period : 2;

}

  • Violations reported using:
  • report_constraint min_period -verbose
  • report_constraint all_violators
  • report_analysis_coverage -check_type min_period

25.3: min_period Violations – Debug Strategy

  1. Check if the clock is actually intended to reach the pin
  2. Verify case analysis completeness
  3. Confirm the pin is used as a clock internally
  4. If valid, escalate to:
  • RTL / Architecture team
  • Library owners

25.4: Clock Latency

Definition

Clock latency is an ideal-mode parameter defined by the user.

  • Not measured
  • Used before CTS

Once CTS is done:

  • Clock latency becomes Insertion Delay
  • Insertion delay is real and measurable

Types of Clock Latency

  • Source Latency: From clock origin to definition point
  • Network Latency: From definition point to sink flop

(Figures 25.3 & 25.4)

Clock latency is also called Clock Insertion Delay.

25.5: Clock Skew

Definition

Clock skew is the difference in clock latency between two interacting flops.

  • Positive Skew: Capture clock arrives later
  • Negative Skew: Capture clock arrives earlier

Ideal skew = 0 (not achievable)

(Figure 25.5)

Zero Skew Case (Figure 25.6)

Setup Slack:
Tclk − Tckq − Tcomb − Tsetup

Hold Slack:
Tckq + Tcomb − Thold

25.6: Positive Clock Skew

Effects:

  •  Setup relaxed
  •  Hold tightened

Equations (Figure 25.7):

  • Setup Slack = Tclk − Tckq − Tcomb − Tsetup + Tskew
  • Hold Slack = Tckq + Tcomb − Thold − Tskew

25.7: Negative Clock Skew

Effects:

  •  Setup tightened
  •  Hold relaxed

Equations (Figure 25.8):

  • Setup Slack = Tclk − Tckq − Tcomb − Tsetup − Tskew
  • Hold Slack = Tckq + Tcomb − Thold + Tskew

Key Insight for Students

  • Setup → next-cycle check
  • Hold → same-cycle check
  • Large data paths → setup issues
  • Small data paths or +ve skew → hold issues
  • Both rarely fail together

25.8: Recovery and Removal Checks

Used for asynchronous signals like reset.

  • Recovery Time (Tr): Reset must be stable before next clock edge
  • Removal Time (Tm): Reset must remain stable after clock edge

Recovery ≈ Setup check
Removal ≈ Hold check

(Figures 25.8 & 25.9)

25.9: Clock Gating Checks

Clock gating checks ensure a glitch-free gated clock.

Conditions for Gating Check

  1. Clock must be used as a clock downstream
  2. Gating signal must not be a clock downstream

(Figure 25.10)

Violations

  • Setup violation → Glitch at leading edge
  • Hold violation → Glitch at trailing edge

Correct behavior:

  • EN must change only when clock is LOW

(Figures 25.11 & 25.12)

25.10: Design Rule Violations (DRVs)

Why DRVs Matter

DRV conditions cause LUT extrapolation, leading to inaccurate timing and silicon failure.

Major DRVs

1. Max Transition

Occurs when input slew exceeds characterized limit.

Fixes:

  • Upsize driver
  • Route on higher metal layers
  • Insert buffers

2. Max Capacitance

Occurs when output load exceeds allowed limit.

Fixes:

  • Upsize driver
  • Split load using buffer tree

3. Max Fanout

Limits the number of loads a pin can safely drive.

Note:
If timing is met and only transition is violated, engineers may relax the constraint deliberately.

End of Chapter 25 – Clock Checks and DRVs

23. Hold Time

Introduction

In earlier sections, we discussed setup time. Let us briefly recap and then extend the discussion to hold time, which is equally important for correct register operation.

The data input of a register, commonly referred to as the D input, must receive incoming data at least Tsetup units before the onset of the latching edge of the clock. After this latching edge, the data becomes available at the output node Q.

In addition to setup time, the input D must be kept stable for a certain duration after the active clock edge. This duration is known as hold time (Thold).

Key Timing Parameters of a Latch

  • Setup Time (Tsetup):
    Minimum time before the active clock edge during which data must be stable.
  • Hold Time (Thold):
    Minimum time after the active clock edge during which data must remain stable.
  • Clock-to-Q Delay (Tclk-q):
    Delay between the time when data and clock are available at the latch input and when the data appears at the output Q.

These timing parameters together ensure correct data storage inside the latch.

Figure 23.1: Positive Level D-Latch Circuit

Clocking Parameters of a Register

Figure 23.2 illustrates the timing parameters associated with a register:

  • Setup Time
  • Hold Time
  • Clock-to-Q Delay
  • Cycle Time (Clock Period)

The clock is a periodic waveform that repeats every P units of time, where P is called the clock period or cycle time.

For a negative edge-triggered register, setup and hold requirements are defined with respect to the falling edge of the clock, as shown in Figure 23.2.

23.1 Hold Time in a Flip-Flop

Figure 23.3: Positive Edge-Triggered D Flip-Flop using Transmission Gates

This flip-flop consists of:

  • Master Stage: Negative level-sensitive latch
  • Slave Stage: Positive level-sensitive latch

Understanding Hold Time Inside a Flip-Flop

Hold time is measured only with respect to the active clock edge.

In Figure 23.3:

  • Input data D passes through an inverter or combinational logic before reaching transmission gate T1.
  • This logic is considered part of the flip-flop itself.
  • CLK and CLK BAR control the switching of the transmission gates.

The CLK and CLK BAR signals are delayed versions of the clock, as they pass through buffers and inverters. Therefore:

  • There is a finite delay between CLK and CLK BAR.
  • Transmission gates do not switch ON or OFF instantaneously.

During this switching interval:

  • The input data must remain stable
  • This stability ensures a stable value at the internal node W
  • A stable node W ensures a correct value at the output Q

This requirement explains why hold time exists inside a flip-flop.

Nature of Hold Time

  • A finite positive setup time always exists
  • Hold time can be:
  • Positive
  • Zero
  • Negative

The reason lies in the delay of the data path before the transmission gate.

If combinational logic exists before the transmission gate (for scan-enable, reset, set, enable, etc.):

  • This logic introduces extra delay
  • This delay determines whether hold time becomes positive, zero, or negative

23.2 Hold Time Analysis in a Register-to-Register Timing Path

Definition

Hold Time:
The minimum time for which the data (D) must remain stable at the input of the capture flip-flop after the active clock edge has arrived.

Register-to-Register Timing Path

Figure 23.4: Register-to-Register Timing Path

Path Definitions

Launch Path (Arrival Path / Data Path)

  • Data is launched from Launch Flip-Flop (FF1)
  • Data passes through:
  • Clock-to-Q delay (Tclk-Q)
  • Combinational logic delay (Tcombo)

Capture Path (Required Path / Clock Path)

  • Data is captured at Capture Flip-Flop (FF2)
  • Capture is governed by:
  • Capture clock
  • Hold time requirement of FF2

Timing Waveform Explanation

Figure 23.5: Timing Waveform for Register-to-Register Path

  • Data is launched from FF1 at time 0 sec
  • This data is intended to be captured by FF2 after one clock period (T)

Why Hold Time is Checked at the Same Clock Edge

At the same clock edge:

  • FF2 captures the current data (n)
  • FF1 launches the next data (n+1)

The purpose of the hold check is to ensure:

  • The newly launched data (n+1) does not arrive too early
  • It must not corrupt the current data (n) being captured by FF2

Therefore:

  • Data (n) must remain stable at FF2 input
  • Stability duration must be at least Thold

This required stability duration is called Hold Time.

Hold Time Equations

Basic Hold Condition

Data Arrival Time > Data Required Time

Mathematically:

Tclk-Q + Tcombo_delay > Thold

Hold Slack

Hold Slack = Data Arrival Time − Data Required Time

  • Positive Hold Slack: No hold violation
  • Negative Hold Slack: Hold violation

If arrival time is less:

  • Data is arriving too fast (early)
  • This causes a hold violation

Effect of Clock Skew on Hold Time

Positive Clock Skew

Figure 23.6: Positive Skew

  • Launch clock arrives earlier at FF1
  • Capture clock arrives later at FF2

Effects:

  • New data (n+1) is launched early
  • Capture clock has not yet arrived
  • Current data (n) must remain stable for:
  • Tskew + Thold duration

If this condition is not met:

  • Data (n) gets corrupted

 Positive skew is BAD for hold time

Negative Clock Skew

Figure 23.7: Negative Skew

  • Capture clock arrives earlier at FF2
  • Launch clock arrives later at FF1

Effects:

  • Data (n) is captured early
  • New data (n+1) has not yet been launched
  • Data (n) remains stable long enough

 Negative skew is GOOD for hold time

Hold Condition with Clock Skew

Final hold condition including skew:

Tclk-Q + Tcombo_logic > Thold + Tskew

  • Positive Tskew: Reduces hold slack (bad)
  • Negative Tskew: Increases hold slack (good)

Refer back to the Hold Slack Equation for a complete understanding of how skew impacts hold timing.

24: Hold Slack – Worked Example

Objective of the Example

To clearly understand how Hold Slack is calculated using a practical register-to-register timing path.

Given Circuit Description (Figure 24.1)

The circuit consists of:

  • A clock source
  • Clock buffers in the clock path
  • Three flip-flops: F1 (launch), F2, F3 (capture)
  • A data path buffer and an AND gate in the data path

Given Timing Parameters

Flip-Flop Parameters

  • Clock-to-Q delay (Tck→q) = 50 ps
  • Hold requirement (Thold) = 50 ps

Data Path Delays

  • Data path buffer delay = 200 ps
  • AND gate delay = 50 ps

Clock Path Delays

  • Clock buffer delay = 100 ps per buffer

Clock and Uncertainty

  • Clock period = 1 ns
  • Hold uncertainty = 50 ps

Hold Slack Condition

For a path to meet the hold requirement:

Data Arrival Time (DAT) > Data Required Time (DRT)

Hold Slack is defined as:

Hold Slack = Data Arrival Time − Data Required Time

 Important Rule: While calculating hold slack, always consider the minimum (fastest) data path.

Step 1: Data Arrival Time (DAT)

Data Arrival Time is calculated from launch flip-flop F1 to capture flip-flop F3.

Components contributing to DAT:

  • Launch clock latency
  • Clock-to-Q delay of launch flip-flop
  • Minimum data path delay

Calculation

  • Launch clock latency = 6 × 100 ps = 600 ps
  • Clock-to-Q delay = 50 ps
  • AND gate delay (minimum data path) = 50 ps

DAT = 600 + 50 + 50 = 700 ps

Step 2: Data Required Time (DRT)

Data Required Time depends on when the capture flip-flop requires stable data.

Components contributing to DRT:

  • Capture clock latency
  • Hold uncertainty
  • Hold requirement of capture flip-flop

Calculation

  • Capture clock latency = 5 × 100 ps = 500 ps
  • Hold uncertainty = 50 ps
  • Hold requirement = 50 ps

DRT = 500 + 50 + 50 = 600 ps

Step 3: Hold Slack Calculation

Hold Slack = DAT − DRT

= 700 ps − 600 ps

= +100 ps

Result

 Hold timing is MET (Positive slack)

Timing Interpretation (Figure 24.2)

  • Clock at F1 starts at 600 ps
  • Clock at F3 starts at 500 ps
  • Data arrives at 700 ps
  • Data is required at 600 ps

Since the data arrives 100 ps later than required, the hold condition is safely satisfied.

Key Student Takeaways

  • Hold analysis always uses the fastest data path
  • Clock frequency does not affect hold timing
  • Positive hold slack → Safe design
  • Negative hold slack → Hold violation (data too fast)
  • Hold fixes must be done before setup fixes

End of Chapter 24 – Hold Slack Example

25: Clock Checks and Design Rule Constraints

This chapter covers clock-related integrity checks and design rule violations that are essential for reliable silicon operation. These checks are heavily used in Static Timing Analysis (STA) and are critical for both student understanding and industry practice.

25.1: Minimum Pulse Width (MPW)

Definition

The Minimum Pulse Width check ensures that the high and low durations of a clock signal are greater than the minimum required value for correct operation of sequential elements.

This requirement depends on:

  • Operating frequency
  • Technology node
  • Clock path implementation

Basic Concept

For a design operating at 1 GHz:

  • Clock period = 1 ns
  • Ideal duty cycle = 50%
  • Ideal high pulse = 0.5 ns
  • Ideal low pulse = 0.5 ns

In real designs:

  • Rise and fall delays are not equal
  • Buffers and inverters distort pulse width
  • Pulse width may shrink or even vanish in deep clock trees

This is especially critical for:

  • Half-cycle paths (+ve edge launch, –ve edge capture)
  • Latch-based designs

Effect of Rise/Fall Delay Mismatch

If a buffer has:

  • Rise delay > Fall delay → High pulse shrinks
  • Fall delay > Rise delay → Low pulse shrinks

Example (Figure 25.1):

  • Ideal pulse = 0.5 ns
  • Rise delay = 0.056 ns
  • Fall delay = 0.049 ns

Calculated pulse widths:

  • High pulse = 0.5 − 0.056 + 0.049 = 0.493 ns
  • Low pulse = 0.5 − 0.049 + 0.056 = 0.507 ns

Real-Time Clock Path Example (Figure 25.2)

A clock passes through multiple buffers, each having different rise and fall delays.

High Pulse Width calculation:
0.5 + (0.049 − 0.056) + (0.034 − 0.039) + (0.023 − 0.026) + (0.042 − 0.046) + (0.061 − 0.061) + (0.051 − 0.054)

= 0.478 ns

Low Pulse Width calculation:
0.5 + (0.056 − 0.049) + (0.038 − 0.034) + (0.026 − 0.023) + (0.046 − 0.042) + (0.061 − 0.061) + (0.054 − 0.051)

= 0.522 ns

MPW Violation Example

  • Required Min Pulse Width = 0.420 ns
  • Clock uncertainty = 80 ps

Effective high pulse width:
0.478 − 0.080 = 0.398 ns

 Violation observed (High pulse < required)

Fix

  • Insert an inverter to flip transitions and rebalance rise/fall paths
  • Optimize clock buffer chain

25.2: Minimum Period Check

Purpose

Ensures that a block is not clocked faster than its characterized limit.

Example Scenario

  • Block A characterized at 2 ns (500 MHz)
  • Top-level clock applied at 1 ns (1 GHz)

 Block A may fail functionally

min_period Constraint in .lib

pin (CP) {

  direction: input;

  capacitance: 1.2;

  min_period : 2;

}

  • Violations reported using:
  • report_constraint min_period -verbose
  • report_constraint all_violators
  • report_analysis_coverage -check_type min_period

25.3: min_period Violations – Debug Strategy

  1. Check if the clock is actually intended to reach the pin
  2. Verify case analysis completeness
  3. Confirm the pin is used as a clock internally
  4. If valid, escalate to:
  • RTL / Architecture team
  • Library owners

25.4: Clock Latency

Definition

Clock latency is an ideal-mode parameter defined by the user.

  • Not measured
  • Used before CTS

Once CTS is done:

  • Clock latency becomes Insertion Delay
  • Insertion delay is real and measurable

Types of Clock Latency

  • Source Latency: From clock origin to definition point
  • Network Latency: From definition point to sink flop

(Figures 25.3 & 25.4)

Clock latency is also called Clock Insertion Delay.

25.5: Clock Skew

Definition

Clock skew is the difference in clock latency between two interacting flops.

  • Positive Skew: Capture clock arrives later
  • Negative Skew: Capture clock arrives earlier

Ideal skew = 0 (not achievable)

(Figure 25.5)

Zero Skew Case (Figure 25.6)

Setup Slack:
Tclk − Tckq − Tcomb − Tsetup

Hold Slack:
Tckq + Tcomb − Thold

25.6: Positive Clock Skew

Effects:

  •  Setup relaxed
  •  Hold tightened

Equations (Figure 25.7):

  • Setup Slack = Tclk − Tckq − Tcomb − Tsetup + Tskew
  • Hold Slack = Tckq + Tcomb − Thold − Tskew

25.7: Negative Clock Skew

Effects:

  •  Setup tightened
  •  Hold relaxed

Equations (Figure 25.8):

  • Setup Slack = Tclk − Tckq − Tcomb − Tsetup − Tskew
  • Hold Slack = Tckq + Tcomb − Thold + Tskew

Key Insight for Students

  • Setup → next-cycle check
  • Hold → same-cycle check
  • Large data paths → setup issues
  • Small data paths or +ve skew → hold issues
  • Both rarely fail together

25.8: Recovery and Removal Checks

Used for asynchronous signals like reset.

  • Recovery Time (Tr): Reset must be stable before next clock edge
  • Removal Time (Tm): Reset must remain stable after clock edge

Recovery ≈ Setup check
Removal ≈ Hold check

(Figures 25.8 & 25.9)

25.9: Clock Gating Checks

Clock gating checks ensure a glitch-free gated clock.

Conditions for Gating Check

  1. Clock must be used as a clock downstream
  2. Gating signal must not be a clock downstream

(Figure 25.10)

Violations

  • Setup violation → Glitch at leading edge
  • Hold violation → Glitch at trailing edge

Correct behavior:

  • EN must change only when clock is LOW

(Figures 25.11 & 25.12)

25.10: Design Rule Violations (DRVs)

Why DRVs Matter

DRV conditions cause LUT extrapolation, leading to inaccurate timing and silicon failure.

Major DRVs

1. Max Transition

Occurs when input slew exceeds characterized limit.

Fixes:

  • Upsize driver
  • Route on higher metal layers
  • Insert buffers

2. Max Capacitance

Occurs when output load exceeds allowed limit.

Fixes:

  • Upsize driver
  • Split load using buffer tree

3. Max Fanout

Limits the number of loads a pin can safely drive.

Note:
If timing is met and only transition is violated, engineers may relax the constraint deliberately.

End of Chapter 25 – Clock Checks and DRVs

Chapter 27: On-Chip Variation (OCV)

27.0 Introduction to OCV

On-Chip Variation (OCV) refers to the intrinsic variability present in semiconductor manufacturing processes and its impact on circuit timing. With technology scaling, timing variability is no longer limited to wafer-to-wafer differences but extends to die-to-die and even intra-die variations.

As a result of these variations, Integrated Circuits (ICs) fabricated from the same batch of wafers may behave as either fast or slow compared to nominal expectations. Initially, Static Timing Analysis (STA) tools handled OCV by applying a single global margin (derate) across the entire chip, based on a percentage or delay value considered safe by designers or foundries.

However, this global derating approach often leads to overly optimistic or pessimistic results, potentially degrading performance while attempting to close timing. Traditional OCV mainly handles global variations using different process corners (best-case, nominal, worst-case). The biggest challenge in OCV is accurately modeling local uncorrelated variations.

27.1 OCV Derating

27.1.1 Derating Concept

Derating is a technique used to model slow and fast signal behavior under On-Chip Variation. It introduces additional pessimism into Static Timing Analysis to ensure reliable chip operation under variability.

For example, a 10% derate implies over-designing timing by 10% so that the chip operates correctly at the desired frequency even in the presence of variation across the die. Derates can be applied independently to:

  • Data paths
  • Clock paths
  • Cell delays
  • Net delays
  • Cell timing checks

Early and late derates are applied to launch and capture paths depending on whether setup or hold analysis is being performed. Maximum and minimum derating involves multiplying the original library delay values by the derate factor.

As the manufacturing process matures, derating requirements reduce. For example:

  • Earlier 65nm designs required ~15% derates
  • Modern designs typically require ~5% derates

Figure 27.1 illustrates the relationship between ideal derate, OCV derate, and AOCV derate as a function of path depth.

27.1.2 OCV Timing Checks

Scaling factors can be independently defined for:

  • Data paths
  • Clock paths
  • Cell delays
  • Net delays
  • Cell timing checks

Early and late derates are selectively applied to launch and capture paths based on whether setup or hold timing is being evaluated.

27.1.3 Setup Check with OCV

For setup analysis under OCV:

  • Maximum clock path delays are considered for the launch register
  • Maximum delays are used along the slowest data path
  • Minimum clock path delays are considered for the capture register

This ensures that the maximum possible data arrival time is checked against the earliest possible clock capture edge.

27.1.4 Hold Check with OCV

For hold analysis under OCV:

  • Minimum delays are used for the clock path to the launch register
  • Minimum delays are used for the shortest data path
  • Maximum delays are used for the clock path to the capture register

This guarantees that the earliest arriving data is validated against the latest capture clock edge.

27.2 OCV Enhancements – Advanced OCV (AOCV)

Advanced OCV (AOCV) improves upon traditional OCV by using context-specific derating instead of a single global derate.

Key features of AOCV include:

  1. Reduces pessimism and results in fewer timing violations
  2. Derate values depend on logic depth and relative cell or net location
  3. Provides less pessimistic margins for deeper logic paths
  4. Accurately models variance to correct optimism and pessimism
  5. Also known as Location-Based OCV or Stage-Based OCV
  6. Stage-based OCV systematically corrects liberty timing models based on logic depth
  7. Logic depth and location-based approaches handle systematic effects
  8. Computes the diagonal length of the bounding box enclosing analyzed cells to select derates
  9. Global variations tend to cancel out over long distances
  10. Data path derates represent statistical delay or corner delay
  11. Clock path derates are related to slew variations
  12. AOCV table generation is independent of analysis methodology
  13. AOCV tables act as companions to .lib files
  14. Derate values are defined per cell and per depth (path length)
  15. AOCV defines 8 derate values for each cell at each depth

Derates are computed by analyzing the ratio of delay at a global corner with local variation to a fixed corner.

Table 27.1: AOCV Derate Combinations

  • Setup:
  • Late data rise, Late data fall
  • Early clock rise, Early clock fall
  • Hold:
  • Early data rise, Early data fall
  • Late clock rise, Late clock fall

27.3 Statistical OCV (SOCV / POCV)

Statistical OCV (SOCV), also known as Parametric OCV (POCV), is a simplified form of Statistical Static Timing Analysis (SSTA). It uses a single local variable as a derate and incorporates key SSTA concepts with reduced computational cost.

SOCV addresses major limitations of AOCV, including:

  • Variation dependency on slew and load
  • Assumptions that identical cells or loads exist along a path

SOCV characteristics:

  • Combines delay variations in cells, wires, and vias
  • Provides near-SSTA accuracy with minimal runtime and memory overhead
  • Supports signoff-accurate Signal Integrity (SI) analysis
  • Handles DPT and other dynamic effects conservatively
  • Ignores correlations and the number of timing paths
  • More accurate than AOCV, especially for graph-based analysis
  • Can be validated using SPICE Monte Carlo simulations

Chapter 28: CRPR / CPPR

28.1 Common Path Pessimism (CPP)

Applying different derates to launch and capture clock paths introduces unnecessary pessimism because the clock tree can only exist in one PVT condition at a time.

Common Path Pessimism (CPP), also known as Clock Re-convergence Pessimism (CRP), refers to the pessimism caused by applying different derating factors to the common portion of the clock tree shared by launch and capture paths.

CPP is defined as:

CPP / CRP = (Maximum clock delay or skew) − (Minimum clock delay or skew)

This artificial pessimism should be removed during timing analysis.

28.2 Common Path Pessimism Removal (CPPR / CRPR)

Common Path Pessimism Removal eliminates artificially introduced pessimism between launch and capture clock paths.

  • CPPR: Cadence terminology
  • CRPR: Synopsys terminology

Both techniques aim to improve timing accuracy by removing unrealistic pessimism.

28.3 Clock Uncertainty

Clock uncertainty represents the deviation of the actual clock edge arrival time from its ideal arrival time. In an ideal scenario, clock edges would reach all clock pins simultaneously; however, this is practically impossible.

To model this behavior, STA assumes a clock uncertainty. For example, a 1 ns clock with 100 ps uncertainty implies the next clock edge may arrive at 1 ns ± 50 ps.

Major contributors to clock uncertainty include:

  1. Clock Skew: Differences in insertion delay between the launch and capture flip-flop clock pins due to unequal clock tree paths.
  2. Clock Jitter: Random variations in clock period caused by PLLs, oscillators, cables, transmitters, receivers, internal PLL circuitry, and thermal noise.
  • Pre-CTS: Uncertainty = Skew + Jitter
  • Post-CTS: Uncertainty = Jitter
  1. On-Chip Variation (OCV): Even identical clock paths can experience different delays due to process, voltage, or temperature variations across the die, effectively increasing skew.

23. Hold Time

Introduction

In earlier sections, we discussed setup time. Let us briefly recap and then extend the discussion to hold time, which is equally important for correct register operation.

The data input of a register, commonly referred to as the D input, must receive incoming data at least Tsetup units before the onset of the latching edge of the clock. After this latching edge, the data becomes available at the output node Q.

In addition to setup time, the input D must be kept stable for a certain duration after the active clock edge. This duration is known as hold time (Thold).

Key Timing Parameters of a Latch

  • Setup Time (Tsetup):
    Minimum time before the active clock edge during which data must be stable.
  • Hold Time (Thold):
    Minimum time after the active clock edge during which data must remain stable.
  • Clock-to-Q Delay (Tclk-q):
    Delay between the time when data and clock are available at the latch input and when the data appears at the output Q.

These timing parameters together ensure correct data storage inside the latch.

Figure 23.1: Positive Level D-Latch Circuit

Clocking Parameters of a Register

Figure 23.2 illustrates the timing parameters associated with a register:

  • Setup Time
  • Hold Time
  • Clock-to-Q Delay
  • Cycle Time (Clock Period)

The clock is a periodic waveform that repeats every P units of time, where P is called the clock period or cycle time.

For a negative edge-triggered register, setup and hold requirements are defined with respect to the falling edge of the clock, as shown in Figure 23.2.

23.1 Hold Time in a Flip-Flop

Figure 23.3: Positive Edge-Triggered D Flip-Flop using Transmission Gates

This flip-flop consists of:

  • Master Stage: Negative level-sensitive latch
  • Slave Stage: Positive level-sensitive latch

Understanding Hold Time Inside a Flip-Flop

Hold time is measured only with respect to the active clock edge.

In Figure 23.3:

  • Input data D passes through an inverter or combinational logic before reaching transmission gate T1.
  • This logic is considered part of the flip-flop itself.
  • CLK and CLK BAR control the switching of the transmission gates.

The CLK and CLK BAR signals are delayed versions of the clock, as they pass through buffers and inverters. Therefore:

  • There is a finite delay between CLK and CLK BAR.
  • Transmission gates do not switch ON or OFF instantaneously.

During this switching interval:

  • The input data must remain stable
  • This stability ensures a stable value at the internal node W
  • A stable node W ensures a correct value at the output Q

This requirement explains why hold time exists inside a flip-flop.

Nature of Hold Time

  • A finite positive setup time always exists
  • Hold time can be:
  • Positive
  • Zero
  • Negative

The reason lies in the delay of the data path before the transmission gate.

If combinational logic exists before the transmission gate (for scan-enable, reset, set, enable, etc.):

  • This logic introduces extra delay
  • This delay determines whether hold time becomes positive, zero, or negative

23.2 Hold Time Analysis in a Register-to-Register Timing Path

Definition

Hold Time:
The minimum time for which the data (D) must remain stable at the input of the capture flip-flop after the active clock edge has arrived.

Register-to-Register Timing Path

Figure 23.4: Register-to-Register Timing Path

Path Definitions

Launch Path (Arrival Path / Data Path)

  • Data is launched from Launch Flip-Flop (FF1)
  • Data passes through:
  • Clock-to-Q delay (Tclk-Q)
  • Combinational logic delay (Tcombo)

Capture Path (Required Path / Clock Path)

  • Data is captured at Capture Flip-Flop (FF2)
  • Capture is governed by:
  • Capture clock
  • Hold time requirement of FF2

Timing Waveform Explanation

Figure 23.5: Timing Waveform for Register-to-Register Path

  • Data is launched from FF1 at time 0 sec
  • This data is intended to be captured by FF2 after one clock period (T)

Why Hold Time is Checked at the Same Clock Edge

At the same clock edge:

  • FF2 captures the current data (n)
  • FF1 launches the next data (n+1)

The purpose of the hold check is to ensure:

  • The newly launched data (n+1) does not arrive too early
  • It must not corrupt the current data (n) being captured by FF2

Therefore:

  • Data (n) must remain stable at FF2 input
  • Stability duration must be at least Thold

This required stability duration is called Hold Time.

Hold Time Equations

Basic Hold Condition

Data Arrival Time > Data Required Time

Mathematically:

Tclk-Q + Tcombo_delay > Thold

Hold Slack

Hold Slack = Data Arrival Time − Data Required Time

  • Positive Hold Slack: No hold violation
  • Negative Hold Slack: Hold violation

If arrival time is less:

  • Data is arriving too fast (early)
  • This causes a hold violation

Effect of Clock Skew on Hold Time

Positive Clock Skew

Figure 23.6: Positive Skew

  • Launch clock arrives earlier at FF1
  • Capture clock arrives later at FF2

Effects:

  • New data (n+1) is launched early
  • Capture clock has not yet arrived
  • Current data (n) must remain stable for:
  • Tskew + Thold duration

If this condition is not met:

  • Data (n) gets corrupted

 Positive skew is BAD for hold time

Negative Clock Skew

Figure 23.7: Negative Skew

  • Capture clock arrives earlier at FF2
