  • Launch clock arrives later at FF1

Effects:

  • Data (n) is captured early
  • New data (n+1) has not yet been launched
  • Data (n) remains stable long enough

 Negative skew is GOOD for hold time

Hold Condition with Clock Skew

Final hold condition including skew:

Tclk-Q + Tcombo_logic > Thold + Tskew

  • Positive Tskew: Reduces hold slack (bad)
  • Negative Tskew: Increases hold slack (good)

Refer back to the Hold Slack Equation for a complete understanding of how skew impacts hold timing.

24: Hold Slack – Worked Example

Objective of the Example

To clearly understand how Hold Slack is calculated using a practical register-to-register timing path.

Given Circuit Description (Figure 24.1)

The circuit consists of:

  • A clock source
  • Clock buffers in the clock path
  • Three flip-flops: F1 (launch), F2, F3 (capture)
  • A data path buffer and an AND gate in the data path

Given Timing Parameters

Flip-Flop Parameters

  • Clock-to-Q delay (Tck→q) = 50 ps
  • Hold requirement (Thold) = 50 ps

Data Path Delays

  • Data path buffer delay = 200 ps
  • AND gate delay = 50 ps

Clock Path Delays

  • Clock buffer delay = 100 ps per buffer

Clock and Uncertainty

  • Clock period = 1 ns
  • Hold uncertainty = 50 ps

Hold Slack Condition

For a path to meet the hold requirement:

Data Arrival Time (DAT) > Data Required Time (DRT)

Hold Slack is defined as:

Hold Slack = Data Arrival Time − Data Required Time

 Important Rule: While calculating hold slack, always consider the minimum (fastest) data path.

Step 1: Data Arrival Time (DAT)

Data Arrival Time is calculated from launch flip-flop F1 to capture flip-flop F3.

Components contributing to DAT:

  • Launch clock latency
  • Clock-to-Q delay of launch flip-flop
  • Minimum data path delay

Calculation

  • Launch clock latency = 6 × 100 ps = 600 ps
  • Clock-to-Q delay = 50 ps
  • AND gate delay (minimum data path) = 50 ps

DAT = 600 + 50 + 50 = 700 ps

Step 2: Data Required Time (DRT)

Data Required Time depends on when the capture flip-flop requires stable data.

Components contributing to DRT:

  • Capture clock latency
  • Hold uncertainty
  • Hold requirement of capture flip-flop

Calculation

  • Capture clock latency = 5 × 100 ps = 500 ps
  • Hold uncertainty = 50 ps
  • Hold requirement = 50 ps

DRT = 500 + 50 + 50 = 600 ps

Step 3: Hold Slack Calculation

Hold Slack = DAT − DRT

= 700 ps − 600 ps

= +100 ps

Result

 Hold timing is MET (Positive slack)

Timing Interpretation (Figure 24.2)

  • Clock at F1 starts at 600 ps
  • Clock at F3 starts at 500 ps
  • Data arrives at 700 ps
  • Data is required at 600 ps

Since the data arrives 100 ps later than required, the hold condition is safely satisfied.

Key Student Takeaways

  • Hold analysis always uses the fastest data path
  • Clock frequency does not affect hold timing
  • Positive hold slack → Safe design
  • Negative hold slack → Hold violation (data too fast)
  • Hold fixes must be done before setup fixes

End of Chapter 24 – Hold Slack Example

25: Clock Checks and Design Rule Constraints

This chapter covers clock-related integrity checks and design rule violations that are essential for reliable silicon operation. These checks are heavily used in Static Timing Analysis (STA) and are critical for both student understanding and industry practice.

25.1: Minimum Pulse Width (MPW)

Definition

The Minimum Pulse Width check ensures that the high and low durations of a clock signal are greater than the minimum required value for correct operation of sequential elements.

This requirement depends on:

  • Operating frequency
  • Technology node
  • Clock path implementation

Basic Concept

For a design operating at 1 GHz:

  • Clock period = 1 ns
  • Ideal duty cycle = 50%
  • Ideal high pulse = 0.5 ns
  • Ideal low pulse = 0.5 ns

In real designs:

  • Rise and fall delays are not equal
  • Buffers and inverters distort pulse width
  • Pulse width may shrink or even vanish in deep clock trees

This is especially critical for:

  • Half-cycle paths (+ve edge launch, –ve edge capture)
  • Latch-based designs

Effect of Rise/Fall Delay Mismatch

If a buffer has:

  • Rise delay > Fall delay → High pulse shrinks
  • Fall delay > Rise delay → Low pulse shrinks

Example (Figure 25.1):

  • Ideal pulse = 0.5 ns
  • Rise delay = 0.056 ns
  • Fall delay = 0.049 ns

Calculated pulse widths:

  • High pulse = 0.5 − 0.056 + 0.049 = 0.493 ns
  • Low pulse = 0.5 − 0.049 + 0.056 = 0.507 ns

Real-Time Clock Path Example (Figure 25.2)

A clock passes through multiple buffers, each having different rise and fall delays.

High Pulse Width calculation:
0.5 + (0.049 − 0.056) + (0.034 − 0.039) + (0.023 − 0.026) + (0.042 − 0.046) + (0.061 − 0.061) + (0.051 − 0.054)

= 0.478 ns

Low Pulse Width calculation:
0.5 + (0.056 − 0.049) + (0.038 − 0.034) + (0.026 − 0.023) + (0.046 − 0.042) + (0.061 − 0.061) + (0.054 − 0.051)

= 0.522 ns

MPW Violation Example

  • Required Min Pulse Width = 0.420 ns
  • Clock uncertainty = 80 ps

Effective high pulse width:
0.478 − 0.080 = 0.398 ns

 Violation observed (High pulse < required)

Fix

  • Insert an inverter to flip transitions and rebalance rise/fall paths
  • Optimize clock buffer chain

25.2: Minimum Period Check

Purpose

Ensures that a block is not clocked faster than its characterized limit.

Example Scenario

  • Block A characterized at 2 ns (500 MHz)
  • Top-level clock applied at 1 ns (1 GHz)

 Block A may fail functionally

min_period Constraint in .lib

pin (CP) {

  direction: input;

  capacitance: 1.2;

  min_period : 2;

}

  • Violations reported using:
  • report_constraint min_period -verbose
  • report_constraint all_violators
  • report_analysis_coverage -check_type min_period

25.3: min_period Violations – Debug Strategy

  1. Check if the clock is actually intended to reach the pin
  2. Verify case analysis completeness
  3. Confirm the pin is used as a clock internally
  4. If valid, escalate to:
  • RTL / Architecture team
  • Library owners

25.4: Clock Latency

Definition

Clock latency is an ideal-mode parameter defined by the user.

  • Not measured
  • Used before CTS

Once CTS is done:

  • Clock latency becomes Insertion Delay
  • Insertion delay is real and measurable

Types of Clock Latency

  • Source Latency: From clock origin to definition point
  • Network Latency: From definition point to sink flop

(Figures 25.3 & 25.4)

Clock latency is also called Clock Insertion Delay.

25.5: Clock Skew

Definition

Clock skew is the difference in clock latency between two interacting flops.

  • Positive Skew: Capture clock arrives later
  • Negative Skew: Capture clock arrives earlier

Ideal skew = 0 (not achievable)

(Figure 25.5)

Zero Skew Case (Figure 25.6)

Setup Slack:
Tclk − Tckq − Tcomb − Tsetup

Hold Slack:
Tckq + Tcomb − Thold

25.6: Positive Clock Skew

Effects:

  •  Setup relaxed
  •  Hold tightened

Equations (Figure 25.7):

  • Setup Slack = Tclk − Tckq − Tcomb − Tsetup + Tskew
  • Hold Slack = Tckq + Tcomb − Thold − Tskew

25.7: Negative Clock Skew

Effects:

  •  Setup tightened
  •  Hold relaxed

  • Setup Slack = Tclk − Tckq − Tcomb − Tsetup − Tskew
  • Hold Slack = Tckq + Tcomb − Thold + Tskew

Key Insight for Students

  • Setup → next-cycle check
  • Hold → same-cycle check
  • Large data paths → setup issues
  • Small data paths or +ve skew → hold issues
  • Both rarely fail together

25.8: Recovery and Removal Checks

Used for asynchronous signals like reset.

  • Recovery Time (Tr): Reset must be stable before next clock edge
  • Removal Time (Tm): Reset must remain stable after clock edge

Recovery ≈ Setup check
Removal ≈ Hold check

25.9: Clock Gating Checks

Clock gating checks ensure a glitch-free gated clock.

Conditions for Gating Check

  1. Clock must be used as a clock downstream
  2. Gating signal must not be a clock downstream

Violations

  • Setup violation → Glitch at leading edge
  • Hold violation → Glitch at trailing edge

Correct behavior:

  • EN must change only when clock is LOW

25.10: Design Rule Violations (DRVs)

Why DRVs Matter

DRV conditions cause LUT extrapolation, leading to inaccurate timing and silicon failure.

Major DRVs

1. Max Transition

Occurs when input slew exceeds characterized limit.

Fixes:

  • Upsize driver
  • Route on higher metal layers
  • Insert buffers

2. Max Capacitance

Occurs when output load exceeds allowed limit.

Fixes:

  • Upsize driver
  • Split load using buffer tree

3. Max Fanout

Limits the number of loads a pin can safely drive.

Note:
If timing is met and only transition is violated, engineers may relax the constraint deliberately.

End of Chapter 25 – Clock Checks and DRVs

Chapter 27: On-Chip Variation (OCV)

27.0 Introduction to OCV

On-Chip Variation (OCV) refers to the intrinsic variability present in semiconductor manufacturing processes and its impact on circuit timing. With technology scaling, timing variability is no longer limited to wafer-to-wafer differences but extends to die-to-die and even intra-die variations.

As a result of these variations, Integrated Circuits (ICs) fabricated from the same batch of wafers may behave as either fast or slow compared to nominal expectations. Initially, Static Timing Analysis (STA) tools handled OCV by applying a single global margin (derate) across the entire chip, based on a percentage or delay value considered safe by designers or foundries.

However, this global derating approach often leads to overly optimistic or pessimistic results, potentially degrading performance while attempting to close timing. Traditional OCV mainly handles global variations using different process corners (best-case, nominal, worst-case). The biggest challenge in OCV is accurately modeling local uncorrelated variations.

27.1 OCV Derating

27.1.1 Derating Concept

Derating is a technique used to model slow and fast signal behavior under On-Chip Variation. It introduces additional pessimism into Static Timing Analysis to ensure reliable chip operation under variability.

For example, a 10% derate implies over-designing timing by 10% so that the chip operates correctly at the desired frequency even in the presence of variation across the die. Derates can be applied independently to:

  • Data paths
  • Clock paths
  • Cell delays
  • Net delays
  • Cell timing checks

Early and late derates are applied to launch and capture paths depending on whether setup or hold analysis is being performed. Maximum and minimum derating involves multiplying the original library delay values by the derate factor.

As the manufacturing process matures, derating requirements reduce. For example:

  • Earlier 65nm designs required ~15% derates
  • Modern designs typically require ~5% derates

Figure 27.1 illustrates the relationship between ideal derate, OCV derate, and AOCV derate as a function of path depth.

27.1.2 OCV Timing Checks

Scaling factors can be independently defined for:

  • Data paths
  • Clock paths
  • Cell delays
  • Net delays
  • Cell timing checks

Early and late derates are selectively applied to launch and capture paths based on whether setup or hold timing is being evaluated.

27.1.3 Setup Check with OCV

For setup analysis under OCV:

  • Maximum clock path delays are considered for the launch register
  • Maximum delays are used along the slowest data path
  • Minimum clock path delays are considered for the capture register

This ensures that the maximum possible data arrival time is checked against the earliest possible clock capture edge.

27.1.4 Hold Check with OCV

For hold analysis under OCV:

  • Minimum delays are used for the clock path to the launch register
  • Minimum delays are used for the shortest data path
  • Maximum delays are used for the clock path to the capture register

This guarantees that the earliest arriving data is validated against the latest capture clock edge.

27.2 OCV Enhancements – Advanced OCV (AOCV)

Advanced OCV (AOCV) improves upon traditional OCV by using context-specific derating instead of a single global derate.

Key features of AOCV include:

  1. Reduces pessimism and results in fewer timing violations
  2. Derate values depend on logic depth and relative cell or net location
  3. Provides less pessimistic margins for deeper logic paths
  4. Accurately models variance to correct optimism and pessimism
  5. Also known as Location-Based OCV or Stage-Based OCV
  6. Stage-based OCV systematically corrects liberty timing models based on logic depth
  7. Logic depth and location-based approaches handle systematic effects
  8. Computes the diagonal length of the bounding box enclosing analyzed cells to select derates
  9. Global variations tend to cancel out over long distances
  10. Data path derates represent statistical delay or corner delay
  11. Clock path derates are related to slew variations
  12. AOCV table generation is independent of analysis methodology
  13. AOCV tables act as companions to .lib files
  14. Derate values are defined per cell and per depth (path length)
  15. AOCV defines 8 derate values for each cell at each depth

Derates are computed by analyzing the ratio of delay at a global corner with local variation to a fixed corner.

Table 27.1: AOCV Derate Combinations

  • Setup:
  • Late data rise, Late data fall
  • Early clock rise, Early clock fall
  • Hold:
  • Early data rise, Early data fall
  • Late clock rise, Late clock fall

27.3 Statistical OCV (SOCV / POCV)

Statistical OCV (SOCV), also known as Parametric OCV (POCV), is a simplified form of Statistical Static Timing Analysis (SSTA). It uses a single local variable as a derate and incorporates key SSTA concepts with reduced computational cost.

SOCV addresses major limitations of AOCV, including:

  • Variation dependency on slew and load
  • Assumptions that identical cells or loads exist along a path

SOCV characteristics:

  • Combines delay variations in cells, wires, and vias
  • Provides near-SSTA accuracy with minimal runtime and memory overhead
  • Supports signoff-accurate Signal Integrity (SI) analysis
  • Handles DPT and other dynamic effects conservatively
  • Ignores correlations and the number of timing paths
  • More accurate than AOCV, especially for graph-based analysis
  • Can be validated using SPICE Monte Carlo simulations

Chapter 28: CRPR / CPPR

28.1 Common Path Pessimism (CPP)

Applying different derates to launch and capture clock paths introduces unnecessary pessimism because the clock tree can only exist in one PVT condition at a time.

Common Path Pessimism (CPP), also known as Clock Re-convergence Pessimism (CRP), refers to the pessimism caused by applying different derating factors to the common portion of the clock tree shared by launch and capture paths.

CPP is defined as:

CPP / CRP = (Maximum clock delay or skew) − (Minimum clock delay or skew)

This artificial pessimism should be removed during timing analysis.

28.2 Common Path Pessimism Removal (CPPR / CRPR)

Common Path Pessimism Removal eliminates artificially introduced pessimism between launch and capture clock paths.

  • CPPR: Cadence terminology
  • CRPR: Synopsys terminology

Both techniques aim to improve timing accuracy by removing unrealistic pessimism.

28.3 Clock Uncertainty

Clock uncertainty represents the deviation of the actual clock edge arrival time from its ideal arrival time. In an ideal scenario, clock edges would reach all clock pins simultaneously; however, this is practically impossible.

To model this behavior, STA assumes a clock uncertainty. For example, a 1 ns clock with 100 ps uncertainty implies the next clock edge may arrive at 1 ns ± 50 ps.

Major contributors to clock uncertainty include:

  1. Clock Skew: Differences in insertion delay between the launch and capture flip-flop clock pins due to unequal clock tree paths.
  2. Clock Jitter: Random variations in clock period caused by PLLs, oscillators, cables, transmitters, receivers, internal PLL circuitry, and thermal noise.
  • Pre-CTS: Uncertainty = Skew + Jitter
  • Post-CTS: Uncertainty = Jitter
  1. On-Chip Variation (OCV): Even identical clock paths can experience different delays due to process, voltage, or temperature variations across the die, effectively increasing skew.
